[[Category:attempting to force efresh]] [[Category:bump 1-20]] [[ja:御師]]

{{Otheruses|Shrine and temple guides|Music teachers for the emperor or imperial family|{{ill|御師 (音楽)|ja|御師 (音楽)}}}} [[File:Oshi 20190826a.jpg|thumb|Fuji-ko Guide (御師), photographed August 26, 2019]] '''Oshi''' (御師) refers to shrine priests associated with specific {{ill|shrines and temples|ja|社寺}}, who perform {{ill|prayers|ja|祈祷}}, guide worshippers, and assist pilgrims with visits and accommodations. In the case of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}, it was traditionally pronounced as "onshi." These guides often formed concentrated communities along highways, known as {{ill|Oshi towns|ja|御師町}}.
== Overview == Originally an abbreviation for "御祈祷師" (prayer guides), the term "oshi" emerged during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, initially referring to {{ill|temple monks|ja|社僧}}. Later, it extended to include shrine priests who facilitated pilgrimages.
== History == During the Heian period, notable examples of oshi included those from {{ill|Iwashimizu Hachiman Shrine|ja|石清水八幡宮}}, {{ill|Kamo Shrine|ja|賀茂神社}}, and {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|ja|日吉大社}}, with the {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} "Kumano Oshi" being particularly famous.<ref name="ushiyama">Yoshiyuki Ushiyama, "Faith and Culture in Medieval Ōhara," Shinshu University Bulletin of Education, Vol. 79 (1993), {{hdl|10091/1626}}</ref> The Kumano Oshi organized prayers, accommodations, and guided noble pilgrims during the late Heian period's {{ill|Kumano pilgrimage|ja|熊野詣}}. Relationships between oshi and pilgrims evolved into stable "master-disciple" ties, known as {{ill|師檀関係|ja|師檀関係}}, encompassing even commoners by the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}.
Between the Kamakura and {{ill|Muromachi periods|ja|室町時代}}, other prominent oshi groups emerged, such as those from {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}, {{ill|Fuji-ko|ja|富士講}}, {{ill|Matsuo Shrine|ja|松尾大社}}, {{ill|Mishima Taisha|ja|三嶋大社}}, and {{ill|Hakusan Shrine|ja|白山}}. Notably, records like the {{ill|Azuma Kagami|ja|吾妻鏡}} document oshi from {{ill|Izumo Taisha|ja|出雲大社}} serving {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝}}.<ref>'Izumo Taisha Teacher Training Manual,' Izumo Taisha Headquarters, 1989, p. 319</ref>
During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, oshi played an essential role as intermediaries between commoners and shrine priests. Pilgrimages like {{ill|Ise pilgrimage|ja|伊勢参り}} and those to {{ill|Mt. Fuji|ja|富士山}} became widely accessible. Oshi organized {{ill|kō (pilgrimage associations)|ja|講}} and hosted pilgrims in their {{ill|shukubō (pilgrim lodgings)|ja|宿坊}}. The economic stability of the Edo period allowed pilgrimages to combine faith and leisure, enhancing oshi influence. However, groups like the Kumano Oshi declined alongside {{ill|Kumano faith|ja|熊野信仰}}.
In Izumo, oshi structures laid the foundation for the {{ill|Izumo Taisha Faith|ja|出雲大社教}}, creating facilities such as "tanjo" (mission centers) to support local preaching.<ref>'Izumo Taisha Teacher Training Manual,' Izumo Taisha Headquarters, 1989, p. 319</ref>
With the establishment of the {{ill|Meiji government|ja|明治政府}}'s {{ill|Shinto|ja|神祇}} system, oshi rapidly declined. In 1869, oshi were barred from conducting {{ill|Shinto funerals|ja|神葬祭}}, and by 1871, the profession was abolished. Most oshi transitioned into farming or running inns. Some attempted to continue religious activities, forming organizations such as {{ill|Fusōkyō|ja|扶桑教}}.<ref>Takahiko Takano, "The Court and Religion in Early Modern Japan," Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2014, ISBN 978-4-642-03461-6, pp. 345-348.</ref>
=== Fuji Oshi === In the {{ill|Tenshōjingo Conflict|ja|天正壬午の乱}}, figures like Watanabe Shūgokuyū played significant roles. Notable Fuji Oshi residences, including {{ill|Old Togawa Residence|ja|旧外川家住宅}} and {{ill|Kosano Residence|ja|小佐野家住宅}}, are designated as {{ill|World Heritage Sites|ja|世界遺産}} and {{ill|Important Cultural Properties of Japan|ja|重要文化財}}.<ref>{{Cite web|title=World Cultural Heritage: Mt. Fuji Components|url=https://www.yamanashi-kankou.jp/heritage/fujisanwatcher/jp/world_heritage/index.html|publisher=Yamanashi Tourism Organization|access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Old Togawa Residence|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/185831|website=National Cultural Heritage Database|access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref>
== Cultural Depictions == * "Oshi Yagorō: Journey to Ise" by {{ill|Saijo Naka|ja|西條奈加}}, ISBN 439634015X * "{{ill|Sakuya-hime|ja|咲夜姫}}" by {{ill|Yamaguchi Kaito|ja|山口歌糸}}, ISBN 4783880204
== References == <references />
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Hagaki|ja|羽書}} * {{ill|Okage-mairi|ja|お蔭参り}} * {{ill|Shukubō|ja|宿坊}} * {{ill|Accommodation facilities|ja|宿泊施設}} * {{ill|Ise Calendar|ja|伊勢暦}} * [http://www.nikko-annai.com/ Dōshabiki] - Edo-period guides for worshippers at {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|ja|日光東照宮}}

== History == During the Heian period, notable examples of oshi included those from {{ill|Iwashimizu Hachiman Shrine|ja|石清水八幡宮}}, {{ill|Kamo Shrine|ja|賀茂神社}}, and {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|ja|日吉大社}}, with the {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} "Kumano Oshi" being particularly famous.<ref name="ushiyama">Yoshiyuki Ushiyama, "Faith and Culture in Medieval Ōhara," Shinshu University Bulletin of Education, Vol. 79 (1993), {{hdl|10091/1626}}</ref> The Kumano Oshi organized prayers, accommodations, and guided noble pilgrims during the late Heian period's {{ill|Kumano pilgrimage|ja|熊野詣}}. Relationships between oshi and pilgrims evolved into stable "master-disciple" ties, known as {{ill|師檀関係|ja|師檀関係}}, encompassing even commoners by the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}.
Between the Kamakura and {{ill|Muromachi periods|ja|室町時代}}, other prominent oshi groups emerged, such as those from {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}, {{ill|Fuji-ko|ja|富士講}}, {{ill|Matsuo Shrine|ja|松尾大社}}, {{ill|Mishima Taisha|ja|三嶋大社}}, and {{ill|Hakusan Shrine|ja|白山}}. Notably, records like the {{ill|Azuma Kagami|ja|吾妻鏡}} document oshi from {{ill|Izumo Taisha|ja|出雲大社}} serving {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝}}.<ref>'Izumo Taisha Teacher Training Manual,' Izumo Taisha Headquarters, 1989, p. 319</ref>
During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, oshi played an essential role as intermediaries between commoners and shrine priests. Pilgrimages like {{ill|Ise pilgrimage|ja|伊勢参り}} and those to {{ill|Mt. Fuji|ja|富士山}} became widely accessible. Oshi organized {{ill|kō (pilgrimage associations)|ja|講}} and hosted pilgrims in their {{ill|shukubō (pilgrim lodgings)|ja|宿坊}}. The economic stability of the Edo period allowed pilgrimages to combine faith and leisure, enhancing oshi influence. However, groups like the Kumano Oshi declined alongside {{ill|Kumano faith|ja|熊野信仰}}.
In Izumo, oshi structures laid the foundation for the {{ill|Izumo Taisha Faith|ja|出雲大社教}}, creating facilities such as "tanjo" (mission centers) to support local preaching.<ref>'Izumo Taisha Teacher Training Manual,' Izumo Taisha Headquarters, 1989, p. 319</ref>
With the establishment of the {{ill|Meiji government|ja|明治政府}}'s {{ill|Shinto|ja|神祇}} system, oshi rapidly declined. In 1869, oshi were barred from conducting {{ill|Shinto funerals|ja|神葬祭}}, and by 1871, the profession was abolished. Most oshi transitioned into farming or running inns. Some attempted to continue religious activities, forming organizations such as {{ill|Fusōkyō|ja|扶桑教}}.<ref>Takahiko Takano, "The Court and Religion in Early Modern Japan," Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2014, ISBN 978-4-642-03461-6, pp. 345-348.</ref>
=== Fuji Oshi === In the {{ill|Tenshōjingo Conflict|ja|天正壬午の乱}}, figures like Watanabe Shūgokuyū played significant roles. Notable Fuji Oshi residences, including {{ill|Old Togawa Residence|ja|旧外川家住宅}} and {{ill|Kosano Residence|ja|小佐野家住宅}}, are designated as {{ill|World Heritage Sites|ja|世界遺産}} and {{ill|Important Cultural Properties of Japan|ja|重要文化財}}.<ref>{{Cite web|title=World Cultural Heritage: Mt. Fuji Components|url=https://www.yamanashi-kankou.jp/heritage/fujisanwatcher/jp/world_heritage/index.html|publisher=Yamanashi Tourism Organization|access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Old Togawa Residence|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/185831|website=National Cultural Heritage Database|access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref>

{{Otheruses|Shrine and temple guides|Music teachers for the emperor or imperial family|{{ill|御師 (音楽)|ja|御師 (音楽)}}}} [[File:Oshi 20190826a.jpg|thumb|Fuji-ko Guide (御師), photographed August 26, 2019]] '''Oshi''' (御師) refers to shrine priests associated with specific {{ill|shrines and temples|ja|社寺}}, who perform {{ill|prayers|ja|祈祷}}, guide worshippers, and assist pilgrims with visits and accommodations. In the case of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}, it was traditionally pronounced as "onshi." These guides often formed concentrated communities along highways, known as {{ill|Oshi towns|ja|御師町}}.

{{Otheruses|Shrine and temple guides|Music teachers for the emperor or imperial family|{{ill|御師 (音楽)|ja|御師 (音楽)}}}} [[File:Oshi 20190826a.jpg|thumb|Fuji-ko Guide (御師), photographed August 26, 2019]] '''Oshi''' (御師) refers to shrine priests associated with specific {{ill|shrines and temples|ja|社寺}}, who perform {{ill|prayers|ja|祈祷}}, guide worshippers, and assist pilgrims with visits and accommodations. In the case of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}, it was traditionally pronounced as "onshi." These guides often formed concentrated communities along highways, known as {{ill|Oshi towns|ja|御師町}}.
== Overview == Originally an abbreviation for "御祈祷師" (prayer guides), the term "oshi" emerged during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, initially referring to {{ill|temple monks|ja|社僧}}. Later, it extended to include shrine priests who facilitated pilgrimages.
== History == During the Heian period, notable examples of oshi included those from {{ill|Iwashimizu Hachiman Shrine|ja|石清水八幡宮}}, {{ill|Kamo Shrine|ja|賀茂神社}}, and {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|ja|日吉大社}}, with the {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} "Kumano Oshi" being particularly famous.<ref name="ushiyama">Yoshiyuki Ushiyama, "Faith and Culture in Medieval Ōhara," Shinshu University Bulletin of Education, Vol. 79 (1993), {{hdl|10091/1626}}</ref> The Kumano Oshi organized prayers, accommodations, and guided noble pilgrims during the late Heian period's {{ill|Kumano pilgrimage|ja|熊野詣}}. Relationships between oshi and pilgrims evolved into stable "master-disciple" ties, known as {{ill|師檀関係|ja|師檀関係}}, encompassing even commoners by the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}.
Between the Kamakura and {{ill|Muromachi periods|ja|室町時代}}, other prominent oshi groups emerged, such as those from {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}, {{ill|Fuji-ko|ja|富士講}}, {{ill|Matsuo Shrine|ja|松尾大社}}, {{ill|Mishima Taisha|ja|三嶋大社}}, and {{ill|Hakusan Shrine|ja|白山}}. Notably, records like the {{ill|Azuma Kagami|ja|吾妻鏡}} document oshi from {{ill|Izumo Taisha|ja|出雲大社}} serving {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝}}.<ref>'Izumo Taisha Teacher Training Manual,' Izumo Taisha Headquarters, 1989, p. 319</ref>
During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, oshi played an essential role as intermediaries between commoners and shrine priests. Pilgrimages like {{ill|Ise pilgrimage|ja|伊勢参り}} and those to {{ill|Mt. Fuji|ja|富士山}} became widely accessible. Oshi organized {{ill|Kō (lecture)|ja|講|Kō (lecture)=Kō (lecture)|lt=kō (pilgrimage associations)}} and hosted pilgrims in their {{ill|Shukubo|ja|宿坊|Shukubo=Shukubo|lt=shukubō (pilgrim lodgings)}}. The economic stability of the Edo period allowed pilgrimages to combine faith and leisure, enhancing oshi influence. However, groups like the Kumano Oshi declined alongside {{ill|Kumano faith|ja|熊野信仰}}.
In Izumo, oshi structures laid the foundation for the {{ill|Izumo Taisha Faith|ja|出雲大社教}}, creating facilities such as "tanjo" (mission centers) to support local preaching.<ref>'Izumo Taisha Teacher Training Manual,' Izumo Taisha Headquarters, 1989, p. 319</ref>
With the establishment of the {{ill|Meiji government|ja|明治政府}}'s {{ill|Shinto|ja|神祇}} system, oshi rapidly declined. In 1869, oshi were barred from conducting {{ill|Shinto funerals|ja|神葬祭}}, and by 1871, the profession was abolished. Most oshi transitioned into farming or running inns. Some attempted to continue religious activities, forming organizations such as {{ill|Fusōkyō|ja|扶桑教}}.<ref>Takahiko Takano, "The Court and Religion in Early Modern Japan," Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2014, ISBN 978-4-642-03461-6, pp. 345-348.</ref>
=== Fuji Oshi === In the {{ill|Tenshōjingo Conflict|ja|天正壬午の乱}}, figures like Watanabe Shūgokuyū played significant roles. Notable Fuji Oshi residences, including {{ill|Old Togawa Residence|ja|旧外川家住宅}} and {{ill|Kosano Residence|ja|小佐野家住宅}}, are designated as {{ill|World Heritage Sites|ja|世界遺産}} and {{ill|Important Cultural Properties of Japan|ja|重要文化財}}.<ref>{{Cite web|title=World Cultural Heritage: Mt. Fuji Components|url=https://www.yamanashi-kankou.jp/heritage/fujisanwatcher/jp/world_heritage/index.html|publisher=Yamanashi Tourism Organization|access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Old Togawa Residence|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/185831|website=National Cultural Heritage Database|access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref>
== Cultural Depictions == * "Oshi Yagorō: Journey to Ise" by {{ill|Saijo Naka|ja|西條奈加}}, ISBN 439634015X * "{{ill|Sakuya-hime|ja|咲夜姫}}" by {{ill|Yamaguchi Kaito|ja|山口歌糸}}, ISBN 4783880204
== References == <references />
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Hagaki|ja|羽書}} * {{ill|Okage-mairi|ja|お蔭参り}} * {{ill|Shukubō|ja|宿坊}} * {{ill|Accommodation facilities|ja|宿泊施設}} * {{ill|Ise Calendar|ja|伊勢暦}} * [http://www.nikko-annai.com/ Dōshabiki] - Edo-period guides for worshippers at {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|ja|日光東照宮}}

Notable Fuji Oshi residences, including {{ill|Old Togawa Residence|ja|旧外川家住宅}} and {{ill|Kosano Residence|ja|小佐野家住宅}}, are designated as {{ill|World Heritage Sites|ja|世界遺産}} and {{ill|Important Cultural Properties of Japan|ja|重要文化財}}.<ref>{{Cite web|title=World Cultural Heritage: Mt. Fuji Components|url=https://www.yamanashi-kankou.jp/heritage/fujisanwatcher/jp/world_heritage/index.html|publisher=Yamanashi Tourism Organization|access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Old Togawa Residence|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/185831|website=National Cultural Heritage Database|access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref>
== Cultural Depictions == * "Oshi Yagorō: Journey to Ise" by {{ill|Saijo Naka|ja|西條奈加}}, ISBN 439634015X * "{{ill|Sakuya-hime|ja|咲夜姫}}" by {{ill|Yamaguchi Kaito|ja|山口歌糸}}, ISBN 4783880204

revision.comment: Copying from [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] to [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:{{subst:PAGENAME}}]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
{{Otheruses|Shrine and temple guides|Music teachers for the emperor or imperial family|{{ill|御師 (音楽)|ja|御師 (音楽)}}}} [[File:Oshi 20190826a.jpg|thumb|Fuji-ko Guide (御師), photographed August 26, 2019]] '''Oshi''' (御師) refers to shrine priests associated with specific {{ill|shrines and temples|ja|社寺}}, who perform {{ill|prayers|ja|祈祷}}, guide worshippers, and assist pilgrims with visits and accommodations. In the case of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}, it was traditionally pronounced as "onshi." These guides often formed concentrated communities along highways, known as {{ill|Oshi towns|ja|御師町}}.
== Overview == Originally an abbreviation for "御祈祷師" (prayer guides), the term "oshi" emerged during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, initially referring to {{ill|temple monks|ja|社僧}}. Later, it extended to include shrine priests who facilitated pilgrimages.
== History == During the Heian period, notable examples of oshi included those from {{ill|Iwashimizu Hachiman Shrine|ja|石清水八幡宮}}, {{ill|Kamo Shrine|ja|賀茂神社}}, and {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|ja|日吉大社}}, with the {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} "Kumano Oshi" being particularly famous.<ref name="ushiyama">Yoshiyuki Ushiyama, "Faith and Culture in Medieval Ōhara," Shinshu University Bulletin of Education, Vol. 79 (1993), {{hdl|10091/1626}}</ref> The Kumano Oshi organized prayers, accommodations, and guided noble pilgrims during the late Heian period's {{ill|Kumano pilgrimage|ja|熊野詣}}. Relationships between oshi and pilgrims evolved into stable "master-disciple" ties, known as {{ill|師檀関係|ja|師檀関係}}, encompassing even commoners by the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}.
Between the Kamakura and {{ill|Muromachi periods|ja|室町時代}}, other prominent oshi groups emerged, such as those from {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}, {{ill|Fuji-ko|ja|富士講}}, {{ill|Matsuo Shrine|ja|松尾大社}}, {{ill|Mishima Taisha|ja|三嶋大社}}, and {{ill|Hakusan Shrine|ja|白山}}. Notably, records like the {{ill|Azuma Kagami|ja|吾妻鏡}} document oshi from {{ill|Izumo Taisha|ja|出雲大社}} serving {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝}}.<ref>'Izumo Taisha Teacher Training Manual,' Izumo Taisha Headquarters, 1989, p. 319</ref>
During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, oshi played an essential role as intermediaries between commoners and shrine priests. Pilgrimages like {{ill|Ise pilgrimage|ja|伊勢参り}} and those to {{ill|Mt. Fuji|ja|富士山}} became widely accessible. Oshi organized {{ill|Kō (lecture)|ja|講|Kō (lecture)=Kō (lecture)|lt=kō (pilgrimage associations)}} and hosted pilgrims in their {{ill|Shukubo|ja|宿坊|Shukubo=Shukubo|lt=shukubō (pilgrim lodgings)}}. The economic stability of the Edo period allowed pilgrimages to combine faith and leisure, enhancing oshi influence. However, groups like the Kumano Oshi declined alongside {{ill|Kumano faith|ja|熊野信仰}}.
In Izumo, oshi structures laid the foundation for the {{ill|Izumo Taisha Faith|ja|出雲大社教}}, creating facilities such as "tanjo" (mission centers) to support local preaching.<ref>'Izumo Taisha Teacher Training Manual,' Izumo Taisha Headquarters, 1989, p. 319</ref>
With the establishment of the {{ill|Meiji government|ja|明治政府}}'s {{ill|Shinto|ja|神祇}} system, oshi rapidly declined. In 1869, oshi were barred from conducting {{ill|Shinto funerals|ja|神葬祭}}, and by 1871, the profession was abolished. Most oshi transitioned into farming or running inns. Some attempted to continue religious activities, forming organizations such as {{ill|Fusōkyō|ja|扶桑教}}.<ref>Takahiko Takano, "The Court and Religion in Early Modern Japan," Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2014, ISBN 978-4-642-03461-6, pp. 345-348.</ref>
=== Fuji Oshi === In the {{ill|Tenshōjingo Conflict|ja|天正壬午の乱}}, figures like Watanabe Shūgokuyū played significant roles. Notable Fuji Oshi residences, including {{ill|Old Togawa Residence|ja|旧外川家住宅}} and {{ill|Kosano Residence|ja|小佐野家住宅}}, are designated as {{ill|World Heritage Sites|ja|世界遺産}} and {{ill|Important Cultural Properties of Japan|ja|重要文化財}}.<ref>{{Cite web|title=World Cultural Heritage: Mt. Fuji Components|url=https://www.yamanashi-kankou.jp/heritage/fujisanwatcher/jp/world_heritage/index.html|publisher=Yamanashi Tourism Organization|access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Old Togawa Residence|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/185831|website=National Cultural Heritage Database|access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref>
== Cultural Depictions == * "Oshi Yagorō: Journey to Ise" by {{ill|Saijo Naka|ja|西條奈加}}, ISBN 439634015X * "{{ill|Sakuya-hime|ja|咲夜姫}}" by {{ill|Yamaguchi Kaito|ja|山口歌糸}}, ISBN 4783880204
== References == <references />
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Hagaki|ja|羽書}} * {{ill|Okage-mairi|ja|お蔭参り}} * {{ill|Shukubō|ja|宿坊}} * {{ill|Accommodation facilities|ja|宿泊施設}} * {{ill|Ise Calendar|ja|伊勢暦}} * [http://www.nikko-annai.com/ Dōshabiki] - Edo-period guides for worshippers at {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|ja|日光東照宮}}

{{Otheruses|Shrine and temple guides|Music teachers for the emperor or imperial family|{{ill|御師 (音楽)|ja|御師 (音楽)}}}} [[File:Oshi 20190826a.jpg|thumb|Fuji-ko Guide (御師), photographed August 26, 2019]] '''Oshi''' (御師) refers to shrine priests associated with specific {{ill|shrines and temples|ja|社寺}}, who perform {{ill|prayers|ja|祈祷}}, guide worshippers, and assist pilgrims with visits and accommodations. In the case of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}, it was traditionally pronounced as "onshi." These guides often formed concentrated communities along highways, known as {{ill|Oshi towns|ja|御師町}}.
== Overview == Originally an abbreviation for "御祈祷師" (prayer guides), the term "oshi" emerged during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, initially referring to {{ill|temple monks|ja|社僧}}. Later, it extended to include shrine priests who facilitated pilgrimages.
== History == During the Heian period, notable examples of oshi included those from {{ill|Iwashimizu Hachiman Shrine|ja|石清水八幡宮}}, {{ill|Kamo Shrine|ja|賀茂神社}}, and {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|ja|日吉大社}}, with the {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} "Kumano Oshi" being particularly famous.<ref name="ushiyama">Yoshiyuki Ushiyama, "Faith and Culture in Medieval Ōhara," Shinshu University Bulletin of Education, Vol. 79 (1993), {{hdl|10091/1626}}</ref> The Kumano Oshi organized prayers, accommodations, and guided noble pilgrims during the late Heian period's {{ill|Kumano pilgrimage|ja|熊野詣}}. Relationships between oshi and pilgrims evolved into stable "master-disciple" ties, known as {{ill|師檀関係|ja|師檀関係}}, encompassing even commoners by the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}.
Between the Kamakura and {{ill|Muromachi periods|ja|室町時代}}, other prominent oshi groups emerged, such as those from {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}, {{ill|Fuji-ko|ja|富士講}}, {{ill|Matsuo Shrine|ja|松尾大社}}, {{ill|Mishima Taisha|ja|三嶋大社}}, and {{ill|Hakusan Shrine|ja|白山}}. Notably, records like the {{ill|Azuma Kagami|ja|吾妻鏡}} document oshi from {{ill|Izumo Taisha|ja|出雲大社}} serving {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝}}.<ref>'Izumo Taisha Teacher Training Manual,' Izumo Taisha Headquarters, 1989, p. 319</ref>
During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, oshi played an essential role as intermediaries between commoners and shrine priests. Pilgrimages like {{ill|Ise pilgrimage|ja|伊勢参り}} and those to {{ill|Mt. Fuji|ja|富士山}} became widely accessible. Oshi organized {{ill|Kō (lecture)|ja|講|Kō (lecture)=Kō (lecture)|lt=kō (pilgrimage associations)}} and hosted pilgrims in their {{ill|Shukubo|ja|宿坊|Shukubo=Shukubo|lt=shukubō (pilgrim lodgings)}}. The economic stability of the Edo period allowed pilgrimages to combine faith and leisure, enhancing oshi influence. However, groups like the Kumano Oshi declined alongside {{ill|Kumano faith|ja|熊野信仰}}.
In Izumo, oshi structures laid the foundation for the {{ill|Izumo Taisha Faith|ja|出雲大社教}}, creating facilities such as "tanjo" (mission centers) to support local preaching.<ref>'Izumo Taisha Teacher Training Manual,' Izumo Taisha Headquarters, 1989, p. 319</ref>
With the establishment of the {{ill|Meiji government|ja|明治政府}}'s {{ill|Shinto|ja|神祇}} system, oshi rapidly declined. In 1869, oshi were barred from conducting {{ill|Shinto funerals|ja|神葬祭}}, and by 1871, the profession was abolished. Most oshi transitioned into farming or running inns. Some attempted to continue religious activities, forming organizations such as {{ill|Fusōkyō|ja|扶桑教}}.<ref>Takahiko Takano, "The Court and Religion in Early Modern Japan," Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2014, ISBN 978-4-642-03461-6, pp. 345-348.</ref>
=== Fuji Oshi === In the {{ill|Tenshōjingo Conflict|ja|天正壬午の乱}}, figures like Watanabe Shūgokuyū played significant roles. Notable Fuji Oshi residences, including {{ill|Old Togawa Residence|ja|旧外川家住宅}} and {{ill|Kosano Residence|ja|小佐野家住宅}}, are designated as {{ill|World Heritage Sites|ja|世界遺産}} and {{ill|Important Cultural Properties of Japan|ja|重要文化財}}.<ref>{{Cite web|title=World Cultural Heritage: Mt. Fuji Components|url=https://www.yamanashi-kankou.jp/heritage/fujisanwatcher/jp/world_heritage/index.html|publisher=Yamanashi Tourism Organization|access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Old Togawa Residence|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/185831|website=National Cultural Heritage Database|access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref>
== Cultural Depictions == * "Oshi Yagorō: Journey to Ise" by {{ill|Saijo Naka|ja|西條奈加}}, ISBN 439634015X * "{{ill|Sakuya-hime|ja|咲夜姫}}" by {{ill|Yamaguchi Kaito|ja|山口歌糸}}, ISBN 4783880204
== References == <references />
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Hagaki|ja|羽書}} * {{ill|Okage-mairi|ja|お蔭参り}} * {{ill|Shukubō|ja|宿坊}} * {{ill|Accommodation facilities|ja|宿泊施設}} * {{ill|Ise Calendar|ja|伊勢暦}} * [http://www.nikko-annai.com/ Dōshabiki] - Edo-period guides for worshippers at {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|ja|日光東照宮}}

Notable Fuji Oshi residences, including {{ill|Old Togawa Residence|ja|旧外川家住宅}} and {{ill|Kosano Residence|ja|小佐野家住宅}}, are designated as {{ill|World Heritage Sites|ja|世界遺産}} and {{ill|Important Cultural Properties of Japan|ja|重要文化財}}.<ref>{{Cite web|title=World Cultural Heritage: Mt. Fuji Components|url=https://www.yamanashi-kankou.jp/heritage/fujisanwatcher/jp/world_heritage/index.html|publisher=Yamanashi Tourism Organization|access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Old Togawa Residence|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/185831|website=National Cultural Heritage Database|access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref>
== Cultural Depictions == * "Oshi Yagorō: Journey to Ise" by {{ill|Saijo Naka|ja|西條奈加}}, ISBN 439634015X * "{{ill|Sakuya-hime|ja|咲夜姫}}" by {{ill|Yamaguchi Kaito|ja|山口歌糸}}, ISBN 4783880204

revision.text:
{{Otheruses|Shrine and temple guides|Music teachers for the emperor or imperial family|{{ill|御師 (音楽)|ja|御師 (音楽)}}}} [[File:Oshi 20190826a.jpg|thumb|Fuji-ko Guide (御師), photographed August 26, 2019]] '''Oshi''' (御師) refers to shrine priests associated with specific {{ill|shrines and temples|ja|社寺}}, who perform {{ill|prayers|ja|祈祷}}, guide worshippers, and assist pilgrims with visits and accommodations. In the case of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}, it was traditionally pronounced as "onshi." These guides often formed concentrated communities along highways, known as {{ill|Oshi towns|ja|御師町}}.
== Overview == Originally an abbreviation for "御祈祷師" (prayer guides), the term "oshi" emerged during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, initially referring to {{ill|temple monks|ja|社僧}}. Later, it extended to include shrine priests who facilitated pilgrimages.
== History == During the Heian period, notable examples of oshi included those from {{ill|Iwashimizu Hachiman Shrine|ja|石清水八幡宮}}, {{ill|Kamo Shrine|ja|賀茂神社}}, and {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|ja|日吉大社}}, with the {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} "Kumano Oshi" being particularly famous.<ref name="ushiyama">Yoshiyuki Ushiyama, "Faith and Culture in Medieval Ōhara," Shinshu University Bulletin of Education, Vol. 79 (1993), {{hdl|10091/1626}}</ref> The Kumano Oshi organized prayers, accommodations, and guided noble pilgrims during the late Heian period's {{ill|Kumano pilgrimage|ja|熊野詣}}. Relationships between oshi and pilgrims evolved into stable "master-disciple" ties, known as {{ill|師檀関係|ja|師檀関係}}, encompassing even commoners by the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}.
Between the Kamakura and {{ill|Muromachi periods|ja|室町時代}}, other prominent oshi groups emerged, such as those from {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}, {{ill|Fuji-ko|ja|富士講}}, {{ill|Matsuo Shrine|ja|松尾大社}}, {{ill|Mishima Taisha|ja|三嶋大社}}, and {{ill|Hakusan Shrine|ja|白山}}. Notably, records like the {{ill|Azuma Kagami|ja|吾妻鏡}} document oshi from {{ill|Izumo Taisha|ja|出雲大社}} serving {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝}}.<ref>'Izumo Taisha Teacher Training Manual,' Izumo Taisha Headquarters, 1989, p. 319</ref>
During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, oshi played an essential role as intermediaries between commoners and shrine priests. Pilgrimages like {{ill|Ise pilgrimage|ja|伊勢参り}} and those to {{ill|Mt. Fuji|ja|富士山}} became widely accessible. Oshi organized {{ill|Kō (lecture)|ja|講|Kō (lecture)=Kō (lecture)|lt=kō (pilgrimage associations)}} and hosted pilgrims in their {{ill|Shukubo|ja|宿坊|Shukubo=Shukubo|lt=shukubō (pilgrim lodgings)}}. The economic stability of the Edo period allowed pilgrimages to combine faith and leisure, enhancing oshi influence. However, groups like the Kumano Oshi declined alongside {{ill|Kumano faith|ja|熊野信仰}}.
In Izumo, oshi structures laid the foundation for the {{ill|Izumo Taisha Faith|ja|出雲大社教}}, creating facilities such as "tanjo" (mission centers) to support local preaching.<ref>'Izumo Taisha Teacher Training Manual,' Izumo Taisha Headquarters, 1989, p. 319</ref>
With the establishment of the {{ill|Meiji government|ja|明治政府}}'s {{ill|Shinto|ja|神祇}} system, oshi rapidly declined. In 1869, oshi were barred from conducting {{ill|Shinto funerals|ja|神葬祭}}, and by 1871, the profession was abolished. Most oshi transitioned into farming or running inns. Some attempted to continue religious activities, forming organizations such as {{ill|Fusōkyō|ja|扶桑教}}.<ref>Takahiko Takano, "The Court and Religion in Early Modern Japan," Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2014, ISBN 978-4-642-03461-6, pp. 345-348.</ref>
=== Fuji Oshi === In the {{ill|Tenshōjingo Conflict|ja|天正壬午の乱}}, figures like Watanabe Shūgokuyū played significant roles. Notable Fuji Oshi residences, including {{ill|Old Togawa Residence|ja|旧外川家住宅}} and {{ill|Kosano Residence|ja|小佐野家住宅}}, are designated as {{ill|World Heritage Sites|ja|世界遺産}} and {{ill|Important Cultural Properties of Japan|ja|重要文化財}}.<ref>{{Cite web|title=World Cultural Heritage: Mt. Fuji Components|url=https://www.yamanashi-kankou.jp/heritage/fujisanwatcher/jp/world_heritage/index.html|publisher=Yamanashi Tourism Organization|access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Old Togawa Residence|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/185831|website=National Cultural Heritage Database|access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref>
== Cultural Depictions == * "Oshi Yagorō: Journey to Ise" by {{ill|Saijo Naka|ja|西條奈加}}, ISBN 439634015X * "{{ill|Sakuya-hime|ja|咲夜姫}}" by {{ill|Yamaguchi Kaito|ja|山口歌糸}}, ISBN 4783880204
== References == <references />
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Hagaki|ja|羽書}} * {{ill|Okage-mairi|ja|お蔭参り}} * {{ill|Shukubō|ja|宿坊}} * {{ill|Accommodation facilities|ja|宿泊施設}} * {{ill|Ise Calendar|ja|伊勢暦}} * [http://www.nikko-annai.com/ Dōshabiki] - Edo-period guides for worshippers at {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|ja|日光東照宮}}

{{Otheruses|Shrine and temple guides|Music teachers for the emperor or imperial family|{{ill|御師 (音楽)|ja|御師 (音楽)}}}} [[File:Oshi 20190826a.jpg|thumb|Fuji-ko Guide (御師), photographed August 26, 2019]] '''Oshi''' (御師) refers to shrine priests associated with specific {{ill|shrines and temples|ja|社寺}}, who perform {{ill|prayers|ja|祈祷}}, guide worshippers, and assist pilgrims with visits and accommodations. In the case of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}, it was traditionally pronounced as "onshi." These guides often formed concentrated communities along highways, known as {{ill|Oshi towns|ja|御師町}}.
== Overview == Originally an abbreviation for "御祈祷師" (prayer guides), the term "oshi" emerged during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, initially referring to {{ill|temple monks|ja|社僧}}. Later, it extended to include shrine priests who facilitated pilgrimages.
== History == During the Heian period, notable examples of oshi included those from {{ill|Iwashimizu Hachiman Shrine|ja|石清水八幡宮}}, {{ill|Kamo Shrine|ja|賀茂神社}}, and {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|ja|日吉大社}}, with the {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} "Kumano Oshi" being particularly famous.<ref name="ushiyama">Yoshiyuki Ushiyama, "Faith and Culture in Medieval Ōhara," Shinshu University Bulletin of Education, Vol. 79 (1993), {{hdl|10091/1626}}</ref> The Kumano Oshi organized prayers, accommodations, and guided noble pilgrims during the late Heian period's {{ill|Kumano pilgrimage|ja|熊野詣}}. Relationships between oshi and pilgrims evolved into stable "master-disciple" ties, known as {{ill|師檀関係|ja|師檀関係}}, encompassing even commoners by the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}.
Between the Kamakura and {{ill|Muromachi periods|ja|室町時代}}, other prominent oshi groups emerged, such as those from {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}, {{ill|Fuji-ko|ja|富士講}}, {{ill|Matsuo Shrine|ja|松尾大社}}, {{ill|Mishima Taisha|ja|三嶋大社}}, and {{ill|Hakusan Shrine|ja|白山}}. Notably, records like the {{ill|Azuma Kagami|ja|吾妻鏡}} document oshi from {{ill|Izumo Taisha|ja|出雲大社}} serving {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝}}.<ref>'Izumo Taisha Teacher Training Manual,' Izumo Taisha Headquarters, 1989, p. 319</ref>
During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, oshi played an essential role as intermediaries between commoners and shrine priests. Pilgrimages like {{ill|Ise pilgrimage|ja|伊勢参り}} and those to {{ill|Mt. Fuji|ja|富士山}} became widely accessible. Oshi organized {{ill|Kō (lecture)|ja|講|Kō (lecture)=Kō (lecture)|lt=kō (pilgrimage associations)}} and hosted pilgrims in their {{ill|Shukubo|ja|宿坊|Shukubo=Shukubo|lt=shukubō (pilgrim lodgings)}}. The economic stability of the Edo period allowed pilgrimages to combine faith and leisure, enhancing oshi influence. However, groups like the Kumano Oshi declined alongside {{ill|Kumano faith|ja|熊野信仰}}.
In Izumo, oshi structures laid the foundation for the {{ill|Izumo Taisha Faith|ja|出雲大社教}}, creating facilities such as "tanjo" (mission centers) to support local preaching.<ref>'Izumo Taisha Teacher Training Manual,' Izumo Taisha Headquarters, 1989, p. 319</ref>
With the establishment of the {{ill|Meiji government|ja|明治政府}}'s {{ill|Shinto|ja|神祇}} system, oshi rapidly declined. In 1869, oshi were barred from conducting {{ill|Shinto funerals|ja|神葬祭}}, and by 1871, the profession was abolished. Most oshi transitioned into farming or running inns. Some attempted to continue religious activities, forming organizations such as {{ill|Fusōkyō|ja|扶桑教}}.<ref>Takahiko Takano, "The Court and Religion in Early Modern Japan," Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2014, ISBN 978-4-642-03461-6, pp. 345-348.</ref>
=== Fuji Oshi === In the {{ill|Tenshōjingo Conflict|ja|天正壬午の乱}}, figures like Watanabe Shūgokuyū played significant roles. Notable Fuji Oshi residences, including {{ill|Old Togawa Residence|ja|旧外川家住宅}} and {{ill|Kosano Residence|ja|小佐野家住宅}}, are designated as {{ill|World Heritage Sites|ja|世界遺産}} and {{ill|Important Cultural Properties of Japan|ja|重要文化財}}.<ref>{{Cite web|title=World Cultural Heritage: Mt. Fuji Components|url=https://www.yamanashi-kankou.jp/heritage/fujisanwatcher/jp/world_heritage/index.html|publisher=Yamanashi Tourism Organization|access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Old Togawa Residence|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/185831|website=National Cultural Heritage Database|access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref>
== Cultural Depictions == * "Oshi Yagorō: Journey to Ise" by {{ill|Saijo Naka|ja|西條奈加}}, ISBN 439634015X * "{{ill|Sakuya-hime|ja|咲夜姫}}" by {{ill|Yamaguchi Kaito|ja|山口歌糸}}, ISBN 4783880204
== References == <references />
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Hagaki|ja|羽書}} * {{ill|Okage-mairi|ja|お蔭参り}} * {{ill|Shukubō|ja|宿坊}} * {{ill|Accommodation facilities|ja|宿泊施設}} * {{ill|Ise Calendar|ja|伊勢暦}} * [http://www.nikko-annai.com/ Dōshabiki] - Edo-period guides for worshippers at {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|ja|日光東照宮}}

revision.comment: 1 revision imported: importing shinto cults 浅間神社
revision.text:
{{Otheruses|社寺に属する御師|天皇や皇族に音楽を教える御師|御師 (音楽)}} [[File:Oshi 20190826a.jpg|thumb|富士構 御師（2019年8月26日撮影）]] '''御師'''（おし、おんし）とは、特定の[[社寺]]に所属して、その社寺へ[[参詣]]する者や[[信者]]の為に[[祈祷]]、案内をし、参拝・[[宿泊]]などの世話をする神職のことである。特に[[伊勢神宮]]のものは「おんし」と読んだ。御師は街道沿いに集住し、[[御師町]]を形成する。
== 概要 == 本来は「御祈祷師」を略したもので、[[平安時代]]のころから神社に所属する[[社僧]]を指すようになり、後に神社の[[参詣]]の世話をする[[神職]]も指すようになった。
== 歴史 == 平安時代の御師には、[[石清水八幡宮|石清水]]・[[賀茂神社|賀茂]]・[[日吉大社|日吉]]などのものがあるが、代表的なのは[[熊野三山]]の'''熊野御師'''<ref name="ushiyama">牛山佳幸、「中世大原の信仰と文化」 信州大学教育学部紀要 79: 163-172（1993）, {{Hdl|10091/1626}}</ref>である。[[熊野詣]]では平安時代末期に[[貴族]]の間で流行したが、その際の祈祷や宿泊の世話、山内の案内をしたのが熊野御師であった。熊野では当初参詣のつど両者間で[[契約]]していたが、次第に御師を「師」とし「[[先達]]」が率いてきた参詣者（道者）を「[[檀那]]」とする恒常的な関係（師檀関係）を形成していった。 [[鎌倉時代]]には[[武士]]にも広まり、[[室町時代]]には[[農民]]などの[[庶民]]まで檀那とするようになった。
鎌倉時代から室町時代初期にかけては、[[伊勢神宮]]<ref name="ushiyama"/>・[[富士講]]・[[松尾大社|松尾]]・[[三嶋大社|三嶋]]・[[白山]]・[[大山 (神奈川県)|大山]]などの御師も活躍した。特に[[出雲大社]]が[[源頼朝]]の御師を行った事は『[[吾妻鏡]]』という[[鎌倉幕府]]の記録を示したものに記されている<ref>『出雲大社教布教師養成講習会』 発行：出雲大社教教務本庁 平成元年9月 全342頁中</ref>{{信頼性要検証|date=2016年12月}}。
[[江戸時代]]には[[百姓]]と[[神職]]の中間の[[身分]]とされ、経済の安定により庶民の間で寺社詣りが信仰と[[レジャー|遊興]]の側面を併せ持つようになっていく中で、伊勢・[[富士山|富士]]を中心に出雲・[[津島神社|津島]]など多くの神社で御師の制度が発達した。特に伊勢や富士では全国に檀那を持つまでに至った。例えば、伊勢御師は全国各地に派遣され、現地の'''[[講]]'''（伊勢講）の世話を行い、彼らが[[伊勢参り]]に訪れた際には自己の[[宿坊]]で迎え入れて便宜を図った。同様のことは各地で行われ、[[中世]]から[[近世]]にかけて、御師の間で'''師職'''（御師の[[職の体系|職]]）や檀那の[[相続]]や譲渡・売買が盛んになり、勢力の強い御師のもとに檀那や祈祷料などが集まった。一方で熊野御師は[[熊野信仰]]の衰退とともに衰退した。なお、出雲大社の御師組織は大きくなり、「出雲講」や「甲子講」もでき、[[出雲大社教]]の基礎を築いた。また、地方で出雲御師が布教する丹所（たんしょ）という建物も建設された<ref>出雲大社教教務本庁『出雲大社教布教師養成講習会』1989年9月 全427頁中319頁</ref>。
[[明治]]に入ると、[[日本国政府|政府]]主導の[[神祇官#神祇官の復興|神祇制度]]が整備されたため、急速に御師は衰退する。[[明治]]2年（[[1869年]]）に明治政府は神職の[[葬儀]]は[[神葬祭]]に改めるように命じるとともに、御師は百姓が兼帯しているもので正規の神職では無いため、神葬祭を行う事が禁じられた。御師側はこうした動きに抗議したものの、明治4年（[[1871年]]）7月には御師職そのものが廃止されてしまい、ほとんどの御師は[[平民]]に編入された<ref>[[河口湖]]の川口御師の[[草分け]]とされる三浦家のように[[徳川将軍家]]とのつながり（[[徳川家綱]]誕生の祈祷を行って特権が与えられた）を理由に[[士族]]として扱われた例外もある。</ref>。御師は百姓や宿屋経営などに転じていくことになるが、富士講の御師を結集して[[扶桑教]]を結成するなど、宗教的な活動を維持しようとする動きもあった<ref>（著）高埜利彦 『近世の朝廷と宗教』吉川弘文館、2014年 ISBN 978-4-642-03461-6 P345-48</ref>。
=== 富士御師 === [[天正壬午の乱]]の時、[[渡辺囚獄佑]]が該当し得る。
富士講の御師の、[[旧外川家住宅]]、[[小佐野家住宅]]は、それぞれ[[世界遺産]][[富士山-信仰の対象と芸術の源泉]]の構成資産であり<ref>{{Cite web|和書|title=世界文化遺産 富士山の構成資産一覧 |url=https://www.yamanashi-kankou.jp/heritage/fujisanwatcher/jp/world_heritage/index.html |website=【公式】富士の国やまなし観光ネット 山梨県公式観光情報 |access-date=2022-04-16 |language=ja |last=公益社団法人やまなし観光推進機構}}</ref>、国の[[重要文化財]]に指定されている<ref>{{Cite web|和書|title=旧外川家住宅（山梨県富士吉田市上吉田） 主屋 文化遺産オンライン |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/185831 |website=bunka.nii.ac.jp |access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|和書|title=小佐野家住宅（山梨県富士吉田市上吉田） 主屋 文化遺産オンライン |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/144548 |website=bunka.nii.ac.jp |access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref>。富士講の御師の、[[上文司家住宅]]、[[原家住宅]]は、それぞれ国の「[[登録有形文化財]]（建造物）」に登録されている<ref>{{Cite web|和書|title=国指定文化財等データベース |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/101/00011949 |website=kunishitei.bunka.go.jp |access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|和書|title=国指定文化財等データベース |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/101/00011948 |website=kunishitei.bunka.go.jp |access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref>。
== 御師を題材とした作品 == *御師弥五郎―お伊勢参り道中記（[[西條奈加]]） ISBN 439634015X *[[咲夜姫]]（[[山口歌糸]]） ISBN 4783880204
== 脚注 == <references />
== 関連項目 == *[[羽書]] *[[お蔭参り]] *[[宿坊]] *[[宿泊施設]] *[[伊勢暦]] *[http://www.nikko-annai.com/ 堂者引き] - 日光東照宮の堂者（=参拝客）を引率する江戸時代から続く社寺公認のガイド

== 歴史 == 平安時代の御師には、[[石清水八幡宮|石清水]]・[[賀茂神社|賀茂]]・[[日吉大社|日吉]]などのものがあるが、代表的なのは[[熊野三山]]の'''熊野御師'''<ref name="ushiyama">牛山佳幸、「中世大原の信仰と文化」 信州大学教育学部紀要 79: 163-172（1993）, {{Hdl|10091/1626}}</ref>である。[[熊野詣]]では平安時代末期に[[貴族]]の間で流行したが、その際の祈祷や宿泊の世話、山内の案内をしたのが熊野御師であった。熊野では当初参詣のつど両者間で[[契約]]していたが、次第に御師を「師」とし「[[先達]]」が率いてきた参詣者（道者）を「[[檀那]]」とする恒常的な関係（師檀関係）を形成していった。 [[鎌倉時代]]には[[武士]]にも広まり、[[室町時代]]には[[農民]]などの[[庶民]]まで檀那とするようになった。
鎌倉時代から室町時代初期にかけては、[[伊勢神宮]]<ref name="ushiyama"/>・[[富士講]]・[[松尾大社|松尾]]・[[三嶋大社|三嶋]]・[[白山]]・[[大山 (神奈川県)|大山]]などの御師も活躍した。特に[[出雲大社]]が[[源頼朝]]の御師を行った事は『[[吾妻鏡]]』という[[鎌倉幕府]]の記録を示したものに記されている<ref>『出雲大社教布教師養成講習会』 発行：出雲大社教教務本庁 平成元年9月 全342頁中</ref>{{信頼性要検証|date=2016年12月}}。
[[江戸時代]]には[[百姓]]と[[神職]]の中間の[[身分]]とされ、経済の安定により庶民の間で寺社詣りが信仰と[[レジャー|遊興]]の側面を併せ持つようになっていく中で、伊勢・[[富士山|富士]]を中心に出雲・[[津島神社|津島]]など多くの神社で御師の制度が発達した。特に伊勢や富士では全国に檀那を持つまでに至った。例えば、伊勢御師は全国各地に派遣され、現地の'''[[講]]'''（伊勢講）の世話を行い、彼らが[[伊勢参り]]に訪れた際には自己の[[宿坊]]で迎え入れて便宜を図った。同様のことは各地で行われ、[[中世]]から[[近世]]にかけて、御師の間で'''師職'''（御師の[[職の体系|職]]）や檀那の[[相続]]や譲渡・売買が盛んになり、勢力の強い御師のもとに檀那や祈祷料などが集まった。一方で熊野御師は[[熊野信仰]]の衰退とともに衰退した。なお、出雲大社の御師組織は大きくなり、「出雲講」や「甲子講」もでき、[[出雲大社教]]の基礎を築いた。また、地方で出雲御師が布教する丹所（たんしょ）という建物も建設された<ref>出雲大社教教務本庁『出雲大社教布教師養成講習会』1989年9月 全427頁中319頁</ref>。
[[明治]]に入ると、[[日本国政府|政府]]主導の[[神祇官#神祇官の復興|神祇制度]]が整備されたため、急速に御師は衰退する。[[明治]]2年（[[1869年]]）に明治政府は神職の[[葬儀]]は[[神葬祭]]に改めるように命じるとともに、御師は百姓が兼帯しているもので正規の神職では無いため、神葬祭を行う事が禁じられた。御師側はこうした動きに抗議したものの、明治4年（[[1871年]]）7月には御師職そのものが廃止されてしまい、ほとんどの御師は[[平民]]に編入された<ref>[[河口湖]]の川口御師の[[草分け]]とされる三浦家のように[[徳川将軍家]]とのつながり（[[徳川家綱]]誕生の祈祷を行って特権が与えられた）を理由に[[士族]]として扱われた例外もある。</ref>。御師は百姓や宿屋経営などに転じていくことになるが、富士講の御師を結集して[[扶桑教]]を結成するなど、宗教的な活動を維持しようとする動きもあった<ref>（著）高埜利彦 『近世の朝廷と宗教』吉川弘文館、2014年 ISBN 978-4-642-03461-6 P345-48</ref>。
=== 富士御師 === [[天正壬午の乱]]の時、[[渡辺囚獄佑]]が該当し得る。
富士講の御師の、[[旧外川家住宅]]、[[小佐野家住宅]]は、それぞれ[[世界遺産]][[富士山-信仰の対象と芸術の源泉]]の構成資産であり<ref>{{Cite web|和書|title=世界文化遺産 富士山の構成資産一覧 |url=https://www.yamanashi-kankou.jp/heritage/fujisanwatcher/jp/world_heritage/index.html |website=【公式】富士の国やまなし観光ネット 山梨県公式観光情報 |access-date=2022-04-16 |language=ja |last=公益社団法人やまなし観光推進機構}}</ref>、国の[[重要文化財]]に指定されている<ref>{{Cite web|和書|title=旧外川家住宅（山梨県富士吉田市上吉田） 主屋 文化遺産オンライン |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/185831 |website=bunka.nii.ac.jp |access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|和書|title=小佐野家住宅（山梨県富士吉田市上吉田） 主屋 文化遺産オンライン |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/144548 |website=bunka.nii.ac.jp |access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref>。富士講の御師の、[[上文司家住宅]]、[[原家住宅]]は、それぞれ国の「[[登録有形文化財]]（建造物）」に登録されている<ref>{{Cite web|和書|title=国指定文化財等データベース |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/101/00011949 |website=kunishitei.bunka.go.jp |access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|和書|title=国指定文化財等データベース |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/101/00011948 |website=kunishitei.bunka.go.jp |access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref>。
== 御師を題材とした作品 == *御師弥五郎―お伊勢参り道中記（[[西條奈加]]） ISBN 439634015X *[[咲夜姫]]（[[山口歌糸]]） ISBN 4783880204
== 脚注 == <references />
== 関連項目 == *[[羽書]] *[[お蔭参り]] *[[宿坊]] *[[宿泊施設]] *[[伊勢暦]] *[http://www.nikko-annai.com/ 堂者引き] - 日光東照宮の堂者（=参拝客）を引率する江戸時代から続く社寺公認のガイド

{{Otheruses|社寺に属する御師|天皇や皇族に音楽を教える御師|御師 (音楽)}} [[File:Oshi 20190826a.jpg|thumb|富士構 御師（2019年8月26日撮影）]] '''御師'''（おし、おんし）とは、特定の[[社寺]]に所属して、その社寺へ[[参詣]]する者や[[信者]]の為に[[祈祷]]、案内をし、参拝・[[宿泊]]などの世話をする神職のことである。特に[[伊勢神宮]]のものは「おんし」と読んだ。御師は街道沿いに集住し、[[御師町]]を形成する。

鎌倉時代から室町時代初期にかけては、[[伊勢神宮]]<ref name="ushiyama"/>・[[富士講]]・[[松尾大社|松尾]]・[[三嶋大社|三嶋]]・[[白山]]・[[大山 (神奈川県)|大山]]などの御師も活躍した。特に[[出雲大社]]が[[源頼朝]]の御師を行った事は『[[吾妻鏡]]』という[[鎌倉幕府]]の記録を示したものに記されている<ref>『出雲大社教布教師養成講習会』 発行：出雲大社教教務本庁 平成元年9月 全342頁中</ref>{{信頼性要検証|date=2016年12月}}。

鎌倉時代から室町時代初期にかけては、[[伊勢神宮]]<ref name="ushiyama"/>・[[富士講]]・[[松尾大社|松尾]]・[[三嶋大社|三嶋]]・[[白山]]・[[大山 (神奈川県)|大山]]などの御師も活躍した。特に[[出雲大社]]が[[源頼朝]]の御師を行った事は『[[吾妻鏡]]』という[[鎌倉幕府]]の記録を示したものに記されている<ref>『出雲大社教布教師養成講習会』 発行：出雲大社教教務本庁 平成元年9月 全342頁中</ref>{{信頼性要検証|date=2016年12月}}。
[[江戸時代]]には[[百姓]]と[[神職]]の中間の[[身分]]とされ、経済の安定により庶民の間で寺社詣りが信仰と[[レジャー|遊興]]の側面を併せ持つようになっていく中で、伊勢・[[富士山|富士]]を中心に出雲・[[津島神社|津島]]など多くの神社で御師の制度が発達した。特に伊勢や富士では全国に檀那を持つまでに至った。例えば、伊勢御師は全国各地に派遣され、現地の'''[[講]]'''（伊勢講）の世話を行い、彼らが[[伊勢参り]]に訪れた際には自己の[[宿坊]]で迎え入れて便宜を図った。同様のことは各地で行われ、[[中世]]から[[近世]]にかけて、御師の間で'''師職'''（御師の[[職の体系|職]]）や檀那の[[相続]]や譲渡・売買が盛んになり、勢力の強い御師のもとに檀那や祈祷料などが集まった。一方で熊野御師は[[熊野信仰]]の衰退とともに衰退した。なお、出雲大社の御師組織は大きくなり、「出雲講」や「甲子講」もでき、[[出雲大社教]]の基礎を築いた。また、地方で出雲御師が布教する丹所（たんしょ）という建物も建設された<ref>出雲大社教教務本庁『出雲大社教布教師養成講習会』1989年9月 全427頁中319頁</ref>。
[[明治]]に入ると、[[日本国政府|政府]]主導の[[神祇官#神祇官の復興|神祇制度]]が整備されたため、急速に御師は衰退する。[[明治]]2年（[[1869年]]）に明治政府は神職の[[葬儀]]は[[神葬祭]]に改めるように命じるとともに、御師は百姓が兼帯しているもので正規の神職では無いため、神葬祭を行う事が禁じられた。御師側はこうした動きに抗議したものの、明治4年（[[1871年]]）7月には御師職そのものが廃止されてしまい、ほとんどの御師は[[平民]]に編入された<ref>[[河口湖]]の川口御師の[[草分け]]とされる三浦家のように[[徳川将軍家]]とのつながり（[[徳川家綱]]誕生の祈祷を行って特権が与えられた）を理由に[[士族]]として扱われた例外もある。</ref>。御師は百姓や宿屋経営などに転じていくことになるが、富士講の御師を結集して[[扶桑教]]を結成するなど、宗教的な活動を維持しようとする動きもあった<ref>（著）高埜利彦 『近世の朝廷と宗教』吉川弘文館、2014年 ISBN 978-4-642-03461-6 P345-48</ref>。
=== 富士御師 === [[天正壬午の乱]]の時、[[渡辺囚獄佑]]が該当し得る。
富士講の御師の、[[旧外川家住宅]]、[[小佐野家住宅]]は、それぞれ[[世界遺産]][[富士山-信仰の対象と芸術の源泉]]の構成資産であり<ref>{{Cite web|和書|title=世界文化遺産 富士山の構成資産一覧 |url=https://www.yamanashi-kankou.jp/heritage/fujisanwatcher/jp/world_heritage/index.html |website=【公式】富士の国やまなし観光ネット 山梨県公式観光情報 |access-date=2022-04-16 |language=ja |last=公益社団法人やまなし観光推進機構}}</ref>、国の[[重要文化財]]に指定されている<ref>{{Cite web|和書|title=旧外川家住宅（山梨県富士吉田市上吉田） 主屋 文化遺産オンライン |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/185831 |website=bunka.nii.ac.jp |access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|和書|title=小佐野家住宅（山梨県富士吉田市上吉田） 主屋 文化遺産オンライン |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/144548 |website=bunka.nii.ac.jp |access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref>。富士講の御師の、[[上文司家住宅]]、[[原家住宅]]は、それぞれ国の「[[登録有形文化財]]（建造物）」に登録されている<ref>{{Cite web|和書|title=国指定文化財等データベース |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/101/00011949 |website=kunishitei.bunka.go.jp |access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|和書|title=国指定文化財等データベース |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/101/00011948 |website=kunishitei.bunka.go.jp |access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref>。
== 御師を題材とした作品 == *御師弥五郎―お伊勢参り道中記（[[西條奈加]]） ISBN 439634015X *[[咲夜姫]]（[[山口歌糸]]） ISBN 4783880204
== 脚注 == <references />
== 関連項目 == *[[羽書]] *[[お蔭参り]] *[[宿坊]] *[[宿泊施設]] *[[伊勢暦]] *[http://www.nikko-annai.com/ 堂者引き] - 日光東照宮の堂者（=参拝客）を引率する江戸時代から続く社寺公認のガイド
{{神道 横}} {{citation needed:おし}} [[Category:仏教僧]] [[Category:仏教の称号・役職]] [[Category:神道の称号・役職]] [[Category:旅行]] [[Category:伊勢神宮]] [[Category:サービス関連の職業]] [[Category:ストリート・カルチャー]] [[Category:富士信仰]] [[Category:浅間神社]] [[Category:mass imported shinto cult pages needing dummy edit for attribution and to be reviewed if a redirect and jawiki link reviewing]][[ja:御師]][[Category:qq]][[Category:Pages needing attribution]]

{{Otheruses|社寺に属する御師|天皇や皇族に音楽を教える御師|御師 (音楽)}} [[File:Oshi 20190826a.jpg|thumb|富士構 御師（2019年8月26日撮影）]] '''御師'''（おし、おんし）とは、特定の[[社寺]]に所属して、その社寺へ[[参詣]]する者や[[信者]]の為に[[祈祷]]、案内をし、参拝・[[宿泊]]などの世話をする神職のことである。特に[[伊勢神宮]]のものは「おんし」と読んだ。御師は街道沿いに集住し、[[御師町]]を形成する。
== 概要 == 本来は「御祈祷師」を略したもので、[[平安時代]]のころから神社に所属する[[社僧]]を指すようになり、後に神社の[[参詣]]の世話をする[[神職]]も指すようになった。
== 歴史 == 平安時代の御師には、[[石清水八幡宮|石清水]]・[[賀茂神社|賀茂]]・[[日吉大社|日吉]]などのものがあるが、代表的なのは[[熊野三山]]の'''熊野御師'''<ref name="ushiyama">牛山佳幸、「中世大原の信仰と文化」 信州大学教育学部紀要 79: 163-172（1993）, {{Hdl|10091/1626}}</ref>である。[[熊野詣]]では平安時代末期に[[貴族]]の間で流行したが、その際の祈祷や宿泊の世話、山内の案内をしたのが熊野御師であった。熊野では当初参詣のつど両者間で[[契約]]していたが、次第に御師を「師」とし「[[先達]]」が率いてきた参詣者（道者）を「[[檀那]]」とする恒常的な関係（師檀関係）を形成していった。 [[鎌倉時代]]には[[武士]]にも広まり、[[室町時代]]には[[農民]]などの[[庶民]]まで檀那とするようになった。
鎌倉時代から室町時代初期にかけては、[[伊勢神宮]]<ref name="ushiyama"/>・[[富士講]]・[[松尾大社|松尾]]・[[三嶋大社|三嶋]]・[[白山]]・[[大山 (神奈川県)|大山]]などの御師も活躍した。特に[[出雲大社]]が[[源頼朝]]の御師を行った事は『[[吾妻鏡]]』という[[鎌倉幕府]]の記録を示したものに記されている<ref>『出雲大社教布教師養成講習会』 発行：出雲大社教教務本庁 平成元年9月 全342頁中</ref>{{信頼性要検証|date=2016年12月}}。
[[江戸時代]]には[[百姓]]と[[神職]]の中間の[[身分]]とされ、経済の安定により庶民の間で寺社詣りが信仰と[[レジャー|遊興]]の側面を併せ持つようになっていく中で、伊勢・[[富士山|富士]]を中心に出雲・[[津島神社|津島]]など多くの神社で御師の制度が発達した。特に伊勢や富士では全国に檀那を持つまでに至った。例えば、伊勢御師は全国各地に派遣され、現地の'''[[講]]'''（伊勢講）の世話を行い、彼らが[[伊勢参り]]に訪れた際には自己の[[宿坊]]で迎え入れて便宜を図った。同様のことは各地で行われ、[[中世]]から[[近世]]にかけて、御師の間で'''師職'''（御師の[[職の体系|職]]）や檀那の[[相続]]や譲渡・売買が盛んになり、勢力の強い御師のもとに檀那や祈祷料などが集まった。一方で熊野御師は[[熊野信仰]]の衰退とともに衰退した。なお、出雲大社の御師組織は大きくなり、「出雲講」や「甲子講」もでき、[[出雲大社教]]の基礎を築いた。また、地方で出雲御師が布教する丹所（たんしょ）という建物も建設された<ref>出雲大社教教務本庁『出雲大社教布教師養成講習会』1989年9月 全427頁中319頁</ref>。
[[明治]]に入ると、[[日本国政府|政府]]主導の[[神祇官#神祇官の復興|神祇制度]]が整備されたため、急速に御師は衰退する。[[明治]]2年（[[1869年]]）に明治政府は神職の[[葬儀]]は[[神葬祭]]に改めるように命じるとともに、御師は百姓が兼帯しているもので正規の神職では無いため、神葬祭を行う事が禁じられた。御師側はこうした動きに抗議したものの、明治4年（[[1871年]]）7月には御師職そのものが廃止されてしまい、ほとんどの御師は[[平民]]に編入された<ref>[[河口湖]]の川口御師の[[草分け]]とされる三浦家のように[[徳川将軍家]]とのつながり（[[徳川家綱]]誕生の祈祷を行って特権が与えられた）を理由に[[士族]]として扱われた例外もある。</ref>。御師は百姓や宿屋経営などに転じていくことになるが、富士講の御師を結集して[[扶桑教]]を結成するなど、宗教的な活動を維持しようとする動きもあった<ref>（著）高埜利彦 『近世の朝廷と宗教』吉川弘文館、2014年 ISBN 978-4-642-03461-6 P345-48</ref>。
=== 富士御師 === [[天正壬午の乱]]の時、[[渡辺囚獄佑]]が該当し得る。
富士講の御師の、[[旧外川家住宅]]、[[小佐野家住宅]]は、それぞれ[[世界遺産]][[富士山-信仰の対象と芸術の源泉]]の構成資産であり<ref>{{Cite web|和書|title=世界文化遺産 富士山の構成資産一覧 |url=https://www.yamanashi-kankou.jp/heritage/fujisanwatcher/jp/world_heritage/index.html |website=【公式】富士の国やまなし観光ネット 山梨県公式観光情報 |access-date=2022-04-16 |language=ja |last=公益社団法人やまなし観光推進機構}}</ref>、国の[[重要文化財]]に指定されている<ref>{{Cite web|和書|title=旧外川家住宅（山梨県富士吉田市上吉田） 主屋 文化遺産オンライン |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/185831 |website=bunka.nii.ac.jp |access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|和書|title=小佐野家住宅（山梨県富士吉田市上吉田） 主屋 文化遺産オンライン |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/144548 |website=bunka.nii.ac.jp |access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref>。富士講の御師の、[[上文司家住宅]]、[[原家住宅]]は、それぞれ国の「[[登録有形文化財]]（建造物）」に登録されている<ref>{{Cite web|和書|title=国指定文化財等データベース |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/101/00011949 |website=kunishitei.bunka.go.jp |access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|和書|title=国指定文化財等データベース |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/101/00011948 |website=kunishitei.bunka.go.jp |access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref>。
== 御師を題材とした作品 == *御師弥五郎―お伊勢参り道中記（[[西條奈加]]） ISBN 439634015X *[[咲夜姫]]（[[山口歌糸]]） ISBN 4783880204

{{Otheruses|社寺に属する御師|天皇や皇族に音楽を教える御師|御師 (音楽)}} [[File:Oshi 20190826a.jpg|thumb|富士構 御師（2019年8月26日撮影）]] '''御師'''（おし、おんし）とは、特定の[[社寺]]に所属して、その社寺へ[[参詣]]する者や[[信者]]の為に[[祈祷]]、案内をし、参拝・[[宿泊]]などの世話をする神職のことである。特に[[伊勢神宮]]のものは「おんし」と読んだ。御師は街道沿いに集住し、[[御師町]]を形成する。

[[明治]]に入ると、[[日本国政府|政府]]主導の[[神祇官#神祇官の復興|神祇制度]]が整備されたため、急速に御師は衰退する。[[明治]]2年（[[1869年]]）に明治政府は神職の[[葬儀]]は[[神葬祭]]に改めるように命じるとともに、御師は百姓が兼帯しているもので正規の神職では無いため、神葬祭を行う事が禁じられた。御師側はこうした動きに抗議したものの、明治4年（[[1871年]]）7月には御師職そのものが廃止されてしまい、ほとんどの御師は[[平民]]に編入された<ref>[[河口湖]]の川口御師の[[草分け]]とされる三浦家のように[[徳川将軍家]]とのつながり（[[徳川家綱]]誕生の祈祷を行って特権が与えられた）を理由に[[士族]]として扱われた例外もある。</ref>。御師は百姓や宿屋経営などに転じていくことになるが、富士講の御師を結集して[[扶桑教]]を結成するなど、宗教的な活動を維持しようとする動きもあった<ref>（著）高埜利彦 『近世の朝廷と宗教』吉川弘文館、2014年 ISBN 978-4-642-03461-6 P345-48</ref>。
=== 富士御師 === [[天正壬午の乱]]の時、[[渡辺囚獄佑]]が該当し得る。
富士講の御師の、[[旧外川家住宅]]、[[小佐野家住宅]]は、それぞれ[[世界遺産]][[富士山-信仰の対象と芸術の源泉]]の構成資産であり<ref>{{Cite web|和書|title=世界文化遺産 富士山の構成資産一覧 |url=https://www.yamanashi-kankou.jp/heritage/fujisanwatcher/jp/world_heritage/index.html |website=【公式】富士の国やまなし観光ネット 山梨県公式観光情報 |access-date=2022-04-16 |language=ja |last=公益社団法人やまなし観光推進機構}}</ref>、国の[[重要文化財]]に指定されている<ref>{{Cite web|和書|title=旧外川家住宅（山梨県富士吉田市上吉田） 主屋 文化遺産オンライン |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/185831 |website=bunka.nii.ac.jp |access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|和書|title=小佐野家住宅（山梨県富士吉田市上吉田） 主屋 文化遺産オンライン |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/144548 |website=bunka.nii.ac.jp |access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref>。富士講の御師の、[[上文司家住宅]]、[[原家住宅]]は、それぞれ国の「[[登録有形文化財]]（建造物）」に登録されている<ref>{{Cite web|和書|title=国指定文化財等データベース |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/101/00011949 |website=kunishitei.bunka.go.jp |access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|和書|title=国指定文化財等データベース |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/101/00011948 |website=kunishitei.bunka.go.jp |access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref>。
== 御師を題材とした作品 == *御師弥五郎―お伊勢参り道中記（[[西條奈加]]） ISBN 439634015X *[[咲夜姫]]（[[山口歌糸]]） ISBN 4783880204
== 脚注 == <references />
== 関連項目 == *[[羽書]] *[[お蔭参り]] *[[宿坊]] *[[宿泊施設]] *[[伊勢暦]] *[http://www.nikko-annai.com/ 堂者引き] - 日光東照宮の堂者（=参拝客）を引率する江戸時代から続く社寺公認のガイド
{{神道 横}} {{citation needed:おし}} [[Category:仏教僧]] [[Category:仏教の称号・役職]] [[Category:神道の称号・役職]] [[Category:旅行]] [[Category:伊勢神宮]] [[Category:サービス関連の職業]] [[Category:ストリート・カルチャー]] [[Category:富士信仰]] [[Category:浅間神社]] [[Category:mass imported shinto cult pages needing dummy edit for attribution and to be reviewed if a redirect and jawiki link reviewing]][[ja:御師]][[Category:qq]]

{{Otheruses|社寺に属する御師|天皇や皇族に音楽を教える御師|御師 (音楽)}} [[File:Oshi 20190826a.jpg|thumb|富士構 御師（2019年8月26日撮影）]] '''御師'''（おし、おんし）とは、特定の[[社寺]]に所属して、その社寺へ[[参詣]]する者や[[信者]]の為に[[祈祷]]、案内をし、参拝・[[宿泊]]などの世話をする神職のことである。特に[[伊勢神宮]]のものは「おんし」と読んだ。御師は街道沿いに集住し、[[御師町]]を形成する。
== 概要 == 本来は「御祈祷師」を略したもので、[[平安時代]]のころから神社に所属する[[社僧]]を指すようになり、後に神社の[[参詣]]の世話をする[[神職]]も指すようになった。
== 歴史 == 平安時代の御師には、[[石清水八幡宮|石清水]]・[[賀茂神社|賀茂]]・[[日吉大社|日吉]]などのものがあるが、代表的なのは[[熊野三山]]の'''熊野御師'''<ref name="ushiyama">牛山佳幸、「中世大原の信仰と文化」 信州大学教育学部紀要 79: 163-172（1993）, {{Hdl|10091/1626}}</ref>である。[[熊野詣]]では平安時代末期に[[貴族]]の間で流行したが、その際の祈祷や宿泊の世話、山内の案内をしたのが熊野御師であった。熊野では当初参詣のつど両者間で[[契約]]していたが、次第に御師を「師」とし「[[先達]]」が率いてきた参詣者（道者）を「[[檀那]]」とする恒常的な関係（師檀関係）を形成していった。 [[鎌倉時代]]には[[武士]]にも広まり、[[室町時代]]には[[農民]]などの[[庶民]]まで檀那とするようになった。
鎌倉時代から室町時代初期にかけては、[[伊勢神宮]]<ref name="ushiyama"/>・[[富士講]]・[[松尾大社|松尾]]・[[三嶋大社|三嶋]]・[[白山]]・[[大山 (神奈川県)|大山]]などの御師も活躍した。特に[[出雲大社]]が[[源頼朝]]の御師を行った事は『[[吾妻鏡]]』という[[鎌倉幕府]]の記録を示したものに記されている<ref>『出雲大社教布教師養成講習会』 発行：出雲大社教教務本庁 平成元年9月 全342頁中</ref>{{信頼性要検証|date=2016年12月}}。
[[江戸時代]]には[[百姓]]と[[神職]]の中間の[[身分]]とされ、経済の安定により庶民の間で寺社詣りが信仰と[[レジャー|遊興]]の側面を併せ持つようになっていく中で、伊勢・[[富士山|富士]]を中心に出雲・[[津島神社|津島]]など多くの神社で御師の制度が発達した。特に伊勢や富士では全国に檀那を持つまでに至った。例えば、伊勢御師は全国各地に派遣され、現地の'''[[講]]'''（伊勢講）の世話を行い、彼らが[[伊勢参り]]に訪れた際には自己の[[宿坊]]で迎え入れて便宜を図った。同様のことは各地で行われ、[[中世]]から[[近世]]にかけて、御師の間で'''師職'''（御師の[[職の体系|職]]）や檀那の[[相続]]や譲渡・売買が盛んになり、勢力の強い御師のもとに檀那や祈祷料などが集まった。一方で熊野御師は[[熊野信仰]]の衰退とともに衰退した。なお、出雲大社の御師組織は大きくなり、「出雲講」や「甲子講」もでき、[[出雲大社教]]の基礎を築いた。また、地方で出雲御師が布教する丹所（たんしょ）という建物も建設された<ref>出雲大社教教務本庁『出雲大社教布教師養成講習会』1989年9月 全427頁中319頁</ref>。
[[明治]]に入ると、[[日本国政府|政府]]主導の[[神祇官#神祇官の復興|神祇制度]]が整備されたため、急速に御師は衰退する。[[明治]]2年（[[1869年]]）に明治政府は神職の[[葬儀]]は[[神葬祭]]に改めるように命じるとともに、御師は百姓が兼帯しているもので正規の神職では無いため、神葬祭を行う事が禁じられた。御師側はこうした動きに抗議したものの、明治4年（[[1871年]]）7月には御師職そのものが廃止されてしまい、ほとんどの御師は[[平民]]に編入された<ref>[[河口湖]]の川口御師の[[草分け]]とされる三浦家のように[[徳川将軍家]]とのつながり（[[徳川家綱]]誕生の祈祷を行って特権が与えられた）を理由に[[士族]]として扱われた例外もある。</ref>。御師は百姓や宿屋経営などに転じていくことになるが、富士講の御師を結集して[[扶桑教]]を結成するなど、宗教的な活動を維持しようとする動きもあった<ref>（著）高埜利彦 『近世の朝廷と宗教』吉川弘文館、2014年 ISBN 978-4-642-03461-6 P345-48</ref>。
=== 富士御師 === [[天正壬午の乱]]の時、[[渡辺囚獄佑]]が該当し得る。
富士講の御師の、[[旧外川家住宅]]、[[小佐野家住宅]]は、それぞれ[[世界遺産]][[富士山-信仰の対象と芸術の源泉]]の構成資産であり<ref>{{Cite web|和書|title=世界文化遺産 富士山の構成資産一覧 |url=https://www.yamanashi-kankou.jp/heritage/fujisanwatcher/jp/world_heritage/index.html |website=【公式】富士の国やまなし観光ネット 山梨県公式観光情報 |access-date=2022-04-16 |language=ja |last=公益社団法人やまなし観光推進機構}}</ref>、国の[[重要文化財]]に指定されている<ref>{{Cite web|和書|title=旧外川家住宅（山梨県富士吉田市上吉田） 主屋 文化遺産オンライン |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/185831 |website=bunka.nii.ac.jp |access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|和書|title=小佐野家住宅（山梨県富士吉田市上吉田） 主屋 文化遺産オンライン |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/144548 |website=bunka.nii.ac.jp |access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref>。富士講の御師の、[[上文司家住宅]]、[[原家住宅]]は、それぞれ国の「[[登録有形文化財]]（建造物）」に登録されている<ref>{{Cite web|和書|title=国指定文化財等データベース |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/101/00011949 |website=kunishitei.bunka.go.jp |access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|和書|title=国指定文化財等データベース |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/101/00011948 |website=kunishitei.bunka.go.jp |access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref>。
== 御師を題材とした作品 == *御師弥五郎―お伊勢参り道中記（[[西條奈加]]） ISBN 439634015X *[[咲夜姫]]（[[山口歌糸]]） ISBN 4783880204
== 脚注 == <references />
== 関連項目 == *[[羽書]] *[[お蔭参り]] *[[宿坊]] *[[宿泊施設]] *[[伊勢暦]] *[http://www.nikko-annai.com/ 堂者引き] - 日光東照宮の堂者（=参拝客）を引率する江戸時代から続く社寺公認のガイド
{{神道 横}} {{citation needed:おし}} [[Category:仏教僧]] [[Category:仏教の称号・役職]] [[Category:神道の称号・役職]] [[Category:旅行]] [[Category:伊勢神宮]] [[Category:サービス関連の職業]] [[Category:ストリート・カルチャー]] [[Category:富士信仰]] [[Category:浅間神社]] [[Category:mass imported shinto cult pages needing dummy edit for attribution and to be reviewed if a redirect and jawiki link reviewing]][[ja:御師]][[Category:qq]]
#REDIRECT [[Onshi]]

{{Otheruses|Shrine and temple guides|Music teachers for the emperor or imperial family|{{ill|御師 (音楽)|ja|御師 (音楽)}}}} [[File:Oshi 20190826a.jpg|thumb|Fuji-ko Guide (御師), photographed August 26, 2019]] '''Oshi''' (御師) refers to shrine priests associated with specific {{ill|shrines and temples|ja|社寺}}, who perform {{ill|prayers|ja|祈祷}}, guide worshippers, and assist pilgrims with visits and accommodations. In the case of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}, it was traditionally pronounced as "onshi." These guides often formed concentrated communities along highways, known as {{ill|Oshi towns|ja|御師町}}.
== Overview == Originally an abbreviation for "御祈祷師" (prayer guides), the term "oshi" emerged during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, initially referring to {{ill|temple monks|ja|社僧}}. Later, it extended to include shrine priests who facilitated pilgrimages.
== History == During the Heian period, notable examples of oshi included those from {{ill|Iwashimizu Hachiman Shrine|ja|石清水八幡宮}}, {{ill|Kamo Shrine|ja|賀茂神社}}, and {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|ja|日吉大社}}, with the {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} "Kumano Oshi" being particularly famous.<ref name="ushiyama">Yoshiyuki Ushiyama, "Faith and Culture in Medieval Ōhara," Shinshu University Bulletin of Education, Vol. 79 (1993), {{hdl|10091/1626}}</ref> The Kumano Oshi organized prayers, accommodations, and guided noble pilgrims during the late Heian period's {{ill|Kumano pilgrimage|ja|熊野詣}}. Relationships between oshi and pilgrims evolved into stable "master-disciple" ties, known as {{ill|師檀関係|ja|師檀関係}}, encompassing even commoners by the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}.
Between the Kamakura and {{ill|Muromachi periods|ja|室町時代}}, other prominent oshi groups emerged, such as those from {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}, {{ill|Fuji-ko|ja|富士講}}, {{ill|Matsuo Shrine|ja|松尾大社}}, {{ill|Mishima Taisha|ja|三嶋大社}}, and {{ill|Hakusan Shrine|ja|白山}}. Notably, records like the {{ill|Azuma Kagami|ja|吾妻鏡}} document oshi from {{ill|Izumo Taisha|ja|出雲大社}} serving {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝}}.<ref>'Izumo Taisha Teacher Training Manual,' Izumo Taisha Headquarters, 1989, p. 319</ref>
During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, oshi played an essential role as intermediaries between commoners and shrine priests. Pilgrimages like {{ill|Ise pilgrimage|ja|伊勢参り}} and those to {{ill|Mt. Fuji|ja|富士山}} became widely accessible. Oshi organized {{ill|Kō (lecture)|ja|講|Kō (lecture)=Kō (lecture)|lt=kō (pilgrimage associations)}} and hosted pilgrims in their {{ill|Shukubo|ja|宿坊|Shukubo=Shukubo|lt=shukubō (pilgrim lodgings)}}. The economic stability of the Edo period allowed pilgrimages to combine faith and leisure, enhancing oshi influence. However, groups like the Kumano Oshi declined alongside {{ill|Kumano faith|ja|熊野信仰}}.
In Izumo, oshi structures laid the foundation for the {{ill|Izumo Taisha Faith|ja|出雲大社教}}, creating facilities such as "tanjo" (mission centers) to support local preaching.<ref>'Izumo Taisha Teacher Training Manual,' Izumo Taisha Headquarters, 1989, p. 319</ref>
With the establishment of the {{ill|Meiji government|ja|明治政府}}'s {{ill|Shinto|ja|神祇}} system, oshi rapidly declined. In 1869, oshi were barred from conducting {{ill|Shinto funerals|ja|神葬祭}}, and by 1871, the profession was abolished. Most oshi transitioned into farming or running inns. Some attempted to continue religious activities, forming organizations such as {{ill|Fusōkyō|ja|扶桑教}}.<ref>Takahiko Takano, "The Court and Religion in Early Modern Japan," Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2014, ISBN 978-4-642-03461-6, pp. 345-348.</ref>
=== Fuji Oshi === In the {{ill|Tenshōjingo Conflict|ja|天正壬午の乱}}, figures like Watanabe Shūgokuyū played significant roles. Notable Fuji Oshi residences, including {{ill|Old Togawa Residence|ja|旧外川家住宅}} and {{ill|Kosano Residence|ja|小佐野家住宅}}, are designated as {{ill|World Heritage Sites|ja|世界遺産}} and {{ill|Important Cultural Properties of Japan|ja|重要文化財}}.<ref>{{Cite web|title=World Cultural Heritage: Mt. Fuji Components|url=https://www.yamanashi-kankou.jp/heritage/fujisanwatcher/jp/world_heritage/index.html|publisher=Yamanashi Tourism Organization|access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Old Togawa Residence|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/185831|website=National Cultural Heritage Database|access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref>
== Cultural Depictions == * "Oshi Yagorō: Journey to Ise" by {{ill|Saijo Naka|ja|西條奈加}}, ISBN 439634015X * "{{ill|Sakuya-hime|ja|咲夜姫}}" by {{ill|Yamaguchi Kaito|ja|山口歌糸}}, ISBN 4783880204

revision.text:
{{Otheruses|Shrine and temple guides|Music teachers for the emperor or imperial family|{{ill|御師 (音楽)|ja|御師 (音楽)}}}} [[File:Oshi 20190826a.jpg|thumb|Fuji-ko Guide (御師), photographed August 26, 2019]] '''Oshi''' (御師) refers to shrine priests associated with specific {{ill|shrines and temples|ja|社寺}}, who perform {{ill|prayers|ja|祈祷}}, guide worshippers, and assist pilgrims with visits and accommodations. In the case of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}, it was traditionally pronounced as "onshi." These guides often formed concentrated communities along highways, known as {{ill|Oshi towns|ja|御師町}}.
== Overview == Originally an abbreviation for "御祈祷師" (prayer guides), the term "oshi" emerged during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, initially referring to {{ill|temple monks|ja|社僧}}. Later, it extended to include shrine priests who facilitated pilgrimages.
== History == During the Heian period, notable examples of oshi included those from {{ill|Iwashimizu Hachiman Shrine|ja|石清水八幡宮}}, {{ill|Kamo Shrine|ja|賀茂神社}}, and {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|ja|日吉大社}}, with the {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} "Kumano Oshi" being particularly famous.<ref name="ushiyama">Yoshiyuki Ushiyama, "Faith and Culture in Medieval Ōhara," Shinshu University Bulletin of Education, Vol. 79 (1993), {{hdl|10091/1626}}</ref> The Kumano Oshi organized prayers, accommodations, and guided noble pilgrims during the late Heian period's {{ill|Kumano pilgrimage|ja|熊野詣}}. Relationships between oshi and pilgrims evolved into stable "master-disciple" ties, known as {{ill|師檀関係|ja|師檀関係}}, encompassing even commoners by the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}.
Between the Kamakura and {{ill|Muromachi periods|ja|室町時代}}, other prominent oshi groups emerged, such as those from {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}, {{ill|Fuji-ko|ja|富士講}}, {{ill|Matsuo Shrine|ja|松尾大社}}, {{ill|Mishima Taisha|ja|三嶋大社}}, and {{ill|Hakusan Shrine|ja|白山}}. Notably, records like the {{ill|Azuma Kagami|ja|吾妻鏡}} document oshi from {{ill|Izumo Taisha|ja|出雲大社}} serving {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝}}.<ref>'Izumo Taisha Teacher Training Manual,' Izumo Taisha Headquarters, 1989, p. 319</ref>
During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, oshi played an essential role as intermediaries between commoners and shrine priests. Pilgrimages like {{ill|Ise pilgrimage|ja|伊勢参り}} and those to {{ill|Mt. Fuji|ja|富士山}} became widely accessible. Oshi organized {{ill|Kō (lecture)|ja|講|Kō (lecture)=Kō (lecture)|lt=kō (pilgrimage associations)}} and hosted pilgrims in their {{ill|Shukubo|ja|宿坊|Shukubo=Shukubo|lt=shukubō (pilgrim lodgings)}}. The economic stability of the Edo period allowed pilgrimages to combine faith and leisure, enhancing oshi influence. However, groups like the Kumano Oshi declined alongside {{ill|Kumano faith|ja|熊野信仰}}.
In Izumo, oshi structures laid the foundation for the {{ill|Izumo Taisha Faith|ja|出雲大社教}}, creating facilities such as "tanjo" (mission centers) to support local preaching.<ref>'Izumo Taisha Teacher Training Manual,' Izumo Taisha Headquarters, 1989, p. 319</ref>
With the establishment of the {{ill|Meiji government|ja|明治政府}}'s {{ill|Shinto|ja|神祇}} system, oshi rapidly declined. In 1869, oshi were barred from conducting {{ill|Shinto funerals|ja|神葬祭}}, and by 1871, the profession was abolished. Most oshi transitioned into farming or running inns. Some attempted to continue religious activities, forming organizations such as {{ill|Fusōkyō|ja|扶桑教}}.<ref>Takahiko Takano, "The Court and Religion in Early Modern Japan," Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2014, ISBN 978-4-642-03461-6, pp. 345-348.</ref>
=== Fuji Oshi === In the {{ill|Tenshōjingo Conflict|ja|天正壬午の乱}}, figures like Watanabe Shūgokuyū played significant roles. Notable Fuji Oshi residences, including {{ill|Old Togawa Residence|ja|旧外川家住宅}} and {{ill|Kosano Residence|ja|小佐野家住宅}}, are designated as {{ill|World Heritage Sites|ja|世界遺産}} and {{ill|Important Cultural Properties of Japan|ja|重要文化財}}.<ref>{{Cite web|title=World Cultural Heritage: Mt. Fuji Components|url=https://www.yamanashi-kankou.jp/heritage/fujisanwatcher/jp/world_heritage/index.html|publisher=Yamanashi Tourism Organization|access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Old Togawa Residence|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/185831|website=National Cultural Heritage Database|access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref>
== Cultural Depictions == * "Oshi Yagorō: Journey to Ise" by {{ill|Saijo Naka|ja|西條奈加}}, ISBN 439634015X * "{{ill|Sakuya-hime|ja|咲夜姫}}" by {{ill|Yamaguchi Kaito|ja|山口歌糸}}, ISBN 4783880204
== References == <references />
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Hagaki|ja|羽書}} * {{ill|Okage-mairi|ja|お蔭参り}} * {{ill|Shukubō|ja|宿坊}} * {{ill|Accommodation facilities|ja|宿泊施設}} * {{ill|Ise Calendar|ja|伊勢暦}} * [http://www.nikko-annai.com/ Dōshabiki] - Edo-period guides for worshippers at {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|ja|日光東照宮}}

[[Category:qq-{{subst:MAINSPACE}}--qq]] [[Category:qq-Onshi-qq]] [[Category:Oshi]][[Category:Asama Shrines]] [[Category:Category:Kk]] [[Category:refreshing imports]] [[ja:御師]]

{{Otheruses|Shrine and temple guides|Music teachers for the emperor or imperial family|{{ill|御師 (音楽)|ja|御師 (音楽)}}}} [[File:Oshi 20190826a.jpg|thumb|Fuji-ko Guide (御師), photographed August 26, 2019]] '''Oshi''' (御師) refers to shrine priests associated with specific {{ill|shrines and temples|ja|社寺}}, who perform {{ill|prayers|ja|祈祷}}, guide worshippers, and assist pilgrims with visits and accommodations. In the case of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}, it was traditionally pronounced as "onshi." These guides often formed concentrated communities along highways, known as {{ill|Oshi towns|ja|御師町}}.
== Overview == Originally an abbreviation for "御祈祷師" (prayer guides), the term "oshi" emerged during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, initially referring to {{ill|temple monks|ja|社僧}}. Later, it extended to include shrine priests who facilitated pilgrimages.
== History == During the Heian period, notable examples of oshi included those from {{ill|Iwashimizu Hachiman Shrine|ja|石清水八幡宮}}, {{ill|Kamo Shrine|ja|賀茂神社}}, and {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|ja|日吉大社}}, with the {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} "Kumano Oshi" being particularly famous.<ref name="ushiyama">Yoshiyuki Ushiyama, "Faith and Culture in Medieval Ōhara," Shinshu University Bulletin of Education, Vol. 79 (1993), {{hdl|10091/1626}}</ref> The Kumano Oshi organized prayers, accommodations, and guided noble pilgrims during the late Heian period's {{ill|Kumano pilgrimage|ja|熊野詣}}. Relationships between oshi and pilgrims evolved into stable "master-disciple" ties, known as {{ill|師檀関係|ja|師檀関係}}, encompassing even commoners by the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}.
Between the Kamakura and {{ill|Muromachi periods|ja|室町時代}}, other prominent oshi groups emerged, such as those from {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}, {{ill|Fuji-ko|ja|富士講}}, {{ill|Matsuo Shrine|ja|松尾大社}}, {{ill|Mishima Taisha|ja|三嶋大社}}, and {{ill|Hakusan Shrine|ja|白山}}. Notably, records like the {{ill|Azuma Kagami|ja|吾妻鏡}} document oshi from {{ill|Izumo Taisha|ja|出雲大社}} serving {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝}}.<ref>'Izumo Taisha Teacher Training Manual,' Izumo Taisha Headquarters, 1989, p. 319</ref>
During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, oshi played an essential role as intermediaries between commoners and shrine priests. Pilgrimages like {{ill|Ise pilgrimage|ja|伊勢参り}} and those to {{ill|Mt. Fuji|ja|富士山}} became widely accessible. Oshi organized {{ill|Kō (lecture)|ja|講|Kō (lecture)=Kō (lecture)|lt=kō (pilgrimage associations)}} and hosted pilgrims in their {{ill|Shukubo|ja|宿坊|Shukubo=Shukubo|lt=shukubō (pilgrim lodgings)}}. The economic stability of the Edo period allowed pilgrimages to combine faith and leisure, enhancing oshi influence. However, groups like the Kumano Oshi declined alongside {{ill|Kumano faith|ja|熊野信仰}}.
In Izumo, oshi structures laid the foundation for the {{ill|Izumo Taisha Faith|ja|出雲大社教}}, creating facilities such as "tanjo" (mission centers) to support local preaching.<ref>'Izumo Taisha Teacher Training Manual,' Izumo Taisha Headquarters, 1989, p. 319</ref>
With the establishment of the {{ill|Meiji government|ja|明治政府}}'s {{ill|Shinto|ja|神祇}} system, oshi rapidly declined. In 1869, oshi were barred from conducting {{ill|Shinto funerals|ja|神葬祭}}, and by 1871, the profession was abolished. Most oshi transitioned into farming or running inns. Some attempted to continue religious activities, forming organizations such as {{ill|Fusōkyō|ja|扶桑教}}.<ref>Takahiko Takano, "The Court and Religion in Early Modern Japan," Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2014, ISBN 978-4-642-03461-6, pp. 345-348.</ref>
=== Fuji Oshi === In the {{ill|Tenshōjingo Conflict|ja|天正壬午の乱}}, figures like Watanabe Shūgokuyū played significant roles. Notable Fuji Oshi residences, including {{ill|Old Togawa Residence|ja|旧外川家住宅}} and {{ill|Kosano Residence|ja|小佐野家住宅}}, are designated as {{ill|World Heritage Sites|ja|世界遺産}} and {{ill|Important Cultural Properties of Japan|ja|重要文化財}}.<ref>{{Cite web|title=World Cultural Heritage: Mt. Fuji Components|url=https://www.yamanashi-kankou.jp/heritage/fujisanwatcher/jp/world_heritage/index.html|publisher=Yamanashi Tourism Organization|access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Old Togawa Residence|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/185831|website=National Cultural Heritage Database|access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref>
== Cultural Depictions == * "Oshi Yagorō: Journey to Ise" by {{ill|Saijo Naka|ja|西條奈加}}, ISBN 439634015X * "{{ill|Sakuya-hime|ja|咲夜姫}}" by {{ill|Yamaguchi Kaito|ja|山口歌糸}}, ISBN 4783880204
== References == <references />

[[Category:qq-{{subst:MAINSPACE}}--qq]] [[Category:qq-Onshi-qq]] [[Category:Oshi]][[Category:Asama Shrines]] [[Category:Category:Kk]] [[Category:refreshing imports]] [[Category:q--1]] [[Category:q--q]] [[ja:御師]]

{{Otheruses|Shrine and temple guides|Music teachers for the emperor or imperial family|{{ill|御師 (音楽)|ja|御師 (音楽)}}}} [[File:Oshi 20190826a.jpg|thumb|Fuji-ko Guide (御師), photographed August 26, 2019]] '''Oshi''' (御師) refers to shrine priests associated with specific {{ill|shrines and temples|ja|社寺}}, who perform {{ill|prayers|ja|祈祷}}, guide worshippers, and assist pilgrims with visits and accommodations. In the case of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}, it was traditionally pronounced as "onshi." These guides often formed concentrated communities along highways, known as {{ill|Oshi towns|ja|御師町}}.
== Overview == Originally an abbreviation for "御祈祷師" (prayer guides), the term "oshi" emerged during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, initially referring to {{ill|temple monks|ja|社僧}}. Later, it extended to include shrine priests who facilitated pilgrimages.
== History == During the Heian period, notable examples of oshi included those from {{ill|Iwashimizu Hachiman Shrine|ja|石清水八幡宮}}, {{ill|Kamo Shrine|ja|賀茂神社}}, and {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|ja|日吉大社}}, with the {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} "Kumano Oshi" being particularly famous.<ref name="ushiyama">Yoshiyuki Ushiyama, "Faith and Culture in Medieval Ōhara," Shinshu University Bulletin of Education, Vol. 79 (1993), {{hdl|10091/1626}}</ref> The Kumano Oshi organized prayers, accommodations, and guided noble pilgrims during the late Heian period's {{ill|Kumano pilgrimage|ja|熊野詣}}. Relationships between oshi and pilgrims evolved into stable "master-disciple" ties, known as {{ill|師檀関係|ja|師檀関係}}, encompassing even commoners by the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}.
Between the Kamakura and {{ill|Muromachi periods|ja|室町時代}}, other prominent oshi groups emerged, such as those from {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}, {{ill|Fuji-ko|ja|富士講}}, {{ill|Matsuo Shrine|ja|松尾大社}}, {{ill|Mishima Taisha|ja|三嶋大社}}, and {{ill|Hakusan Shrine|ja|白山}}. Notably, records like the {{ill|Azuma Kagami|ja|吾妻鏡}} document oshi from {{ill|Izumo Taisha|ja|出雲大社}} serving {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝}}.<ref>'Izumo Taisha Teacher Training Manual,' Izumo Taisha Headquarters, 1989, p. 319</ref>
During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, oshi played an essential role as intermediaries between commoners and shrine priests. Pilgrimages like {{ill|Ise pilgrimage|ja|伊勢参り}} and those to {{ill|Mt. Fuji|ja|富士山}} became widely accessible. Oshi organized {{ill|Kō (lecture)|ja|講|Kō (lecture)=Kō (lecture)|lt=kō (pilgrimage associations)}} and hosted pilgrims in their {{ill|Shukubo|ja|宿坊|Shukubo=Shukubo|lt=shukubō (pilgrim lodgings)}}. The economic stability of the Edo period allowed pilgrimages to combine faith and leisure, enhancing oshi influence. However, groups like the Kumano Oshi declined alongside {{ill|Kumano faith|ja|熊野信仰}}.
In Izumo, oshi structures laid the foundation for the {{ill|Izumo Taisha Faith|ja|出雲大社教}}, creating facilities such as "tanjo" (mission centers) to support local preaching.<ref>'Izumo Taisha Teacher Training Manual,' Izumo Taisha Headquarters, 1989, p. 319</ref>
With the establishment of the {{ill|Meiji government|ja|明治政府}}'s {{ill|Shinto|ja|神祇}} system, oshi rapidly declined. In 1869, oshi were barred from conducting {{ill|Shinto funerals|ja|神葬祭}}, and by 1871, the profession was abolished. Most oshi transitioned into farming or running inns. Some attempted to continue religious activities, forming organizations such as {{ill|Fusōkyō|ja|扶桑教}}.<ref>Takahiko Takano, "The Court and Religion in Early Modern Japan," Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2014, ISBN 978-4-642-03461-6, pp. 345-348.</ref>
=== Fuji Oshi === In the {{ill|Tenshōjingo Conflict|ja|天正壬午の乱}}, figures like Watanabe Shūgokuyū played significant roles. Notable Fuji Oshi residences, including {{ill|Old Togawa Residence|ja|旧外川家住宅}} and {{ill|Kosano Residence|ja|小佐野家住宅}}, are designated as {{ill|World Heritage Sites|ja|世界遺産}} and {{ill|Important Cultural Properties of Japan|ja|重要文化財}}.<ref>{{Cite web|title=World Cultural Heritage: Mt. Fuji Components|url=https://www.yamanashi-kankou.jp/heritage/fujisanwatcher/jp/world_heritage/index.html|publisher=Yamanashi Tourism Organization|access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Old Togawa Residence|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/185831|website=National Cultural Heritage Database|access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref>
== Cultural Depictions == * "Oshi Yagorō: Journey to Ise" by {{ill|Saijo Naka|ja|西條奈加}}, ISBN 439634015X * "{{ill|Sakuya-hime|ja|咲夜姫}}" by {{ill|Yamaguchi Kaito|ja|山口歌糸}}, ISBN 4783880204

[[Category:q--q]] [[Category:Subst:MAINSPACE]] [[Category:q--q]] [[ja:御師]]

{{Otheruses|Shrine and temple guides|Music teachers for the emperor or imperial family|{{ill|御師 (音楽)|ja|御師 (音楽)}}}} [[File:Oshi 20190826a.jpg|thumb|Fuji-ko Guide (御師), photographed August 26, 2019]] '''Oshi''' (御師) refers to shrine priests associated with specific {{ill|shrines and temples|ja|社寺}}, who perform {{ill|prayers|ja|祈祷}}, guide worshippers, and assist pilgrims with visits and accommodations. In the case of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}, it was traditionally pronounced as "onshi." These guides often formed concentrated communities along highways, known as {{ill|Oshi towns|ja|御師町}}.
== Overview == Originally an abbreviation for "御祈祷師" (prayer guides), the term "oshi" emerged during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, initially referring to {{ill|temple monks|ja|社僧}}. Later, it extended to include shrine priests who facilitated pilgrimages.
== History == During the Heian period, notable examples of oshi included those from {{ill|Iwashimizu Hachiman Shrine|ja|石清水八幡宮}}, {{ill|Kamo Shrine|ja|賀茂神社}}, and {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|ja|日吉大社}}, with the {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} "Kumano Oshi" being particularly famous.<ref name="ushiyama">Yoshiyuki Ushiyama, "Faith and Culture in Medieval Ōhara," Shinshu University Bulletin of Education, Vol. 79 (1993), {{hdl|10091/1626}}</ref> The Kumano Oshi organized prayers, accommodations, and guided noble pilgrims during the late Heian period's {{ill|Kumano pilgrimage|ja|熊野詣}}. Relationships between oshi and pilgrims evolved into stable "master-disciple" ties, known as {{ill|師檀関係|ja|師檀関係}}, encompassing even commoners by the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}.
Between the Kamakura and {{ill|Muromachi periods|ja|室町時代}}, other prominent oshi groups emerged, such as those from {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}, {{ill|Fuji-ko|ja|富士講}}, {{ill|Matsuo Shrine|ja|松尾大社}}, {{ill|Mishima Taisha|ja|三嶋大社}}, and {{ill|Hakusan Shrine|ja|白山}}. Notably, records like the {{ill|Azuma Kagami|ja|吾妻鏡}} document oshi from {{ill|Izumo Taisha|ja|出雲大社}} serving {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝}}.<ref>'Izumo Taisha Teacher Training Manual,' Izumo Taisha Headquarters, 1989, p. 319</ref>
During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, oshi played an essential role as intermediaries between commoners and shrine priests. Pilgrimages like {{ill|Ise pilgrimage|ja|伊勢参り}} and those to {{ill|Mt. Fuji|ja|富士山}} became widely accessible. Oshi organized {{ill|Kō (lecture)|ja|講|Kō (lecture)=Kō (lecture)|lt=kō (pilgrimage associations)}} and hosted pilgrims in their {{ill|Shukubo|ja|宿坊|Shukubo=Shukubo|lt=shukubō (pilgrim lodgings)}}. The economic stability of the Edo period allowed pilgrimages to combine faith and leisure, enhancing oshi influence. However, groups like the Kumano Oshi declined alongside {{ill|Kumano faith|ja|熊野信仰}}.
In Izumo, oshi structures laid the foundation for the {{ill|Izumo Taisha Faith|ja|出雲大社教}}, creating facilities such as "tanjo" (mission centers) to support local preaching.<ref>'Izumo Taisha Teacher Training Manual,' Izumo Taisha Headquarters, 1989, p. 319</ref>
With the establishment of the {{ill|Meiji government|ja|明治政府}}'s {{ill|Shinto|ja|神祇}} system, oshi rapidly declined. In 1869, oshi were barred from conducting {{ill|Shinto funerals|ja|神葬祭}}, and by 1871, the profession was abolished. Most oshi transitioned into farming or running inns. Some attempted to continue religious activities, forming organizations such as {{ill|Fusōkyō|ja|扶桑教}}.<ref>Takahiko Takano, "The Court and Religion in Early Modern Japan," Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2014, ISBN 978-4-642-03461-6, pp. 345-348.</ref>
=== Fuji Oshi === In the {{ill|Tenshōjingo Conflict|ja|天正壬午の乱}}, figures like Watanabe Shūgokuyū played significant roles. Notable Fuji Oshi residences, including {{ill|Old Togawa Residence|ja|旧外川家住宅}} and {{ill|Kosano Residence|ja|小佐野家住宅}}, are designated as {{ill|World Heritage Sites|ja|世界遺産}} and {{ill|Important Cultural Properties of Japan|ja|重要文化財}}.<ref>{{Cite web|title=World Cultural Heritage: Mt. Fuji Components|url=https://www.yamanashi-kankou.jp/heritage/fujisanwatcher/jp/world_heritage/index.html|publisher=Yamanashi Tourism Organization|access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Old Togawa Residence|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/185831|website=National Cultural Heritage Database|access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref>
== Cultural Depictions == * "Oshi Yagorō: Journey to Ise" by {{ill|Saijo Naka|ja|西條奈加}}, ISBN 439634015X * "{{ill|Sakuya-hime|ja|咲夜姫}}" by {{ill|Yamaguchi Kaito|ja|山口歌糸}}, ISBN 4783880204
== References == <references />
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Hagaki|ja|羽書}} * {{ill|Okage-mairi|ja|お蔭参り}} * {{ill|Shukubō|ja|宿坊}} * {{ill|Accommodation facilities|ja|宿泊施設}} * {{ill|Ise Calendar|ja|伊勢暦}} * [http://www.nikko-annai.com/ Dōshabiki] - Edo-period guides for worshippers at {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|ja|日光東照宮}}

=== Fuji Oshi === In the {{ill|Tenshōjingo Conflict|ja|天正壬午の乱}}, figures like Watanabe Shūgokuyū played significant roles. Notable Fuji Oshi residences, including {{ill|Old Togawa Residence|ja|旧外川家住宅}} and {{ill|Kosano Residence|ja|小佐野家住宅}}, are designated as {{ill|World Heritage Sites|ja|世界遺産}} and {{ill|Important Cultural Properties of Japan|ja|重要文化財}}.<ref>{{Cite web|title=World Cultural Heritage: Mt. Fuji Components|url=https://www.yamanashi-kankou.jp/heritage/fujisanwatcher/jp/world_heritage/index.html|publisher=Yamanashi Tourism Organization|access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Old Togawa Residence|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/185831|website=National Cultural Heritage Database|access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref>
