revision.comment: Copying from [[Category:Completion tier 1]] to [[Category:qq]][[sn:{{subst:PAGENAME}}]][[Category:qq]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
{{Otheruses|Shrine and temple guides|Music teachers for the emperor or imperial family|{{ill|御師 (音楽)|ja|御師 (音楽)}}}} [[File:Oshi 20190826a.jpg|thumb|Fuji-ko Guide (御師), photographed August 26, 2019]] '''Oshi''' (御師) refers to shrine priests associated with specific {{ill|shrines and temples|ja|社寺}}, who perform {{ill|prayers|ja|祈祷}}, guide worshippers, and assist pilgrims with visits and accommodations. In the case of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}, it was traditionally pronounced as "onshi." These guides often formed concentrated communities along highways, known as {{ill|Oshi towns|ja|御師町}}.
== Overview == Originally an abbreviation for "御祈祷師" (prayer guides), the term "oshi" emerged during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, initially referring to {{ill|temple monks|ja|社僧}}. Later, it extended to include shrine priests who facilitated pilgrimages.
== History == During the Heian period, notable examples of oshi included those from {{ill|Iwashimizu Hachiman Shrine|ja|石清水八幡宮}}, {{ill|Kamo Shrine|ja|賀茂神社}}, and {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|ja|日吉大社}}, with the {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} "Kumano Oshi" being particularly famous.<ref name="ushiyama">Yoshiyuki Ushiyama, "Faith and Culture in Medieval Ōhara," Shinshu University Bulletin of Education, Vol. 79 (1993), {{hdl|10091/1626}}</ref> The Kumano Oshi organized prayers, accommodations, and guided noble pilgrims during the late Heian period's {{ill|Kumano pilgrimage|ja|熊野詣}}. Relationships between oshi and pilgrims evolved into stable "master-disciple" ties, known as {{ill|師檀関係|ja|師檀関係}}, encompassing even commoners by the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}.
Between the Kamakura and {{ill|Muromachi periods|ja|室町時代}}, other prominent oshi groups emerged, such as those from {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}, {{ill|Fuji-ko|ja|富士講}}, {{ill|Matsuo Shrine|ja|松尾大社}}, {{ill|Mishima Taisha|ja|三嶋大社}}, and {{ill|Hakusan Shrine|ja|白山}}. Notably, records like the {{ill|Azuma Kagami|ja|吾妻鏡}} document oshi from {{ill|Izumo Taisha|ja|出雲大社}} serving {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝}}.<ref>'Izumo Taisha Teacher Training Manual,' Izumo Taisha Headquarters, 1989, p. 319</ref>
During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, oshi played an essential role as intermediaries between commoners and shrine priests. Pilgrimages like {{ill|Okage-mairi|lt=Ise pilgrimage|ja|伊勢参り}} and those to {{ill|Mt. Fuji|ja|富士山}} became widely accessible. Oshi organized {{ill|Kō (lecture)|ja|講|Kō (lecture)=Kō (lecture)|lt=kō (pilgrimage associations)}} and hosted pilgrims in their {{ill|Shukubo|ja|宿坊|Shukubo=Shukubo|lt=shukubō (pilgrim lodgings)}}. The economic stability of the Edo period allowed pilgrimages to combine faith and leisure, enhancing oshi influence. However, groups like the Kumano Oshi declined alongside {{ill|Kumano faith|ja|熊野信仰}}.
In Izumo, oshi structures laid the foundation for the {{ill|Izumo Taisha Faith|ja|出雲大社教}}, creating facilities such as "tanjo" (mission centers) to support local preaching.<ref>'Izumo Taisha Teacher Training Manual,' Izumo Taisha Headquarters, 1989, p. 319</ref>
With the establishment of the {{ill|Meiji government|ja|明治政府}}'s {{ill|Shinto|ja|神祇}} system, oshi rapidly declined. In 1869, oshi were barred from conducting {{ill|Shinto funerals|ja|神葬祭}}, and by 1871, the profession was abolished. Most oshi transitioned into farming or running inns. Some attempted to continue religious activities, forming organizations such as {{ill|Fusōkyō|ja|扶桑教}}.<ref>Takahiko Takano, "The Court and Religion in Early Modern Japan," Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2014, ISBN 978-4-642-03461-6, pp. 345-348.</ref>
=== Fuji Oshi === In the {{ill|Tenshōjingo Conflict|ja|天正壬午の乱}}, figures like Watanabe Shūgokuyū played significant roles. Notable Fuji Oshi residences, including {{ill|Old Togawa Residence|ja|旧外川家住宅}} and {{ill|Kosano Residence|ja|小佐野家住宅}}, are designated as {{ill|World Heritage Sites|ja|世界遺産}} and {{ill|Important Cultural Properties of Japan|ja|重要文化財}}.<ref>{{Cite web|title=World Cultural Heritage: Mt. Fuji Components|url=https://www.yamanashi-kankou.jp/heritage/fujisanwatcher/jp/world_heritage/index.html|publisher=Yamanashi Tourism Organization|access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Old Togawa Residence|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/185831|website=National Cultural Heritage Database|access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref>
== Cultural Depictions == * "Oshi Yagorō: Journey to Ise" by {{ill|Saijo Naka|ja|西條奈加}}, ISBN 439634015X * "{{ill|Sakuya-hime|ja|咲夜姫}}" by {{ill|Yamaguchi Kaito|ja|山口歌糸}}, ISBN 4783880204
== References == <references />
== See Also == * {{ill|Hagaki|ja|羽書}} * {{ill|Okage-mairi|ja|お蔭参り}} * {{ill|Shukubō|ja|宿坊}} * {{ill|Accommodation facilities|ja|宿泊施設}} * {{ill|Ise Calendar|ja|伊勢暦}} * [http://www.nikko-annai.com/ Dōshabiki] - Edo-period guides for worshippers at {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|ja|日光東照宮}}

{{Otheruses|Shrine and temple guides|Music teachers for the emperor or imperial family|{{ill|御師 (音楽)|ja|御師 (音楽)}}}} [[File:Oshi 20190826a.jpg|thumb|Fuji-ko Guide (御師), photographed August 26, 2019]] '''Oshi''' (御師) refers to shrine priests associated with specific {{ill|shrines and temples|ja|社寺}}, who perform {{ill|prayers|ja|祈祷}}, guide worshippers, and assist pilgrims with visits and accommodations. In the case of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}, it was traditionally pronounced as "onshi." These guides often formed concentrated communities along highways, known as {{ill|Oshi towns|ja|御師町}}.
== Overview == Originally an abbreviation for "御祈祷師" (prayer guides), the term "oshi" emerged during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, initially referring to {{ill|temple monks|ja|社僧}}. Later, it extended to include shrine priests who facilitated pilgrimages.
== History == During the Heian period, notable examples of oshi included those from {{ill|Iwashimizu Hachiman Shrine|ja|石清水八幡宮}}, {{ill|Kamo Shrine|ja|賀茂神社}}, and {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|ja|日吉大社}}, with the {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} "Kumano Oshi" being particularly famous.<ref name="ushiyama">Yoshiyuki Ushiyama, "Faith and Culture in Medieval Ōhara," Shinshu University Bulletin of Education, Vol. 79 (1993), {{hdl|10091/1626}}</ref> The Kumano Oshi organized prayers, accommodations, and guided noble pilgrims during the late Heian period's {{ill|Kumano pilgrimage|ja|熊野詣}}. Relationships between oshi and pilgrims evolved into stable "master-disciple" ties, known as {{ill|師檀関係|ja|師檀関係}}, encompassing even commoners by the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}.
Between the Kamakura and {{ill|Muromachi periods|ja|室町時代}}, other prominent oshi groups emerged, such as those from {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}, {{ill|Fuji-ko|ja|富士講}}, {{ill|Matsuo Shrine|ja|松尾大社}}, {{ill|Mishima Taisha|ja|三嶋大社}}, and {{ill|Hakusan Shrine|ja|白山}}. Notably, records like the {{ill|Azuma Kagami|ja|吾妻鏡}} document oshi from {{ill|Izumo Taisha|ja|出雲大社}} serving {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝}}.<ref>'Izumo Taisha Teacher Training Manual,' Izumo Taisha Headquarters, 1989, p. 319</ref>
During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, oshi played an essential role as intermediaries between commoners and shrine priests. Pilgrimages like {{ill|Okage-mairi|lt=Ise pilgrimage|ja|伊勢参り}} and those to {{ill|Mt. Fuji|ja|富士山}} became widely accessible. Oshi organized {{ill|Kō (lecture)|ja|講|Kō (lecture)=Kō (lecture)|lt=kō (pilgrimage associations)}} and hosted pilgrims in their {{ill|Shukubo|ja|宿坊|Shukubo=Shukubo|lt=shukubō (pilgrim lodgings)}}. The economic stability of the Edo period allowed pilgrimages to combine faith and leisure, enhancing oshi influence. However, groups like the Kumano Oshi declined alongside {{ill|Kumano faith|ja|熊野信仰}}.
In Izumo, oshi structures laid the foundation for the {{ill|Izumo Taisha Faith|ja|出雲大社教}}, creating facilities such as "tanjo" (mission centers) to support local preaching.<ref>'Izumo Taisha Teacher Training Manual,' Izumo Taisha Headquarters, 1989, p. 319</ref>
With the establishment of the {{ill|Meiji government|ja|明治政府}}'s {{ill|Shinto|ja|神祇}} system, oshi rapidly declined. In 1869, oshi were barred from conducting {{ill|Shinto funerals|ja|神葬祭}}, and by 1871, the profession was abolished. Most oshi transitioned into farming or running inns. Some attempted to continue religious activities, forming organizations such as {{ill|Fusōkyō|ja|扶桑教}}.<ref>Takahiko Takano, "The Court and Religion in Early Modern Japan," Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2014, ISBN 978-4-642-03461-6, pp. 345-348.</ref>
=== Fuji Oshi === In the {{ill|Tenshōjingo Conflict|ja|天正壬午の乱}}, figures like Watanabe Shūgokuyū played significant roles. Notable Fuji Oshi residences, including {{ill|Old Togawa Residence|ja|旧外川家住宅}} and {{ill|Kosano Residence|ja|小佐野家住宅}}, are designated as {{ill|World Heritage Sites|ja|世界遺産}} and {{ill|Important Cultural Properties of Japan|ja|重要文化財}}.<ref>{{Cite web|title=World Cultural Heritage: Mt. Fuji Components|url=https://www.yamanashi-kankou.jp/heritage/fujisanwatcher/jp/world_heritage/index.html|publisher=Yamanashi Tourism Organization|access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Old Togawa Residence|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/185831|website=National Cultural Heritage Database|access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref>
== Cultural Depictions == * "Oshi Yagorō: Journey to Ise" by {{ill|Saijo Naka|ja|西條奈加}}, ISBN 439634015X * "{{ill|Sakuya-hime|ja|咲夜姫}}" by {{ill|Yamaguchi Kaito|ja|山口歌糸}}, ISBN 4783880204

{{Otheruses|Shrine and temple guides|Music teachers for the emperor or imperial family|{{ill|御師 (音楽)|ja|御師 (音楽)}}}} [[File:Oshi 20190826a.jpg|thumb|Fuji-ko Guide (御師), photographed August 26, 2019]] '''Oshi''' (御師) refers to shrine priests associated with specific {{ill|shrines and temples|ja|社寺}}, who perform {{ill|prayers|ja|祈祷}}, guide worshippers, and assist pilgrims with visits and accommodations. In the case of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}, it was traditionally pronounced as "onshi." These guides often formed concentrated communities along highways, known as {{ill|Oshi towns|ja|御師町}}.
== Overview == Originally an abbreviation for "御祈祷師" (prayer guides), the term "oshi" emerged during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, initially referring to {{ill|temple monks|ja|社僧}}. Later, it extended to include shrine priests who facilitated pilgrimages.
== History == During the Heian period, notable examples of oshi included those from {{ill|Iwashimizu Hachiman Shrine|ja|石清水八幡宮}}, {{ill|Kamo Shrine|ja|賀茂神社}}, and {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|ja|日吉大社}}, with the {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} "Kumano Oshi" being particularly famous.<ref name="ushiyama">Yoshiyuki Ushiyama, "Faith and Culture in Medieval Ōhara," Shinshu University Bulletin of Education, Vol. 79 (1993), {{hdl|10091/1626}}</ref> The Kumano Oshi organized prayers, accommodations, and guided noble pilgrims during the late Heian period's {{ill|Kumano pilgrimage|ja|熊野詣}}. Relationships between oshi and pilgrims evolved into stable "master-disciple" ties, known as {{ill|師檀関係|ja|師檀関係}}, encompassing even commoners by the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}.
Between the Kamakura and {{ill|Muromachi periods|ja|室町時代}}, other prominent oshi groups emerged, such as those from {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}, {{ill|Fuji-ko|ja|富士講}}, {{ill|Matsuo Shrine|ja|松尾大社}}, {{ill|Mishima Taisha|ja|三嶋大社}}, and {{ill|Hakusan Shrine|ja|白山}}. Notably, records like the {{ill|Azuma Kagami|ja|吾妻鏡}} document oshi from {{ill|Izumo Taisha|ja|出雲大社}} serving {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝}}.<ref>'Izumo Taisha Teacher Training Manual,' Izumo Taisha Headquarters, 1989, p. 319</ref>
During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, oshi played an essential role as intermediaries between commoners and shrine priests. Pilgrimages like {{ill|Okage-mairi|lt=Ise pilgrimage|ja|伊勢参り}} and those to {{ill|Mt. Fuji|ja|富士山}} became widely accessible. Oshi organized {{ill|Kō (lecture)|ja|講|Kō (lecture)=Kō (lecture)|lt=kō (pilgrimage associations)}} and hosted pilgrims in their {{ill|Shukubo|ja|宿坊|Shukubo=Shukubo|lt=shukubō (pilgrim lodgings)}}. The economic stability of the Edo period allowed pilgrimages to combine faith and leisure, enhancing oshi influence. However, groups like the Kumano Oshi declined alongside {{ill|Kumano faith|ja|熊野信仰}}.
In Izumo, oshi structures laid the foundation for the {{ill|Izumo Taisha Faith|ja|出雲大社教}}, creating facilities such as "tanjo" (mission centers) to support local preaching.<ref>'Izumo Taisha Teacher Training Manual,' Izumo Taisha Headquarters, 1989, p. 319</ref>
With the establishment of the {{ill|Meiji government|ja|明治政府}}'s {{ill|Shinto|ja|神祇}} system, oshi rapidly declined. In 1869, oshi were barred from conducting {{ill|Shinto funerals|ja|神葬祭}}, and by 1871, the profession was abolished. Most oshi transitioned into farming or running inns. Some attempted to continue religious activities, forming organizations such as {{ill|Fusōkyō|ja|扶桑教}}.<ref>Takahiko Takano, "The Court and Religion in Early Modern Japan," Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2014, ISBN 978-4-642-03461-6, pp. 345-348.</ref>
=== Fuji Oshi === In the {{ill|Tenshōjingo Conflict|ja|天正壬午の乱}}, figures like Watanabe Shūgokuyū played significant roles. Notable Fuji Oshi residences, including {{ill|Old Togawa Residence|ja|旧外川家住宅}} and {{ill|Kosano Residence|ja|小佐野家住宅}}, are designated as {{ill|World Heritage Sites|ja|世界遺産}} and {{ill|Important Cultural Properties of Japan|ja|重要文化財}}.<ref>{{Cite web|title=World Cultural Heritage: Mt. Fuji Components|url=https://www.yamanashi-kankou.jp/heritage/fujisanwatcher/jp/world_heritage/index.html|publisher=Yamanashi Tourism Organization|access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Old Togawa Residence|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/185831|website=National Cultural Heritage Database|access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref>

{{Otheruses|Shrine and temple guides|Music teachers for the emperor or imperial family|{{ill|御師 (音楽)|ja|御師 (音楽)}}}} [[File:Oshi 20190826a.jpg|thumb|Fuji-ko Guide (御師), photographed August 26, 2019]] '''Oshi''' (御師) refers to shrine priests associated with specific {{ill|shrines and temples|ja|社寺}}, who perform {{ill|prayers|ja|祈祷}}, guide worshippers, and assist pilgrims with visits and accommodations. In the case of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}, it was traditionally pronounced as "onshi." These guides often formed concentrated communities along highways, known as {{ill|Oshi towns|ja|御師町}}.
== Overview == Originally an abbreviation for "御祈祷師" (prayer guides), the term "oshi" emerged during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, initially referring to {{ill|temple monks|ja|社僧}}. Later, it extended to include shrine priests who facilitated pilgrimages.
== History == During the Heian period, notable examples of oshi included those from {{ill|Iwashimizu Hachiman Shrine|ja|石清水八幡宮}}, {{ill|Kamo Shrine|ja|賀茂神社}}, and {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|ja|日吉大社}}, with the {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} "Kumano Oshi" being particularly famous.<ref name="ushiyama">Yoshiyuki Ushiyama, "Faith and Culture in Medieval Ōhara," Shinshu University Bulletin of Education, Vol. 79 (1993), {{hdl|10091/1626}}</ref> The Kumano Oshi organized prayers, accommodations, and guided noble pilgrims during the late Heian period's {{ill|Kumano pilgrimage|ja|熊野詣}}. Relationships between oshi and pilgrims evolved into stable "master-disciple" ties, known as {{ill|師檀関係|ja|師檀関係}}, encompassing even commoners by the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}.
Between the Kamakura and {{ill|Muromachi periods|ja|室町時代}}, other prominent oshi groups emerged, such as those from {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}, {{ill|Fuji-ko|ja|富士講}}, {{ill|Matsuo Shrine|ja|松尾大社}}, {{ill|Mishima Taisha|ja|三嶋大社}}, and {{ill|Hakusan Shrine|ja|白山}}. Notably, records like the {{ill|Azuma Kagami|ja|吾妻鏡}} document oshi from {{ill|Izumo Taisha|ja|出雲大社}} serving {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝}}.<ref>'Izumo Taisha Teacher Training Manual,' Izumo Taisha Headquarters, 1989, p. 319</ref>
During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, oshi played an essential role as intermediaries between commoners and shrine priests. Pilgrimages like {{ill|Okage-mairi|lt=Ise pilgrimage|ja|伊勢参り}} and those to {{ill|Mt. Fuji|ja|富士山}} became widely accessible. Oshi organized {{ill|Kō (lecture)|ja|講|Kō (lecture)=Kō (lecture)|lt=kō (pilgrimage associations)}} and hosted pilgrims in their {{ill|Shukubo|ja|宿坊|Shukubo=Shukubo|lt=shukubō (pilgrim lodgings)}}. The economic stability of the Edo period allowed pilgrimages to combine faith and leisure, enhancing oshi influence. However, groups like the Kumano Oshi declined alongside {{ill|Kumano faith|ja|熊野信仰}}.
In Izumo, oshi structures laid the foundation for the {{ill|Izumo Taisha Faith|ja|出雲大社教}}, creating facilities such as "tanjo" (mission centers) to support local preaching.<ref>'Izumo Taisha Teacher Training Manual,' Izumo Taisha Headquarters, 1989, p. 319</ref>
With the establishment of the {{ill|Meiji government|ja|明治政府}}'s {{ill|Shinto|ja|神祇}} system, oshi rapidly declined. In 1869, oshi were barred from conducting {{ill|Shinto funerals|ja|神葬祭}}, and by 1871, the profession was abolished. Most oshi transitioned into farming or running inns. Some attempted to continue religious activities, forming organizations such as {{ill|Fusōkyō|ja|扶桑教}}.<ref>Takahiko Takano, "The Court and Religion in Early Modern Japan," Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2014, ISBN 978-4-642-03461-6, pp. 345-348.</ref>
=== Fuji Oshi === In the {{ill|Tenshōjingo Conflict|ja|天正壬午の乱}}, figures like Watanabe Shūgokuyū played significant roles. Notable Fuji Oshi residences, including {{ill|Old Togawa Residence|ja|旧外川家住宅}} and {{ill|Kosano Residence|ja|小佐野家住宅}}, are designated as {{ill|World Heritage Sites|ja|世界遺産}} and {{ill|Important Cultural Properties of Japan|ja|重要文化財}}.<ref>{{Cite web|title=World Cultural Heritage: Mt. Fuji Components|url=https://www.yamanashi-kankou.jp/heritage/fujisanwatcher/jp/world_heritage/index.html|publisher=Yamanashi Tourism Organization|access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Old Togawa Residence|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/185831|website=National Cultural Heritage Database|access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref>
== Cultural Depictions == * "Oshi Yagorō: Journey to Ise" by {{ill|Saijo Naka|ja|西條奈加}}, ISBN 439634015X * "{{ill|Sakuya-hime|ja|咲夜姫}}" by {{ill|Yamaguchi Kaito|ja|山口歌糸}}, ISBN 4783880204

revision.text:
{{Otheruses|Shrine and temple guides|Music teachers for the emperor or imperial family|{{ill|御師 (音楽)|ja|御師 (音楽)}}}} [[File:Oshi 20190826a.jpg|thumb|Fuji-ko Guide (御師), photographed August 26, 2019]] '''Oshi''' (御師) refers to shrine priests associated with specific {{ill|shrines and temples|ja|社寺}}, who perform {{ill|prayers|ja|祈祷}}, guide worshippers, and assist pilgrims with visits and accommodations. In the case of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}, it was traditionally pronounced as "onshi." These guides often formed concentrated communities along highways, known as {{ill|Oshi towns|ja|御師町}}.
== Overview == Originally an abbreviation for "御祈祷師" (prayer guides), the term "oshi" emerged during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, initially referring to {{ill|temple monks|ja|社僧}}. Later, it extended to include shrine priests who facilitated pilgrimages.
== History == During the Heian period, notable examples of oshi included those from {{ill|Iwashimizu Hachiman Shrine|ja|石清水八幡宮}}, {{ill|Kamo Shrine|ja|賀茂神社}}, and {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|ja|日吉大社}}, with the {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} "Kumano Oshi" being particularly famous.<ref name="ushiyama">Yoshiyuki Ushiyama, "Faith and Culture in Medieval Ōhara," Shinshu University Bulletin of Education, Vol. 79 (1993), {{hdl|10091/1626}}</ref> The Kumano Oshi organized prayers, accommodations, and guided noble pilgrims during the late Heian period's {{ill|Kumano pilgrimage|ja|熊野詣}}. Relationships between oshi and pilgrims evolved into stable "master-disciple" ties, known as {{ill|師檀関係|ja|師檀関係}}, encompassing even commoners by the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}.
Between the Kamakura and {{ill|Muromachi periods|ja|室町時代}}, other prominent oshi groups emerged, such as those from {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}, {{ill|Fuji-ko|ja|富士講}}, {{ill|Matsuo Shrine|ja|松尾大社}}, {{ill|Mishima Taisha|ja|三嶋大社}}, and {{ill|Hakusan Shrine|ja|白山}}. Notably, records like the {{ill|Azuma Kagami|ja|吾妻鏡}} document oshi from {{ill|Izumo Taisha|ja|出雲大社}} serving {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝}}.<ref>'Izumo Taisha Teacher Training Manual,' Izumo Taisha Headquarters, 1989, p. 319</ref>
During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, oshi played an essential role as intermediaries between commoners and shrine priests. Pilgrimages like {{ill|Okage-mairi|lt=Ise pilgrimage|ja|伊勢参り}} and those to {{ill|Mt. Fuji|ja|富士山}} became widely accessible. Oshi organized {{ill|Kō (lecture)|ja|講|Kō (lecture)=Kō (lecture)|lt=kō (pilgrimage associations)}} and hosted pilgrims in their {{ill|Shukubo|ja|宿坊|Shukubo=Shukubo|lt=shukubō (pilgrim lodgings)}}. The economic stability of the Edo period allowed pilgrimages to combine faith and leisure, enhancing oshi influence. However, groups like the Kumano Oshi declined alongside {{ill|Kumano faith|ja|熊野信仰}}.
In Izumo, oshi structures laid the foundation for the {{ill|Izumo Taisha Faith|ja|出雲大社教}}, creating facilities such as "tanjo" (mission centers) to support local preaching.<ref>'Izumo Taisha Teacher Training Manual,' Izumo Taisha Headquarters, 1989, p. 319</ref>
With the establishment of the {{ill|Meiji government|ja|明治政府}}'s {{ill|Shinto|ja|神祇}} system, oshi rapidly declined. In 1869, oshi were barred from conducting {{ill|Shinto funerals|ja|神葬祭}}, and by 1871, the profession was abolished. Most oshi transitioned into farming or running inns. Some attempted to continue religious activities, forming organizations such as {{ill|Fusōkyō|ja|扶桑教}}.<ref>Takahiko Takano, "The Court and Religion in Early Modern Japan," Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2014, ISBN 978-4-642-03461-6, pp. 345-348.</ref>
=== Fuji Oshi === In the {{ill|Tenshōjingo Conflict|ja|天正壬午の乱}}, figures like Watanabe Shūgokuyū played significant roles. Notable Fuji Oshi residences, including {{ill|Old Togawa Residence|ja|旧外川家住宅}} and {{ill|Kosano Residence|ja|小佐野家住宅}}, are designated as {{ill|World Heritage Sites|ja|世界遺産}} and {{ill|Important Cultural Properties of Japan|ja|重要文化財}}.<ref>{{Cite web|title=World Cultural Heritage: Mt. Fuji Components|url=https://www.yamanashi-kankou.jp/heritage/fujisanwatcher/jp/world_heritage/index.html|publisher=Yamanashi Tourism Organization|access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Old Togawa Residence|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/185831|website=National Cultural Heritage Database|access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref>
== Cultural Depictions == * "Oshi Yagorō: Journey to Ise" by {{ill|Saijo Naka|ja|西條奈加}}, ISBN 439634015X * "{{ill|Sakuya-hime|ja|咲夜姫}}" by {{ill|Yamaguchi Kaito|ja|山口歌糸}}, ISBN 4783880204
== References == <references />== See Also == * {{ill|Hagaki|ja|羽書}} * {{ill|Okage-mairi|ja|お蔭参り}} * {{ill|Shukubō|ja|宿坊}} * {{ill|Accommodation facilities|ja|宿泊施設}} * {{ill|Ise Calendar|ja|伊勢暦}} * [http://www.nikko-annai.com/ Dōshabiki] - Edo-period guides for worshippers at {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|ja|日光東照宮}}

{{Otheruses|Shrine and temple guides|Music teachers for the emperor or imperial family|{{ill|御師 (音楽)|ja|御師 (音楽)}}}} [[File:Oshi 20190826a.jpg|thumb|Fuji-ko Guide (御師), photographed August 26, 2019]] '''Oshi''' (御師) refers to shrine priests associated with specific {{ill|shrines and temples|ja|社寺}}, who perform {{ill|prayers|ja|祈祷}}, guide worshippers, and assist pilgrims with visits and accommodations. In the case of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}, it was traditionally pronounced as "onshi." These guides often formed concentrated communities along highways, known as {{ill|Oshi towns|ja|御師町}}.
== Overview == Originally an abbreviation for "御祈祷師" (prayer guides), the term "oshi" emerged during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, initially referring to {{ill|temple monks|ja|社僧}}. Later, it extended to include shrine priests who facilitated pilgrimages.
== History == During the Heian period, notable examples of oshi included those from {{ill|Iwashimizu Hachiman Shrine|ja|石清水八幡宮}}, {{ill|Kamo Shrine|ja|賀茂神社}}, and {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|ja|日吉大社}}, with the {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} "Kumano Oshi" being particularly famous.<ref name="ushiyama">Yoshiyuki Ushiyama, "Faith and Culture in Medieval Ōhara," Shinshu University Bulletin of Education, Vol. 79 (1993), {{hdl|10091/1626}}</ref> The Kumano Oshi organized prayers, accommodations, and guided noble pilgrims during the late Heian period's {{ill|Kumano pilgrimage|ja|熊野詣}}. Relationships between oshi and pilgrims evolved into stable "master-disciple" ties, known as {{ill|師檀関係|ja|師檀関係}}, encompassing even commoners by the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}.
Between the Kamakura and {{ill|Muromachi periods|ja|室町時代}}, other prominent oshi groups emerged, such as those from {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}, {{ill|Fuji-ko|ja|富士講}}, {{ill|Matsuo Shrine|ja|松尾大社}}, {{ill|Mishima Taisha|ja|三嶋大社}}, and {{ill|Hakusan Shrine|ja|白山}}. Notably, records like the {{ill|Azuma Kagami|ja|吾妻鏡}} document oshi from {{ill|Izumo Taisha|ja|出雲大社}} serving {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝}}.<ref>'Izumo Taisha Teacher Training Manual,' Izumo Taisha Headquarters, 1989, p. 319</ref>
During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, oshi played an essential role as intermediaries between commoners and shrine priests. Pilgrimages like {{ill|Okage-mairi|lt=Ise pilgrimage|ja|伊勢参り}} and those to {{ill|Mt. Fuji|ja|富士山}} became widely accessible. Oshi organized {{ill|Kō (lecture)|ja|講|Kō (lecture)=Kō (lecture)|lt=kō (pilgrimage associations)}} and hosted pilgrims in their {{ill|Shukubo|ja|宿坊|Shukubo=Shukubo|lt=shukubō (pilgrim lodgings)}}. The economic stability of the Edo period allowed pilgrimages to combine faith and leisure, enhancing oshi influence. However, groups like the Kumano Oshi declined alongside {{ill|Kumano faith|ja|熊野信仰}}.
In Izumo, oshi structures laid the foundation for the {{ill|Izumo Taisha Faith|ja|出雲大社教}}, creating facilities such as "tanjo" (mission centers) to support local preaching.<ref>'Izumo Taisha Teacher Training Manual,' Izumo Taisha Headquarters, 1989, p. 319</ref>
With the establishment of the {{ill|Meiji government|ja|明治政府}}'s {{ill|Shinto|ja|神祇}} system, oshi rapidly declined. In 1869, oshi were barred from conducting {{ill|Shinto funerals|ja|神葬祭}}, and by 1871, the profession was abolished. Most oshi transitioned into farming or running inns. Some attempted to continue religious activities, forming organizations such as {{ill|Fusōkyō|ja|扶桑教}}.<ref>Takahiko Takano, "The Court and Religion in Early Modern Japan," Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2014, ISBN 978-4-642-03461-6, pp. 345-348.</ref>
=== Fuji Oshi === In the {{ill|Tenshōjingo Conflict|ja|天正壬午の乱}}, figures like Watanabe Shūgokuyū played significant roles. Notable Fuji Oshi residences, including {{ill|Old Togawa Residence|ja|旧外川家住宅}} and {{ill|Kosano Residence|ja|小佐野家住宅}}, are designated as {{ill|World Heritage Sites|ja|世界遺産}} and {{ill|Important Cultural Properties of Japan|ja|重要文化財}}.<ref>{{Cite web|title=World Cultural Heritage: Mt. Fuji Components|url=https://www.yamanashi-kankou.jp/heritage/fujisanwatcher/jp/world_heritage/index.html|publisher=Yamanashi Tourism Organization|access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Old Togawa Residence|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/185831|website=National Cultural Heritage Database|access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref>
== Cultural Depictions == * "Oshi Yagorō: Journey to Ise" by {{ill|Saijo Naka|ja|西條奈加}}, ISBN 439634015X * "{{ill|Sakuya-hime|ja|咲夜姫}}" by {{ill|Yamaguchi Kaito|ja|山口歌糸}}, ISBN 4783880204
== References == <references />== See Also == * {{ill|Hagaki|ja|羽書}} * {{ill|Okage-mairi|ja|お蔭参り}} * {{ill|Shukubō|ja|宿坊}} * {{ill|Accommodation facilities|ja|宿泊施設}} * {{ill|Ise Calendar|ja|伊勢暦}} * [http://www.nikko-annai.com/ Dōshabiki] - Edo-period guides for worshippers at {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|ja|日光東照宮}}

revision.text:
{{Otheruses|Shrine and temple guides|Music teachers for the emperor or imperial family|{{ill|御師 (音楽)|ja|御師 (音楽)}}}} [[File:Oshi 20190826a.jpg|thumb|Fuji-ko Guide (御師), photographed August 26, 2019]] '''Oshi''' (御師) refers to shrine priests associated with specific {{ill|shrines and temples|ja|社寺}}, who perform {{ill|prayers|ja|祈祷}}, guide worshippers, and assist pilgrims with visits and accommodations. In the case of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}, it was traditionally pronounced as "onshi." These guides often formed concentrated communities along highways, known as {{ill|Oshi towns|ja|御師町}}.
== Overview == Originally an abbreviation for "御祈祷師" (prayer guides), the term "oshi" emerged during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, initially referring to {{ill|temple monks|ja|社僧}}. Later, it extended to include shrine priests who facilitated pilgrimages.
== History == During the Heian period, notable examples of oshi included those from {{ill|Iwashimizu Hachiman Shrine|ja|石清水八幡宮}}, {{ill|Kamo Shrine|ja|賀茂神社}}, and {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|ja|日吉大社}}, with the {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} "Kumano Oshi" being particularly famous.<ref name="ushiyama">Yoshiyuki Ushiyama, "Faith and Culture in Medieval Ōhara," Shinshu University Bulletin of Education, Vol. 79 (1993), {{hdl|10091/1626}}</ref> The Kumano Oshi organized prayers, accommodations, and guided noble pilgrims during the late Heian period's {{ill|Kumano pilgrimage|ja|熊野詣}}. Relationships between oshi and pilgrims evolved into stable "master-disciple" ties, known as {{ill|師檀関係|ja|師檀関係}}, encompassing even commoners by the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}.
Between the Kamakura and {{ill|Muromachi periods|ja|室町時代}}, other prominent oshi groups emerged, such as those from {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}, {{ill|Fuji-ko|ja|富士講}}, {{ill|Matsuo Shrine|ja|松尾大社}}, {{ill|Mishima Taisha|ja|三嶋大社}}, and {{ill|Hakusan Shrine|ja|白山}}. Notably, records like the {{ill|Azuma Kagami|ja|吾妻鏡}} document oshi from {{ill|Izumo Taisha|ja|出雲大社}} serving {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝}}.<ref>'Izumo Taisha Teacher Training Manual,' Izumo Taisha Headquarters, 1989, p. 319</ref>
During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, oshi played an essential role as intermediaries between commoners and shrine priests. Pilgrimages like {{ill|Okage-mairi|lt=Ise pilgrimage|ja|伊勢参り}} and those to {{ill|Mt. Fuji|ja|富士山}} became widely accessible. Oshi organized {{ill|Kō (lecture)|ja|講|Kō (lecture)=Kō (lecture)|lt=kō (pilgrimage associations)}} and hosted pilgrims in their {{ill|Shukubo|ja|宿坊|Shukubo=Shukubo|lt=shukubō (pilgrim lodgings)}}. The economic stability of the Edo period allowed pilgrimages to combine faith and leisure, enhancing oshi influence. However, groups like the Kumano Oshi declined alongside {{ill|Kumano faith|ja|熊野信仰}}.
In Izumo, oshi structures laid the foundation for the {{ill|Izumo Taisha Faith|ja|出雲大社教}}, creating facilities such as "tanjo" (mission centers) to support local preaching.<ref>'Izumo Taisha Teacher Training Manual,' Izumo Taisha Headquarters, 1989, p. 319</ref>
With the establishment of the {{ill|Meiji government|ja|明治政府}}'s {{ill|Shinto|ja|神祇}} system, oshi rapidly declined. In 1869, oshi were barred from conducting {{ill|Shinto funerals|ja|神葬祭}}, and by 1871, the profession was abolished. Most oshi transitioned into farming or running inns. Some attempted to continue religious activities, forming organizations such as {{ill|Fusōkyō|ja|扶桑教}}.<ref>Takahiko Takano, "The Court and Religion in Early Modern Japan," Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2014, ISBN 978-4-642-03461-6, pp. 345-348.</ref>
=== Fuji Oshi === In the {{ill|Tenshōjingo Conflict|ja|天正壬午の乱}}, figures like Watanabe Shūgokuyū played significant roles. Notable Fuji Oshi residences, including {{ill|Old Togawa Residence|ja|旧外川家住宅}} and {{ill|Kosano Residence|ja|小佐野家住宅}}, are designated as {{ill|World Heritage Sites|ja|世界遺産}} and {{ill|Important Cultural Properties of Japan|ja|重要文化財}}.<ref>{{Cite web|title=World Cultural Heritage: Mt. Fuji Components|url=https://www.yamanashi-kankou.jp/heritage/fujisanwatcher/jp/world_heritage/index.html|publisher=Yamanashi Tourism Organization|access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Old Togawa Residence|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/185831|website=National Cultural Heritage Database|access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref>
== Cultural Depictions == * "Oshi Yagorō: Journey to Ise" by {{ill|Saijo Naka|ja|西條奈加}}, ISBN 439634015X * "{{ill|Sakuya-hime|ja|咲夜姫}}" by {{ill|Yamaguchi Kaito|ja|山口歌糸}}, ISBN 4783880204
== References == <references /> == See Also! == * {{ill|Hagaki|ja|羽書}} * {{ill|Okage-mairi|ja|お蔭参り}} * {{ill|Shukubō|ja|宿坊}} * {{ill|Accommodation facilities|ja|宿泊施設}} * {{ill|Ise Calendar|ja|伊勢暦}} * [http://www.nikko-annai.com/ Dōshabiki] - Edo-period guides for worshippers at {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|ja|日光東照宮}}

revision.text:
{{Otheruses|Shrine and temple guides|Music teachers for the emperor or imperial family|{{ill|御師 (音楽)|ja|御師 (音楽)}}}} [[File:Oshi 20190826a.jpg|thumb|Fuji-ko Guide (御師), photographed August 26, 2019]] '''Oshi''' (御師) refers to shrine priests associated with specific {{ill|shrines and temples|ja|社寺}}, who perform {{ill|prayers|ja|祈祷}}, guide worshippers, and assist pilgrims with visits and accommodations. In the case of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}, it was traditionally pronounced as "onshi." These guides often formed concentrated communities along highways, known as {{ill|Oshi towns|ja|御師町}}.
== Overview == Originally an abbreviation for "御祈祷師" (prayer guides), the term "oshi" emerged during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, initially referring to {{ill|temple monks|ja|社僧}}. Later, it extended to include shrine priests who facilitated pilgrimages.
== History == During the Heian period, notable examples of oshi included those from {{ill|Iwashimizu Hachiman Shrine|ja|石清水八幡宮}}, {{ill|Kamo Shrine|ja|賀茂神社}}, and {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|ja|日吉大社}}, with the {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} "Kumano Oshi" being particularly famous.<ref name="ushiyama">Yoshiyuki Ushiyama, "Faith and Culture in Medieval Ōhara," Shinshu University Bulletin of Education, Vol. 79 (1993), {{hdl|10091/1626}}</ref> The Kumano Oshi organized prayers, accommodations, and guided noble pilgrims during the late Heian period's {{ill|Kumano pilgrimage|ja|熊野詣}}. Relationships between oshi and pilgrims evolved into stable "master-disciple" ties, known as {{ill|師檀関係|ja|師檀関係}}, encompassing even commoners by the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}.
Between the Kamakura and {{ill|Muromachi periods|ja|室町時代}}, other prominent oshi groups emerged, such as those from {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}, {{ill|Fuji-ko|ja|富士講}}, {{ill|Matsuo Shrine|ja|松尾大社}}, {{ill|Mishima Taisha|ja|三嶋大社}}, and {{ill|Hakusan Shrine|ja|白山}}. Notably, records like the {{ill|Azuma Kagami|ja|吾妻鏡}} document oshi from {{ill|Izumo Taisha|ja|出雲大社}} serving {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝}}.<ref>'Izumo Taisha Teacher Training Manual,' Izumo Taisha Headquarters, 1989, p. 319</ref>
During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, oshi played an essential role as intermediaries between commoners and shrine priests. Pilgrimages like {{ill|Okage-mairi|lt=Ise pilgrimage|ja|伊勢参り}} and those to {{ill|Mt. Fuji|ja|富士山}} became widely accessible. Oshi organized {{ill|Kō (lecture)|ja|講|Kō (lecture)=Kō (lecture)|lt=kō (pilgrimage associations)}} and hosted pilgrims in their {{ill|Shukubo|ja|宿坊|Shukubo=Shukubo|lt=shukubō (pilgrim lodgings)}}. The economic stability of the Edo period allowed pilgrimages to combine faith and leisure, enhancing oshi influence. However, groups like the Kumano Oshi declined alongside {{ill|Kumano faith|ja|熊野信仰}}.
In Izumo, oshi structures laid the foundation for the {{ill|Izumo Taisha Faith|ja|出雲大社教}}, creating facilities such as "tanjo" (mission centers) to support local preaching.<ref>'Izumo Taisha Teacher Training Manual,' Izumo Taisha Headquarters, 1989, p. 319</ref>
With the establishment of the {{ill|Meiji government|ja|明治政府}}'s {{ill|Shinto|ja|神祇}} system, oshi rapidly declined. In 1869, oshi were barred from conducting {{ill|Shinto funerals|ja|神葬祭}}, and by 1871, the profession was abolished. Most oshi transitioned into farming or running inns. Some attempted to continue religious activities, forming organizations such as {{ill|Fusōkyō|ja|扶桑教}}.<ref>Takahiko Takano, "The Court and Religion in Early Modern Japan," Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2014, ISBN 978-4-642-03461-6, pp. 345-348.</ref>
=== Fuji Oshi === In the {{ill|Tenshōjingo Conflict|ja|天正壬午の乱}}, figures like Watanabe Shūgokuyū played significant roles. Notable Fuji Oshi residences, including {{ill|Old Togawa Residence|ja|旧外川家住宅}} and {{ill|Kosano Residence|ja|小佐野家住宅}}, are designated as {{ill|World Heritage Sites|ja|世界遺産}} and {{ill|Important Cultural Properties of Japan|ja|重要文化財}}.<ref>{{Cite web|title=World Cultural Heritage: Mt. Fuji Components|url=https://www.yamanashi-kankou.jp/heritage/fujisanwatcher/jp/world_heritage/index.html|publisher=Yamanashi Tourism Organization|access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Old Togawa Residence|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/185831|website=National Cultural Heritage Database|access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref>
== Cultural Depictions == * "Oshi Yagorō: Journey to Ise" by {{ill|Saijo Naka|ja|西條奈加}}, ISBN 439634015X * "{{ill|Sakuya-hime|ja|咲夜姫}}" by {{ill|Yamaguchi Kaito|ja|山口歌糸}}, ISBN 4783880204
== References == <references /> == See Also == * {{ill|Hagaki|ja|羽書}} * {{ill|Okage-mairi|ja|お蔭参り}} * {{ill|Shukubō|ja|宿坊}} * {{ill|Accommodation facilities|ja|宿泊施設}} * {{ill|Ise Calendar|ja|伊勢暦}} * [http://www.nikko-annai.com/ Dōshabiki] - Edo-period guides for worshippers at {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|ja|日光東照宮}}

{{Otheruses|Shrine and temple guides|Music teachers for the emperor or imperial family|{{ill|御師 (音楽)|ja|御師 (音楽)}}}} [[File:Oshi 20190826a.jpg|thumb|Fuji-ko Guide (御師), photographed August 26, 2019]] '''Oshi''' (御師) refers to shrine priests associated with specific {{ill|shrines and temples|ja|社寺}}, who perform {{ill|prayers|ja|祈祷}}, guide worshippers, and assist pilgrims with visits and accommodations. In the case of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}, it was traditionally pronounced as "onshi." These guides often formed concentrated communities along highways, known as {{ill|Oshi towns|ja|御師町}}.
== Overview == Originally an abbreviation for "御祈祷師" (prayer guides), the term "oshi" emerged during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, initially referring to {{ill|temple monks|ja|社僧}}. Later, it extended to include shrine priests who facilitated pilgrimages.
== History == During the Heian period, notable examples of oshi included those from {{ill|Iwashimizu Hachiman Shrine|ja|石清水八幡宮}}, {{ill|Kamo Shrine|ja|賀茂神社}}, and {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|ja|日吉大社}}, with the {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} "Kumano Oshi" being particularly famous.<ref name="ushiyama">Yoshiyuki Ushiyama, "Faith and Culture in Medieval Ōhara," Shinshu University Bulletin of Education, Vol. 79 (1993), {{hdl|10091/1626}}</ref> The Kumano Oshi organized prayers, accommodations, and guided noble pilgrims during the late Heian period's {{ill|Kumano pilgrimage|ja|熊野詣}}. Relationships between oshi and pilgrims evolved into stable "master-disciple" ties, known as {{ill|師檀関係|ja|師檀関係}}, encompassing even commoners by the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}.
Between the Kamakura and {{ill|Muromachi periods|ja|室町時代}}, other prominent oshi groups emerged, such as those from {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}, {{ill|Fuji-ko|ja|富士講}}, {{ill|Matsuo Shrine|ja|松尾大社}}, {{ill|Mishima Taisha|ja|三嶋大社}}, and {{ill|Hakusan Shrine|ja|白山}}. Notably, records like the {{ill|Azuma Kagami|ja|吾妻鏡}} document oshi from {{ill|Izumo Taisha|ja|出雲大社}} serving {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝}}.<ref>'Izumo Taisha Teacher Training Manual,' Izumo Taisha Headquarters, 1989, p. 319</ref>
During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, oshi played an essential role as intermediaries between commoners and shrine priests. Pilgrimages like {{ill|Okage-mairi|lt=Ise pilgrimage|ja|伊勢参り}} and those to {{ill|Mt. Fuji|ja|富士山}} became widely accessible. Oshi organized {{ill|Kō (lecture)|ja|講|Kō (lecture)=Kō (lecture)|lt=kō (pilgrimage associations)}} and hosted pilgrims in their {{ill|Shukubo|ja|宿坊|Shukubo=Shukubo|lt=shukubō (pilgrim lodgings)}}. The economic stability of the Edo period allowed pilgrimages to combine faith and leisure, enhancing oshi influence. However, groups like the Kumano Oshi declined alongside {{ill|Kumano faith|ja|熊野信仰}}.
In Izumo, oshi structures laid the foundation for the {{ill|Izumo Taisha Faith|ja|出雲大社教}}, creating facilities such as "tanjo" (mission centers) to support local preaching.<ref>'Izumo Taisha Teacher Training Manual,' Izumo Taisha Headquarters, 1989, p. 319</ref>
With the establishment of the {{ill|Meiji government|ja|明治政府}}'s {{ill|Shinto|ja|神祇}} system, oshi rapidly declined. In 1869, oshi were barred from conducting {{ill|Shinto funerals|ja|神葬祭}}, and by 1871, the profession was abolished. Most oshi transitioned into farming or running inns. Some attempted to continue religious activities, forming organizations such as {{ill|Fusōkyō|ja|扶桑教}}.<ref>Takahiko Takano, "The Court and Religion in Early Modern Japan," Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2014, ISBN 978-4-642-03461-6, pp. 345-348.</ref>
=== Fuji Oshi === In the {{ill|Tenshōjingo Conflict|ja|天正壬午の乱}}, figures like Watanabe Shūgokuyū played significant roles. Notable Fuji Oshi residences, including {{ill|Old Togawa Residence|ja|旧外川家住宅}} and {{ill|Kosano Residence|ja|小佐野家住宅}}, are designated as {{ill|World Heritage Sites|ja|世界遺産}} and {{ill|Important Cultural Properties of Japan|ja|重要文化財}}.<ref>{{Cite web|title=World Cultural Heritage: Mt. Fuji Components|url=https://www.yamanashi-kankou.jp/heritage/fujisanwatcher/jp/world_heritage/index.html|publisher=Yamanashi Tourism Organization|access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Old Togawa Residence|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/185831|website=National Cultural Heritage Database|access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref>
== Cultural Depictions == * "Oshi Yagorō: Journey to Ise" by {{ill|Saijo Naka|ja|西條奈加}}, ISBN 439634015X * "{{ill|Sakuya-hime|ja|咲夜姫}}" by {{ill|Yamaguchi Kaito|ja|山口歌糸}}, ISBN 4783880204

{{Otheruses|Shrine and temple guides|Music teachers for the emperor or imperial family|{{ill|御師 (音楽)|ja|御師 (音楽)}}}} [[File:Oshi 20190826a.jpg|thumb|Fuji-ko Guide (御師), photographed August 26, 2019]] '''Oshi''' (御師) refers to shrine priests associated with specific {{ill|shrines and temples|ja|社寺}}, who perform {{ill|prayers|ja|祈祷}}, guide worshippers, and assist pilgrims with visits and accommodations. In the case of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}, it was traditionally pronounced as "onshi." These guides often formed concentrated communities along highways, known as {{ill|Oshi towns|ja|御師町}}.
== Overview == Originally an abbreviation for "御祈祷師" (prayer guides), the term "oshi" emerged during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, initially referring to {{ill|temple monks|ja|社僧}}. Later, it extended to include shrine priests who facilitated pilgrimages.
== History == During the Heian period, notable examples of oshi included those from {{ill|Iwashimizu Hachiman Shrine|ja|石清水八幡宮}}, {{ill|Kamo Shrine|ja|賀茂神社}}, and {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|ja|日吉大社}}, with the {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} "Kumano Oshi" being particularly famous.<ref name="ushiyama">Yoshiyuki Ushiyama, "Faith and Culture in Medieval Ōhara," Shinshu University Bulletin of Education, Vol. 79 (1993), {{hdl|10091/1626}}</ref> The Kumano Oshi organized prayers, accommodations, and guided noble pilgrims during the late Heian period's {{ill|Kumano pilgrimage|ja|熊野詣}}. Relationships between oshi and pilgrims evolved into stable "master-disciple" ties, known as {{ill|師檀関係|ja|師檀関係}}, encompassing even commoners by the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}.
Between the Kamakura and {{ill|Muromachi periods|ja|室町時代}}, other prominent oshi groups emerged, such as those from {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}, {{ill|Fuji-ko|ja|富士講}}, {{ill|Matsuo Shrine|ja|松尾大社}}, {{ill|Mishima Taisha|ja|三嶋大社}}, and {{ill|Hakusan Shrine|ja|白山}}. Notably, records like the {{ill|Azuma Kagami|ja|吾妻鏡}} document oshi from {{ill|Izumo Taisha|ja|出雲大社}} serving {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝}}.<ref>'Izumo Taisha Teacher Training Manual,' Izumo Taisha Headquarters, 1989, p. 319</ref>
During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, oshi played an essential role as intermediaries between commoners and shrine priests. Pilgrimages like {{ill|Okage-mairi|lt=Ise pilgrimage|ja|伊勢参り}} and those to {{ill|Mt. Fuji|ja|富士山}} became widely accessible. Oshi organized {{ill|Kō (lecture)|ja|講|Kō (lecture)=Kō (lecture)|lt=kō (pilgrimage associations)}} and hosted pilgrims in their {{ill|Shukubo|ja|宿坊|Shukubo=Shukubo|lt=shukubō (pilgrim lodgings)}}. The economic stability of the Edo period allowed pilgrimages to combine faith and leisure, enhancing oshi influence. However, groups like the Kumano Oshi declined alongside {{ill|Kumano faith|ja|熊野信仰}}.
In Izumo, oshi structures laid the foundation for the {{ill|Izumo Taisha Faith|ja|出雲大社教}}, creating facilities such as "tanjo" (mission centers) to support local preaching.<ref>'Izumo Taisha Teacher Training Manual,' Izumo Taisha Headquarters, 1989, p. 319</ref>
With the establishment of the {{ill|Meiji government|ja|明治政府}}'s {{ill|Shinto|ja|神祇}} system, oshi rapidly declined. In 1869, oshi were barred from conducting {{ill|Shinto funerals|ja|神葬祭}}, and by 1871, the profession was abolished. Most oshi transitioned into farming or running inns. Some attempted to continue religious activities, forming organizations such as {{ill|Fusōkyō|ja|扶桑教}}.<ref>Takahiko Takano, "The Court and Religion in Early Modern Japan," Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2014, ISBN 978-4-642-03461-6, pp. 345-348.</ref>
=== Fuji Oshi === In the {{ill|Tenshōjingo Conflict|ja|天正壬午の乱}}, figures like Watanabe Shūgokuyū played significant roles. Notable Fuji Oshi residences, including {{ill|Old Togawa Residence|ja|旧外川家住宅}} and {{ill|Kosano Residence|ja|小佐野家住宅}}, are designated as {{ill|World Heritage Sites|ja|世界遺産}} and {{ill|Important Cultural Properties of Japan|ja|重要文化財}}.<ref>{{Cite web|title=World Cultural Heritage: Mt. Fuji Components|url=https://www.yamanashi-kankou.jp/heritage/fujisanwatcher/jp/world_heritage/index.html|publisher=Yamanashi Tourism Organization|access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Old Togawa Residence|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/185831|website=National Cultural Heritage Database|access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref>
== Cultural Depictions == * "Oshi Yagorō: Journey to Ise" by {{ill|Saijo Naka|ja|西條奈加}}, ISBN 439634015X * "{{ill|Sakuya-hime|ja|咲夜姫}}" by {{ill|Yamaguchi Kaito|ja|山口歌糸}}, ISBN 4783880204

revision.comment: Adding [[Category:qq]][[sn:{{subst:PAGENAME}}]][[Category:pages which might possibly be incomplete but are most likely complete]][[Category:I think I cleared all the incomplete or blank pages earlier]][[Category:qq]] on (CatAlot)
revision.text:
{{Otheruses|Shrine and temple guides|Music teachers for the emperor or imperial family|{{ill|御師 (音楽)|ja|御師 (音楽)}}}} [[File:Oshi 20190826a.jpg|thumb|Fuji-ko Guide (御師), photographed August 26, 2019]] '''Oshi''' (御師) refers to shrine priests associated with specific {{ill|shrines and temples|ja|社寺}}, who perform {{ill|prayers|ja|祈祷}}, guide worshippers, and assist pilgrims with visits and accommodations. In the case of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}, it was traditionally pronounced as "onshi." These guides often formed concentrated communities along highways, known as {{ill|Oshi towns|ja|御師町}}.
== Overview == Originally an abbreviation for "御祈祷師" (prayer guides), the term "oshi" emerged during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, initially referring to {{ill|temple monks|ja|社僧}}. Later, it extended to include shrine priests who facilitated pilgrimages.
== History == During the Heian period, notable examples of oshi included those from {{ill|Iwashimizu Hachiman Shrine|ja|石清水八幡宮}}, {{ill|Kamo Shrine|ja|賀茂神社}}, and {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|ja|日吉大社}}, with the {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} "Kumano Oshi" being particularly famous.<ref name="ushiyama">Yoshiyuki Ushiyama, "Faith and Culture in Medieval Ōhara," Shinshu University Bulletin of Education, Vol. 79 (1993), {{hdl|10091/1626}}</ref> The Kumano Oshi organized prayers, accommodations, and guided noble pilgrims during the late Heian period's {{ill|Kumano pilgrimage|ja|熊野詣}}. Relationships between oshi and pilgrims evolved into stable "master-disciple" ties, known as {{ill|師檀関係|ja|師檀関係}}, encompassing even commoners by the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}.
Between the Kamakura and {{ill|Muromachi periods|ja|室町時代}}, other prominent oshi groups emerged, such as those from {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}, {{ill|Fuji-ko|ja|富士講}}, {{ill|Matsuo Shrine|ja|松尾大社}}, {{ill|Mishima Taisha|ja|三嶋大社}}, and {{ill|Hakusan Shrine|ja|白山}}. Notably, records like the {{ill|Azuma Kagami|ja|吾妻鏡}} document oshi from {{ill|Izumo Taisha|ja|出雲大社}} serving {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝}}.<ref>'Izumo Taisha Teacher Training Manual,' Izumo Taisha Headquarters, 1989, p. 319</ref>
During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, oshi played an essential role as intermediaries between commoners and shrine priests. Pilgrimages like {{ill|Okage-mairi|lt=Ise pilgrimage|ja|伊勢参り}} and those to {{ill|Mt. Fuji|ja|富士山}} became widely accessible. Oshi organized {{ill|Kō (lecture)|ja|講|Kō (lecture)=Kō (lecture)|lt=kō (pilgrimage associations)}} and hosted pilgrims in their {{ill|Shukubo|ja|宿坊|Shukubo=Shukubo|lt=shukubō (pilgrim lodgings)}}. The economic stability of the Edo period allowed pilgrimages to combine faith and leisure, enhancing oshi influence. However, groups like the Kumano Oshi declined alongside {{ill|Kumano faith|ja|熊野信仰}}.
In Izumo, oshi structures laid the foundation for the {{ill|Izumo Taisha Faith|ja|出雲大社教}}, creating facilities such as "tanjo" (mission centers) to support local preaching.<ref>'Izumo Taisha Teacher Training Manual,' Izumo Taisha Headquarters, 1989, p. 319</ref>
With the establishment of the {{ill|Meiji government|ja|明治政府}}'s {{ill|Shinto|ja|神祇}} system, oshi rapidly declined. In 1869, oshi were barred from conducting {{ill|Shinto funerals|ja|神葬祭}}, and by 1871, the profession was abolished. Most oshi transitioned into farming or running inns. Some attempted to continue religious activities, forming organizations such as {{ill|Fusōkyō|ja|扶桑教}}.<ref>Takahiko Takano, "The Court and Religion in Early Modern Japan," Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2014, ISBN 978-4-642-03461-6, pp. 345-348.</ref>
=== Fuji Oshi === In the {{ill|Tenshōjingo Conflict|ja|天正壬午の乱}}, figures like Watanabe Shūgokuyū played significant roles. Notable Fuji Oshi residences, including {{ill|Old Togawa Residence|ja|旧外川家住宅}} and {{ill|Kosano Residence|ja|小佐野家住宅}}, are designated as {{ill|World Heritage Sites|ja|世界遺産}} and {{ill|Important Cultural Properties of Japan|ja|重要文化財}}.<ref>{{Cite web|title=World Cultural Heritage: Mt. Fuji Components|url=https://www.yamanashi-kankou.jp/heritage/fujisanwatcher/jp/world_heritage/index.html|publisher=Yamanashi Tourism Organization|access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Old Togawa Residence|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/185831|website=National Cultural Heritage Database|access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref>
== Cultural Depictions == * "Oshi Yagorō: Journey to Ise" by {{ill|Saijo Naka|ja|西條奈加}}, ISBN 439634015X * "{{ill|Sakuya-hime|ja|咲夜姫}}" by {{ill|Yamaguchi Kaito|ja|山口歌糸}}, ISBN 4783880204
== References == <references /> == See Also == * {{ill|Hagaki|ja|羽書}} * {{ill|Okage-mairi|ja|お蔭参り}} * {{ill|Shukubō|ja|宿坊}} * {{ill|Accommodation facilities|ja|宿泊施設}} * {{ill|Ise Calendar|ja|伊勢暦}} * [http://www.nikko-annai.com/ Dōshabiki] - Edo-period guides for worshippers at {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|ja|日光東照宮}}

{{Otheruses|Shrine and temple guides|Music teachers for the emperor or imperial family|{{ill|御師 (音楽)|ja|御師 (音楽)}}}} [[File:Oshi 20190826a.jpg|thumb|Fuji-ko Guide (御師), photographed August 26, 2019]] '''Oshi''' (御師) refers to shrine priests associated with specific {{ill|shrines and temples|ja|社寺}}, who perform {{ill|prayers|ja|祈祷}}, guide worshippers, and assist pilgrims with visits and accommodations. In the case of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}, it was traditionally pronounced as "onshi." These guides often formed concentrated communities along highways, known as {{ill|Oshi towns|ja|御師町}}.
== Overview == Originally an abbreviation for "御祈祷師" (prayer guides), the term "oshi" emerged during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, initially referring to {{ill|temple monks|ja|社僧}}. Later, it extended to include shrine priests who facilitated pilgrimages.
== History == During the Heian period, notable examples of oshi included those from {{ill|Iwashimizu Hachiman Shrine|ja|石清水八幡宮}}, {{ill|Kamo Shrine|ja|賀茂神社}}, and {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|ja|日吉大社}}, with the {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} "Kumano Oshi" being particularly famous.<ref name="ushiyama">Yoshiyuki Ushiyama, "Faith and Culture in Medieval Ōhara," Shinshu University Bulletin of Education, Vol. 79 (1993), {{hdl|10091/1626}}</ref> The Kumano Oshi organized prayers, accommodations, and guided noble pilgrims during the late Heian period's {{ill|Kumano pilgrimage|ja|熊野詣}}. Relationships between oshi and pilgrims evolved into stable "master-disciple" ties, known as {{ill|師檀関係|ja|師檀関係}}, encompassing even commoners by the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}.
Between the Kamakura and {{ill|Muromachi periods|ja|室町時代}}, other prominent oshi groups emerged, such as those from {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}, {{ill|Fuji-ko|ja|富士講}}, {{ill|Matsuo Shrine|ja|松尾大社}}, {{ill|Mishima Taisha|ja|三嶋大社}}, and {{ill|Hakusan Shrine|ja|白山}}. Notably, records like the {{ill|Azuma Kagami|ja|吾妻鏡}} document oshi from {{ill|Izumo Taisha|ja|出雲大社}} serving {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝}}.<ref>'Izumo Taisha Teacher Training Manual,' Izumo Taisha Headquarters, 1989, p. 319</ref>
During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, oshi played an essential role as intermediaries between commoners and shrine priests. Pilgrimages like {{ill|Okage-mairi|lt=Ise pilgrimage|ja|伊勢参り}} and those to {{ill|Mt. Fuji|ja|富士山}} became widely accessible. Oshi organized {{ill|Kō (lecture)|ja|講|Kō (lecture)=Kō (lecture)|lt=kō (pilgrimage associations)}} and hosted pilgrims in their {{ill|Shukubo|ja|宿坊|Shukubo=Shukubo|lt=shukubō (pilgrim lodgings)}}. The economic stability of the Edo period allowed pilgrimages to combine faith and leisure, enhancing oshi influence. However, groups like the Kumano Oshi declined alongside {{ill|Kumano faith|ja|熊野信仰}}.
In Izumo, oshi structures laid the foundation for the {{ill|Izumo Taisha Faith|ja|出雲大社教}}, creating facilities such as "tanjo" (mission centers) to support local preaching.<ref>'Izumo Taisha Teacher Training Manual,' Izumo Taisha Headquarters, 1989, p. 319</ref>
With the establishment of the {{ill|Meiji government|ja|明治政府}}'s {{ill|Shinto|ja|神祇}} system, oshi rapidly declined. In 1869, oshi were barred from conducting {{ill|Shinto funerals|ja|神葬祭}}, and by 1871, the profession was abolished. Most oshi transitioned into farming or running inns. Some attempted to continue religious activities, forming organizations such as {{ill|Fusōkyō|ja|扶桑教}}.<ref>Takahiko Takano, "The Court and Religion in Early Modern Japan," Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2014, ISBN 978-4-642-03461-6, pp. 345-348.</ref>
=== Fuji Oshi === In the {{ill|Tenshōjingo Conflict|ja|天正壬午の乱}}, figures like Watanabe Shūgokuyū played significant roles. Notable Fuji Oshi residences, including {{ill|Old Togawa Residence|ja|旧外川家住宅}} and {{ill|Kosano Residence|ja|小佐野家住宅}}, are designated as {{ill|World Heritage Sites|ja|世界遺産}} and {{ill|Important Cultural Properties of Japan|ja|重要文化財}}.<ref>{{Cite web|title=World Cultural Heritage: Mt. Fuji Components|url=https://www.yamanashi-kankou.jp/heritage/fujisanwatcher/jp/world_heritage/index.html|publisher=Yamanashi Tourism Organization|access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Old Togawa Residence|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/185831|website=National Cultural Heritage Database|access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref>
== Cultural Depictions == * "Oshi Yagorō: Journey to Ise" by {{ill|Saijo Naka|ja|西條奈加}}, ISBN 439634015X * "{{ill|Sakuya-hime|ja|咲夜姫}}" by {{ill|Yamaguchi Kaito|ja|山口歌糸}}, ISBN 4783880204

revision.comment: Bot: Reformatted page - moved categories, interwikis, DEFAULTSORT, and removed duplicates.
revision.text:
{{Otheruses|Shrine and temple guides|Music teachers for the emperor or imperial family|{{ill|御師 (音楽)|ja|御師 (音楽)}}}} [[File:Oshi 20190826a.jpg|thumb|Fuji-ko Guide (御師), photographed August 26, 2019]] '''Oshi''' (御師) refers to shrine priests associated with specific {{ill|shrines and temples|ja|社寺}}, who perform {{ill|prayers|ja|祈祷}}, guide worshippers, and assist pilgrims with visits and accommodations. In the case of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}, it was traditionally pronounced as "onshi." These guides often formed concentrated communities along highways, known as {{ill|Oshi towns|ja|御師町}}.
== Overview == Originally an abbreviation for "御祈祷師" (prayer guides), the term "oshi" emerged during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, initially referring to {{ill|temple monks|ja|社僧}}. Later, it extended to include shrine priests who facilitated pilgrimages.
== History == During the Heian period, notable examples of oshi included those from {{ill|Iwashimizu Hachiman Shrine|ja|石清水八幡宮}}, {{ill|Kamo Shrine|ja|賀茂神社}}, and {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|ja|日吉大社}}, with the {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} "Kumano Oshi" being particularly famous.<ref name="ushiyama">Yoshiyuki Ushiyama, "Faith and Culture in Medieval Ōhara," Shinshu University Bulletin of Education, Vol. 79 (1993), {{hdl|10091/1626}}</ref> The Kumano Oshi organized prayers, accommodations, and guided noble pilgrims during the late Heian period's {{ill|Kumano pilgrimage|ja|熊野詣}}. Relationships between oshi and pilgrims evolved into stable "master-disciple" ties, known as {{ill|師檀関係|ja|師檀関係}}, encompassing even commoners by the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}.
Between the Kamakura and {{ill|Muromachi periods|ja|室町時代}}, other prominent oshi groups emerged, such as those from {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}, {{ill|Fuji-ko|ja|富士講}}, {{ill|Matsuo Shrine|ja|松尾大社}}, {{ill|Mishima Taisha|ja|三嶋大社}}, and {{ill|Hakusan Shrine|ja|白山}}. Notably, records like the {{ill|Azuma Kagami|ja|吾妻鏡}} document oshi from {{ill|Izumo Taisha|ja|出雲大社}} serving {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝}}.<ref>'Izumo Taisha Teacher Training Manual,' Izumo Taisha Headquarters, 1989, p. 319</ref>
During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, oshi played an essential role as intermediaries between commoners and shrine priests. Pilgrimages like {{ill|Okage-mairi|lt=Ise pilgrimage|ja|伊勢参り}} and those to {{ill|Mt. Fuji|ja|富士山}} became widely accessible. Oshi organized {{ill|Kō (lecture)|ja|講|Kō (lecture)=Kō (lecture)|lt=kō (pilgrimage associations)}} and hosted pilgrims in their {{ill|Shukubo|ja|宿坊|Shukubo=Shukubo|lt=shukubō (pilgrim lodgings)}}. The economic stability of the Edo period allowed pilgrimages to combine faith and leisure, enhancing oshi influence. However, groups like the Kumano Oshi declined alongside {{ill|Kumano faith|ja|熊野信仰}}.
In Izumo, oshi structures laid the foundation for the {{ill|Izumo Taisha Faith|ja|出雲大社教}}, creating facilities such as "tanjo" (mission centers) to support local preaching.<ref>'Izumo Taisha Teacher Training Manual,' Izumo Taisha Headquarters, 1989, p. 319</ref>
With the establishment of the {{ill|Meiji government|ja|明治政府}}'s {{ill|Shinto|ja|神祇}} system, oshi rapidly declined. In 1869, oshi were barred from conducting {{ill|Shinto funerals|ja|神葬祭}}, and by 1871, the profession was abolished. Most oshi transitioned into farming or running inns. Some attempted to continue religious activities, forming organizations such as {{ill|Fusōkyō|ja|扶桑教}}.<ref>Takahiko Takano, "The Court and Religion in Early Modern Japan," Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2014, ISBN 978-4-642-03461-6, pp. 345-348.</ref>
=== Fuji Oshi === In the {{ill|Tenshōjingo Conflict|ja|天正壬午の乱}}, figures like Watanabe Shūgokuyū played significant roles. Notable Fuji Oshi residences, including {{ill|Old Togawa Residence|ja|旧外川家住宅}} and {{ill|Kosano Residence|ja|小佐野家住宅}}, are designated as {{ill|World Heritage Sites|ja|世界遺産}} and {{ill|Important Cultural Properties of Japan|ja|重要文化財}}.<ref>{{Cite web|title=World Cultural Heritage: Mt. Fuji Components|url=https://www.yamanashi-kankou.jp/heritage/fujisanwatcher/jp/world_heritage/index.html|publisher=Yamanashi Tourism Organization|access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Old Togawa Residence|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/185831|website=National Cultural Heritage Database|access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref>
== Cultural Depictions == * "Oshi Yagorō: Journey to Ise" by {{ill|Saijo Naka|ja|西條奈加}}, ISBN 439634015X * "{{ill|Sakuya-hime|ja|咲夜姫}}" by {{ill|Yamaguchi Kaito|ja|山口歌糸}}, ISBN 4783880204
== References == <references /> == See Also == * {{ill|Hagaki|ja|羽書}} * {{ill|Okage-mairi|ja|お蔭参り}} * {{ill|Shukubō|ja|宿坊}} * {{ill|Accommodation facilities|ja|宿泊施設}} * {{ill|Ise Calendar|ja|伊勢暦}} * [http://www.nikko-annai.com/ Dōshabiki] - Edo-period guides for worshippers at {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|ja|日光東照宮}}
{{Shinto sidebar}}

== Overview == Originally an abbreviation for "御祈祷師" (prayer guides), the term "oshi" emerged during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代|12=draft}}, initially referring to {{ill|temple monks|ja|社僧|12=draft}}. Later, it extended to include shrine priests who facilitated pilgrimages.
== History == During the Heian period, notable examples of oshi included those from {{ill|Iwashimizu Hachiman Shrine|ja|石清水八幡宮|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kamo Shrine|ja|賀茂神社|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|ja|日吉大社|12=draft}}, with the {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山|12=draft}} "Kumano Oshi" being particularly famous.<ref name="ushiyama">Yoshiyuki Ushiyama, "Faith and Culture in Medieval Ōhara," Shinshu University Bulletin of Education, Vol. 79 (1993), {{hdl|10091/1626}}</ref> The Kumano Oshi organized prayers, accommodations, and guided noble pilgrims during the late Heian period's {{ill|Kumano pilgrimage|ja|熊野詣|12=draft}}. Relationships between oshi and pilgrims evolved into stable "master-disciple" ties, known as {{ill|師檀関係|ja|師檀関係|12=draft}}, encompassing even commoners by the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代|12=draft}}.
Between the Kamakura and {{ill|Muromachi periods|ja|室町時代|12=draft}}, other prominent oshi groups emerged, such as those from {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮|12=draft}}, {{ill|Fuji-ko|ja|富士講|12=draft}}, {{ill|Matsuo Shrine|ja|松尾大社|12=draft}}, {{ill|Mishima Taisha|ja|三嶋大社|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Hakusan Shrine|ja|白山|12=draft}}. Notably, records like the {{ill|Azuma Kagami|ja|吾妻鏡|12=draft}} document oshi from {{ill|Izumo Taisha|ja|出雲大社|12=draft}} serving {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝|12=draft}}.<ref>'Izumo Taisha Teacher Training Manual,' Izumo Taisha Headquarters, 1989, p. 319</ref>
During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|12=draft}}, oshi played an essential role as intermediaries between commoners and shrine priests. Pilgrimages like {{ill|Okage-mairi|lt=Ise pilgrimage|ja|伊勢参り|12=draft}} and those to {{ill|Mt. Fuji|ja|富士山|12=draft}} became widely accessible. Oshi organized {{ill|Kō (lecture)|ja|講|Kō (lecture)=Kō (lecture)|lt=kō (pilgrimage associations)|12=draft}} and hosted pilgrims in their {{ill|Shukubo|ja|宿坊|Shukubo=Shukubo|lt=shukubō (pilgrim lodgings)|12=draft}}. The economic stability of the Edo period allowed pilgrimages to combine faith and leisure, enhancing oshi influence. However, groups like the Kumano Oshi declined alongside {{ill|Kumano faith|ja|熊野信仰|12=draft}}.
In Izumo, oshi structures laid the foundation for the {{ill|Izumo Taisha Faith|ja|出雲大社教|12=draft}}, creating facilities such as "tanjo" (mission centers) to support local preaching.<ref>'Izumo Taisha Teacher Training Manual,' Izumo Taisha Headquarters, 1989, p. 319</ref>
With the establishment of the {{ill|Meiji government|ja|明治政府|12=draft}}'s {{ill|Shinto|ja|神祇|12=draft}} system, oshi rapidly declined. In 1869, oshi were barred from conducting {{ill|Shinto funerals|ja|神葬祭|12=draft}}, and by 1871, the profession was abolished. Most oshi transitioned into farming or running inns. Some attempted to continue religious activities, forming organizations such as {{ill|Fusōkyō|ja|扶桑教|12=draft}}.<ref>Takahiko Takano, "The Court and Religion in Early Modern Japan," Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2014, ISBN 978-4-642-03461-6, pp. 345-348.</ref>
=== Fuji Oshi === In the {{ill|Tenshōjingo Conflict|ja|天正壬午の乱|12=draft}}, figures like Watanabe Shūgokuyū played significant roles. Notable Fuji Oshi residences, including {{ill|Old Togawa Residence|ja|旧外川家住宅|12=draft}} and {{ill|Kosano Residence|ja|小佐野家住宅|12=draft}}, are designated as {{ill|World Heritage Sites|ja|世界遺産|12=draft}} and {{ill|Important Cultural Properties of Japan|ja|重要文化財|12=draft}}.<ref>{{Cite web|title=World Cultural Heritage: Mt. Fuji Components|url=https://www.yamanashi-kankou.jp/heritage/fujisanwatcher/jp/world_heritage/index.html|publisher=Yamanashi Tourism Organization|access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Old Togawa Residence|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/185831|website=National Cultural Heritage Database|access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref>
== Cultural Depictions == * "Oshi Yagorō: Journey to Ise" by {{ill|Saijo Naka|ja|西條奈加|12=draft}}, ISBN 439634015X * "{{ill|Sakuya-hime|ja|咲夜姫|12=draft}}" by {{ill|Yamaguchi Kaito|ja|山口歌糸|12=draft}}, ISBN 4783880204
== References == <references /> == See Also == * {{ill|Hagaki|ja|羽書|12=draft}} * {{ill|Okage-mairi|ja|お蔭参り|12=draft}} * {{ill|Shukubō|ja|宿坊|12=draft}} * {{ill|Accommodation facilities|ja|宿泊施設|12=draft}} * {{ill|Ise Calendar|ja|伊勢暦|12=draft}} * [http://www.nikko-annai.com/ Dōshabiki] - Edo-period guides for worshippers at {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|ja|日光東照宮|12=draft}}

== History == During the Heian period, notable examples of oshi included those from {{ill|Iwashimizu Hachiman Shrine|ja|石清水八幡宮|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kamo Shrine|ja|賀茂神社|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|ja|日吉大社|12=draft}}, with the {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山|12=draft}} "Kumano Oshi" being particularly famous.<ref name="ushiyama">Yoshiyuki Ushiyama, "Faith and Culture in Medieval Ōhara," Shinshu University Bulletin of Education, Vol. 79 (1993), {{hdl|10091/1626}}</ref> The Kumano Oshi organized prayers, accommodations, and guided noble pilgrims during the late Heian period's {{ill|Kumano pilgrimage|ja|熊野詣|12=draft}}. Relationships between oshi and pilgrims evolved into stable "master-disciple" ties, known as {{ill|師檀関係|ja|師檀関係|12=draft}}, encompassing even commoners by the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代|12=draft}}.
Between the Kamakura and {{ill|Muromachi periods|ja|室町時代|12=draft}}, other prominent oshi groups emerged, such as those from {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮|12=draft}}, {{ill|Fuji-ko|ja|富士講|12=draft}}, {{ill|Matsuo Shrine|ja|松尾大社|12=draft}}, {{ill|Mishima Taisha|ja|三嶋大社|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Hakusan Shrine|ja|白山|12=draft}}. Notably, records like the {{ill|Azuma Kagami|ja|吾妻鏡|12=draft}} document oshi from {{ill|Izumo Taisha|ja|出雲大社|12=draft}} serving {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝|12=draft}}.<ref>'Izumo Taisha Teacher Training Manual,' Izumo Taisha Headquarters, 1989, p. 319</ref>
During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|12=draft}}, oshi played an essential role as intermediaries between commoners and shrine priests. Pilgrimages like {{ill|Okage-mairi|lt=Ise pilgrimage|ja|伊勢参り|12=draft}} and those to {{ill|Mt. Fuji|ja|富士山|12=draft}} became widely accessible. Oshi organized {{ill|Kō (lecture)|ja|講|Kō (lecture)=Kō (lecture)|lt=kō (pilgrimage associations)|12=draft}} and hosted pilgrims in their {{ill|Shukubo|ja|宿坊|Shukubo=Shukubo|lt=shukubō (pilgrim lodgings)|12=draft}}. The economic stability of the Edo period allowed pilgrimages to combine faith and leisure, enhancing oshi influence. However, groups like the Kumano Oshi declined alongside {{ill|Kumano faith|ja|熊野信仰|12=draft}}.
In Izumo, oshi structures laid the foundation for the {{ill|Izumo Taisha Faith|ja|出雲大社教|12=draft}}, creating facilities such as "tanjo" (mission centers) to support local preaching.<ref>'Izumo Taisha Teacher Training Manual,' Izumo Taisha Headquarters, 1989, p. 319</ref>
With the establishment of the {{ill|Meiji government|ja|明治政府|12=draft}}'s {{ill|Shinto|ja|神祇|12=draft}} system, oshi rapidly declined. In 1869, oshi were barred from conducting {{ill|Shinto funerals|ja|神葬祭|12=draft}}, and by 1871, the profession was abolished. Most oshi transitioned into farming or running inns. Some attempted to continue religious activities, forming organizations such as {{ill|Fusōkyō|ja|扶桑教|12=draft}}.<ref>Takahiko Takano, "The Court and Religion in Early Modern Japan," Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2014, ISBN 978-4-642-03461-6, pp. 345-348.</ref>
=== Fuji Oshi === In the {{ill|Tenshōjingo Conflict|ja|天正壬午の乱|12=draft}}, figures like Watanabe Shūgokuyū played significant roles. Notable Fuji Oshi residences, including {{ill|Old Togawa Residence|ja|旧外川家住宅|12=draft}} and {{ill|Kosano Residence|ja|小佐野家住宅|12=draft}}, are designated as {{ill|World Heritage Sites|ja|世界遺産|12=draft}} and {{ill|Important Cultural Properties of Japan|ja|重要文化財|12=draft}}.<ref>{{Cite web|title=World Cultural Heritage: Mt. Fuji Components|url=https://www.yamanashi-kankou.jp/heritage/fujisanwatcher/jp/world_heritage/index.html|publisher=Yamanashi Tourism Organization|access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Old Togawa Residence|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/185831|website=National Cultural Heritage Database|access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref>
== Cultural Depictions == * "Oshi Yagorō: Journey to Ise" by {{ill|Saijo Naka|ja|西條奈加|12=draft}}, ISBN 439634015X * "{{ill|Sakuya-hime|ja|咲夜姫|12=draft}}" by {{ill|Yamaguchi Kaito|ja|山口歌糸|12=draft}}, ISBN 4783880204
== References == <references /> == See Also == * {{ill|Hagaki|ja|羽書|12=draft}} * {{ill|Okage-mairi|ja|お蔭参り|12=draft}} * {{ill|Shukubō|ja|宿坊|12=draft}} * {{ill|Accommodation facilities|ja|宿泊施設|12=draft}} * {{ill|Ise Calendar|ja|伊勢暦|12=draft}} * [http://www.nikko-annai.com/ Dōshabiki] - Edo-period guides for worshippers at {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|ja|日光東照宮|12=draft}}

In the case of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮|12=draft}}, it was traditionally pronounced as "onshi." These guides often formed concentrated communities along highways, known as {{ill|Oshi towns|ja|御師町|12=draft}}.

Relationships between oshi and pilgrims evolved into stable "master-disciple" ties, known as {{ill|師檀関係|ja|師檀関係|12=draft}}, encompassing even commoners by the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代|12=draft}}.
Between the Kamakura and {{ill|Muromachi periods|ja|室町時代|12=draft}}, other prominent oshi groups emerged, such as those from {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮|12=draft}}, {{ill|Fuji-ko|ja|富士講|12=draft}}, {{ill|Matsuo Shrine|ja|松尾大社|12=draft}}, {{ill|Mishima Taisha|ja|三嶋大社|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Hakusan Shrine|ja|白山|12=draft}}. Notably, records like the {{ill|Azuma Kagami|ja|吾妻鏡|12=draft}} document oshi from {{ill|Izumo Taisha|ja|出雲大社|12=draft}} serving {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝|12=draft}}.<ref>'Izumo Taisha Teacher Training Manual,' Izumo Taisha Headquarters, 1989, p. 319</ref>
During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|12=draft}}, oshi played an essential role as intermediaries between commoners and shrine priests. Pilgrimages like {{ill|Okage-mairi|lt=Ise pilgrimage|ja|伊勢参り|12=draft}} and those to {{ill|Mt. Fuji|ja|富士山|12=draft}} became widely accessible. Oshi organized {{ill|Kō (lecture)|ja|講|Kō (lecture)=Kō (lecture)|lt=kō (pilgrimage associations)|12=draft}} and hosted pilgrims in their {{ill|Shukubo|ja|宿坊|Shukubo=Shukubo|lt=shukubō (pilgrim lodgings)|12=draft}}. The economic stability of the Edo period allowed pilgrimages to combine faith and leisure, enhancing oshi influence. However, groups like the Kumano Oshi declined alongside {{ill|Kumano faith|ja|熊野信仰|12=draft}}.
In Izumo, oshi structures laid the foundation for the {{ill|Izumo Taisha Faith|ja|出雲大社教|12=draft}}, creating facilities such as "tanjo" (mission centers) to support local preaching.<ref>'Izumo Taisha Teacher Training Manual,' Izumo Taisha Headquarters, 1989, p. 319</ref>
With the establishment of the {{ill|Meiji government|ja|明治政府|12=draft}}'s {{ill|Shinto|ja|神祇|12=draft}} system, oshi rapidly declined. In 1869, oshi were barred from conducting {{ill|Shinto funerals|ja|神葬祭|12=draft}}, and by 1871, the profession was abolished. Most oshi transitioned into farming or running inns. Some attempted to continue religious activities, forming organizations such as {{ill|Fusōkyō|ja|扶桑教|12=draft}}.<ref>Takahiko Takano, "The Court and Religion in Early Modern Japan," Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2014, ISBN 978-4-642-03461-6, pp. 345-348.</ref>
=== Fuji Oshi === In the {{ill|Tenshōjingo Conflict|ja|天正壬午の乱|12=draft}}, figures like Watanabe Shūgokuyū played significant roles. Notable Fuji Oshi residences, including {{ill|Old Togawa Residence|ja|旧外川家住宅|12=draft}} and {{ill|Kosano Residence|ja|小佐野家住宅|12=draft}}, are designated as {{ill|World Heritage Sites|ja|世界遺産|12=draft}} and {{ill|Important Cultural Properties of Japan|ja|重要文化財|12=draft}}.<ref>{{Cite web|title=World Cultural Heritage: Mt. Fuji Components|url=https://www.yamanashi-kankou.jp/heritage/fujisanwatcher/jp/world_heritage/index.html|publisher=Yamanashi Tourism Organization|access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Old Togawa Residence|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/185831|website=National Cultural Heritage Database|access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref>
== Cultural Depictions == * "Oshi Yagorō: Journey to Ise" by {{ill|Saijo Naka|ja|西條奈加|12=draft}}, ISBN 439634015X * "{{ill|Sakuya-hime|ja|咲夜姫|12=draft}}" by {{ill|Yamaguchi Kaito|ja|山口歌糸|12=draft}}, ISBN 4783880204
== References == <references /> == See Also == * {{ill|Hagaki|ja|羽書|12=draft}} * {{ill|Okage-mairi|ja|お蔭参り|12=draft}} * {{ill|Shukubō|ja|宿坊|12=draft}} * {{ill|Accommodation facilities|ja|宿泊施設|12=draft}} * {{ill|Ise Calendar|ja|伊勢暦|12=draft}} * [http://www.nikko-annai.com/ Dōshabiki] - Edo-period guides for worshippers at {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|ja|日光東照宮|12=draft}}

{{Otheruses|Shrine and temple guides|Music teachers for the emperor or imperial family|{{ill|御師 (音楽)|ja|御師 (音楽)|12=draft}}}} [[File:Oshi 20190826a.jpg|thumb|Fuji-ko Guide (御師), photographed August 26, 2019]] '''Oshi''' (御師) refers to shrine priests associated with specific {{ill|shrines and temples|ja|社寺|12=draft}}, who perform {{ill|prayers|ja|祈祷|12=draft}}, guide worshippers, and assist pilgrims with visits and accommodations. In the case of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮|12=draft}}, it was traditionally pronounced as "onshi." These guides often formed concentrated communities along highways, known as {{ill|Oshi towns|ja|御師町|12=draft}}.
== Overview == Originally an abbreviation for "御祈祷師" (prayer guides), the term "oshi" emerged during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代|12=draft}}, initially referring to {{ill|temple monks|ja|社僧|12=draft}}. Later, it extended to include shrine priests who facilitated pilgrimages.
== History == During the Heian period, notable examples of oshi included those from {{ill|Iwashimizu Hachiman Shrine|ja|石清水八幡宮|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kamo Shrine|ja|賀茂神社|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|ja|日吉大社|12=draft}}, with the {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山|12=draft}} "Kumano Oshi" being particularly famous.<ref name="ushiyama">Yoshiyuki Ushiyama, "Faith and Culture in Medieval Ōhara," Shinshu University Bulletin of Education, Vol. 79 (1993), {{hdl|10091/1626}}</ref> The Kumano Oshi organized prayers, accommodations, and guided noble pilgrims during the late Heian period's {{ill|Kumano pilgrimage|ja|熊野詣|12=draft}}. Relationships between oshi and pilgrims evolved into stable "master-disciple" ties, known as {{ill|師檀関係|ja|師檀関係|12=draft}}, encompassing even commoners by the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代|12=draft}}.
Between the Kamakura and {{ill|Muromachi periods|ja|室町時代|12=draft}}, other prominent oshi groups emerged, such as those from {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮|12=draft}}, {{ill|Fuji-ko|ja|富士講|12=draft}}, {{ill|Matsuo Shrine|ja|松尾大社|12=draft}}, {{ill|Mishima Taisha|ja|三嶋大社|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Hakusan Shrine|ja|白山|12=draft}}. Notably, records like the {{ill|Azuma Kagami|ja|吾妻鏡|12=draft}} document oshi from {{ill|Izumo Taisha|ja|出雲大社|12=draft}} serving {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝|12=draft}}.<ref>'Izumo Taisha Teacher Training Manual,' Izumo Taisha Headquarters, 1989, p. 319</ref>
During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|12=draft}}, oshi played an essential role as intermediaries between commoners and shrine priests. Pilgrimages like {{ill|Okage-mairi|lt=Ise pilgrimage|ja|伊勢参り|12=draft}} and those to {{ill|Mt. Fuji|ja|富士山|12=draft}} became widely accessible. Oshi organized {{ill|Kō (lecture)|ja|講|Kō (lecture)=Kō (lecture)|lt=kō (pilgrimage associations)|12=draft}} and hosted pilgrims in their {{ill|Shukubo|ja|宿坊|Shukubo=Shukubo|lt=shukubō (pilgrim lodgings)|12=draft}}. The economic stability of the Edo period allowed pilgrimages to combine faith and leisure, enhancing oshi influence. However, groups like the Kumano Oshi declined alongside {{ill|Kumano faith|ja|熊野信仰|1=Kumano Three Shrines|lt=Kumano faith|12=draft}}.
In Izumo, oshi structures laid the foundation for the {{ill|Izumo Taisha Faith|ja|出雲大社教|12=draft}}, creating facilities such as "tanjo" (mission centers) to support local preaching.<ref>'Izumo Taisha Teacher Training Manual,' Izumo Taisha Headquarters, 1989, p. 319</ref>
With the establishment of the {{ill|Meiji government|ja|明治政府|12=draft}}'s {{ill|Shinto|ja|神祇|12=draft}} system, oshi rapidly declined. In 1869, oshi were barred from conducting {{ill|Shinto funerals|ja|神葬祭|12=draft}}, and by 1871, the profession was abolished. Most oshi transitioned into farming or running inns. Some attempted to continue religious activities, forming organizations such as {{ill|Fusōkyō|ja|扶桑教|12=draft}}.<ref>Takahiko Takano, "The Court and Religion in Early Modern Japan," Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2014, ISBN 978-4-642-03461-6, pp. 345-348.</ref>
=== Fuji Oshi === In the {{ill|Tenshōjingo Conflict|ja|天正壬午の乱|12=draft}}, figures like Watanabe Shūgokuyū played significant roles. Notable Fuji Oshi residences, including {{ill|Old Togawa Residence|ja|旧外川家住宅|12=draft}} and {{ill|Kosano Residence|ja|小佐野家住宅|12=draft}}, are designated as {{ill|World Heritage Sites|ja|世界遺産|12=draft}} and {{ill|Important Cultural Properties of Japan|ja|重要文化財|12=draft}}.<ref>{{Cite web|title=World Cultural Heritage: Mt. Fuji Components|url=https://www.yamanashi-kankou.jp/heritage/fujisanwatcher/jp/world_heritage/index.html|publisher=Yamanashi Tourism Organization|access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Old Togawa Residence|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/185831|website=National Cultural Heritage Database|access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref>
== Cultural Depictions == * "Oshi Yagorō: Journey to Ise" by {{ill|Saijo Naka|ja|西條奈加|12=draft}}, ISBN 439634015X * "{{ill|Sakuya-hime|ja|咲夜姫|12=draft}}" by {{ill|Yamaguchi Kaito|ja|山口歌糸|12=draft}}, ISBN 4783880204
== References == <references /> == See Also == * {{ill|Hagaki|ja|羽書|12=draft}} * {{ill|Okage-mairi|ja|お蔭参り|12=draft}} * {{ill|Shukubō|ja|宿坊|12=draft}} * {{ill|Accommodation facilities|ja|宿泊施設|12=draft}} * {{ill|Ise Calendar|ja|伊勢暦|12=draft}} * [http://www.nikko-annai.com/ Dōshabiki] - Edo-period guides for worshippers at {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|ja|日光東照宮|12=draft}}

{{Otheruses|Shrine and temple guides|Music teachers for the emperor or imperial family|{{ill|御師 (音楽)|ja|御師 (音楽)|qq=draft}}}} [[File:Oshi 20190826a.jpg|thumb|Fuji-ko Guide (御師), photographed August 26, 2019]] '''Oshi''' (御師) refers to shrine priests associated with specific {{ill|shrines and temples|ja|社寺|qq=draft}}, who perform {{ill|prayers|ja|祈祷|qq=draft}}, guide worshippers, and assist pilgrims with visits and accommodations.
