Oshi organized {{ill|Kō (lecture)|ja|講|Kō (lecture)=Kō (lecture)|lt=kō (pilgrimage associations)|qq=draft}} and hosted pilgrims in their {{ill|Shukubo|ja|宿坊|Shukubo=Shukubo|lt=shukubō (pilgrim lodgings)|qq=draft}}. The economic stability of the Edo period allowed pilgrimages to combine faith and leisure, enhancing oshi influence. However, groups like the Kumano Oshi declined alongside {{ill|Kumano faith|ja|熊野信仰|1=Kumano Three Shrines|lt=Kumano faith|qq=draft}}.
In Izumo, oshi structures laid the foundation for the {{ill|Izumo Taisha Faith|ja|出雲大社教|qq=draft}}, creating facilities such as "tanjo" (mission centers) to support local preaching.<ref>'Izumo Taisha Teacher Training Manual,' Izumo Taisha Headquarters, 1989, p. 319</ref>
With the establishment of the {{ill|Meiji government|ja|明治政府|qq=draft}}'s {{ill|Shinto|ja|神祇|qq=draft}} system, oshi rapidly declined. In 1869, oshi were barred from conducting {{ill|Shinto funerals|ja|神葬祭|qq=draft}}, and by 1871, the profession was abolished. Most oshi transitioned into farming or running inns. Some attempted to continue religious activities, forming organizations such as {{ill|Fusōkyō|ja|扶桑教|qq=draft}}.<ref>Takahiko Takano, "The Court and Religion in Early Modern Japan," Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2014, ISBN 978-4-642-03461-6, pp. 345-348.</ref>
=== Fuji Oshi === In the {{ill|Tenshōjingo Conflict|ja|天正壬午の乱|qq=draft}}, figures like Watanabe Shūgokuyū played significant roles. Notable Fuji Oshi residences, including {{ill|Old Togawa Residence|ja|旧外川家住宅|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Kosano Residence|ja|小佐野家住宅|qq=draft}}, are designated as {{ill|World Heritage Sites|ja|世界遺産|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Important Cultural Properties of Japan|ja|重要文化財|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)|2=ja|3=重要文化財|lt=Important Cultural Property|qq= (Japan)|4=ko|5=일본의_중요문화재|qq=draft}}.<ref>{{Cite web|title=World Cultural Heritage: Mt. Fuji Components|url=https://www.yamanashi-kankou.jp/heritage/fujisanwatcher/jp/world_heritage/index.html|publisher=Yamanashi Tourism Organization|access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Old Togawa Residence|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/185831|website=National Cultural Heritage Database|access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref>
== Cultural Depictions == * "Oshi Yagorō: Journey to Ise" by {{ill|Saijo Naka|ja|西條奈加|qq=draft}}, ISBN 439634015X * "{{ill|Sakuya-hime|ja|咲夜姫|qq=draft}}" by {{ill|Yamaguchi Kaito|ja|山口歌糸|qq=draft}}, ISBN 4783880204
== References == <references /> == See Also == * {{ill|Hagaki|ja|羽書|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Okage-mairi|ja|お蔭参り|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Shukubō|ja|宿坊|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Accommodation facilities|ja|宿泊施設|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Ise Calendar|ja|伊勢暦|qq=draft}} * [http://www.nikko-annai.com/ Dōshabiki] - Edo-period guides for worshippers at {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|ja|日光東照宮|qq=draft}}

revision.text:
{{Otheruses|Shrine and temple guides|Music teachers for the emperor or imperial family|{{ill|御師 (音楽)|ja|御師 (音楽)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/御師 (音楽)|qq=}}}} [[File:Oshi 20190826a.jpg|thumb|Fuji-ko Guide (御師), photographed August 26, 2019]] '''Oshi''' (御師) refers to shrine priests associated with specific {{ill|shrines and temples|ja|社寺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/shrines and temples|qq=}}, who perform {{ill|prayers|ja|祈祷|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/prayers|qq=}}, guide worshippers, and assist pilgrims with visits and accommodations. In the case of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Grand Shrine|qq=}}, it was traditionally pronounced as "onshi." These guides often formed concentrated communities along highways, known as {{ill|Oshi towns|ja|御師町|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oshi towns|qq=}}.
== Overview == Originally an abbreviation for "御祈祷師" (prayer guides), the term "oshi" emerged during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heian period|qq=}}, initially referring to {{ill|temple monks|ja|社僧|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/temple monks|qq=}}. Later, it extended to include shrine priests who facilitated pilgrimages.
== History == During the Heian period, notable examples of oshi included those from {{ill|Iwashimizu Hachiman Shrine|ja|石清水八幡宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwashimizu Hachiman Shrine|qq=}}, {{ill|Kamo Shrine|ja|賀茂神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamo Shrine|qq=}}, and {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|ja|日吉大社|1=Hiyoshi Taisha|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hiyoshi Taisha|13=User:Immanuelle/Hiyoshi Taisha|qq=}}, with the {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Sanzan|qq=}} "Kumano Oshi" being particularly famous.<ref name="ushiyama">Yoshiyuki Ushiyama, "Faith and Culture in Medieval Ōhara," Shinshu University Bulletin of Education, Vol. 79 (1993), {{hdl|10091/1626}}</ref> The Kumano Oshi organized prayers, accommodations, and guided noble pilgrims during the late Heian period's {{ill|Kumano pilgrimage|ja|熊野詣|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano pilgrimage|qq=}}. Relationships between oshi and pilgrims evolved into stable "master-disciple" ties, known as {{ill|師檀関係|ja|師檀関係|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/師檀関係|qq=}}, encompassing even commoners by the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamakura period|qq=}}.
Between the Kamakura and {{ill|Muromachi periods|ja|室町時代|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muromachi periods|qq=}}, other prominent oshi groups emerged, such as those from {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Grand Shrine|qq=}}, {{ill|Fuji-ko|ja|富士講|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fuji-ko|qq=}}, {{ill|Matsuo Shrine|ja|松尾大社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsuo Shrine|qq=}}, {{ill|Mishima Taisha|ja|三嶋大社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mishima Taisha|qq=}}, and {{ill|Hakusan Shrine|ja|白山|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hakusan Shrine|qq=}}. Notably, records like the {{ill|Azuma Kagami|ja|吾妻鏡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Azuma Kagami|qq=}} document oshi from {{ill|Izumo Taisha|ja|出雲大社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumo Taisha|qq=}} serving {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Yoritomo|qq=}}.<ref>'Izumo Taisha Teacher Training Manual,' Izumo Taisha Headquarters, 1989, p. 319</ref>
During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Edo period|qq=}}, oshi played an essential role as intermediaries between commoners and shrine priests. Pilgrimages like {{ill|Okage-mairi|lt=Ise pilgrimage|ja|伊勢参り|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okage-mairi|qq=}} and those to {{ill|Mt. Fuji|ja|富士山|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mt. Fuji|qq=}} became widely accessible. Oshi organized {{ill|Kō (lecture)|ja|講|Kō (lecture)=Kō (lecture)|lt=kō (pilgrimage associations)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kō (lecture)|qq=}} and hosted pilgrims in their {{ill|Shukubo|ja|宿坊|Shukubo=Shukubo|lt=shukubō (pilgrim lodgings)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shukubo|qq=}}. The economic stability of the Edo period allowed pilgrimages to combine faith and leisure, enhancing oshi influence. However, groups like the Kumano Oshi declined alongside {{ill|Kumano faith|ja|熊野信仰|1=Kumano Three Shrines|lt=Kumano faith|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano faith|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Three Shrines|qq=}}.
In Izumo, oshi structures laid the foundation for the {{ill|Izumo Taisha Faith|ja|出雲大社教|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumo Taisha Faith|qq=}}, creating facilities such as "tanjo" (mission centers) to support local preaching.<ref>'Izumo Taisha Teacher Training Manual,' Izumo Taisha Headquarters, 1989, p. 319</ref>
With the establishment of the {{ill|Meiji government|ja|明治政府|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji government|qq=}}'s {{ill|Shinto|ja|神祇|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto|qq=}} system, oshi rapidly declined. In 1869, oshi were barred from conducting {{ill|Shinto funerals|ja|神葬祭|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto funerals|qq=}}, and by 1871, the profession was abolished. Most oshi transitioned into farming or running inns. Some attempted to continue religious activities, forming organizations such as {{ill|Fusōkyō|ja|扶桑教|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fusōkyō|qq=}}.<ref>Takahiko Takano, "The Court and Religion in Early Modern Japan," Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2014, ISBN 978-4-642-03461-6, pp. 345-348.</ref>
=== Fuji Oshi === In the {{ill|Tenshōjingo Conflict|ja|天正壬午の乱|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenshōjingo Conflict|qq=}}, figures like Watanabe Shūgokuyū played significant roles. Notable Fuji Oshi residences, including {{ill|Old Togawa Residence|ja|旧外川家住宅|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Old Togawa Residence|qq=}} and {{ill|Kosano Residence|ja|小佐野家住宅|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kosano Residence|qq=}}, are designated as {{ill|World Heritage Sites|ja|世界遺産|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/World Heritage Sites|qq=}} and {{ill|Important Cultural Properties of Japan|ja|重要文化財|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)|2=ja|3=重要文化財|lt=Important Cultural Property|qq= (Japan)|4=ko|5=일본의_중요문화재|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Important Cultural Properties of Japan|13=User:Immanuelle/Important Cultural Property (Japan)|qq=}}.<ref>{{Cite web|title=World Cultural Heritage: Mt. Fuji Components|url=https://www.yamanashi-kankou.jp/heritage/fujisanwatcher/jp/world_heritage/index.html|publisher=Yamanashi Tourism Organization|access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Old Togawa Residence|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/185831|website=National Cultural Heritage Database|access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref>
== Cultural Depictions == * "Oshi Yagorō: Journey to Ise" by {{ill|Saijo Naka|ja|西條奈加|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saijo Naka|qq=}}, ISBN 439634015X * "{{ill|Sakuya-hime|ja|咲夜姫|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakuya-hime|qq=}}" by {{ill|Yamaguchi Kaito|ja|山口歌糸|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamaguchi Kaito|qq=}}, ISBN 4783880204
== References == <references /> == See Also == * {{ill|Hagaki|ja|羽書|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hagaki|qq=}} * {{ill|Okage-mairi|ja|お蔭参り|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okage-mairi|qq=}} * {{ill|Shukubō|ja|宿坊|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shukubō|qq=}} * {{ill|Accommodation facilities|ja|宿泊施設|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Accommodation facilities|qq=}} * {{ill|Ise Calendar|ja|伊勢暦|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Calendar|qq=}} * [http://www.nikko-annai.com/ Dōshabiki] - Edo-period guides for worshippers at {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|ja|日光東照宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nikkō Tōshō-gū|qq=}}

Fuji Components|url=https://www.yamanashi-kankou.jp/heritage/fujisanwatcher/jp/world_heritage/index.html|publisher=Yamanashi Tourism Organization|access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Old Togawa Residence|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/185831|website=National Cultural Heritage Database|access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref>
== Cultural Depictions == * "Oshi Yagorō: Journey to Ise" by {{ill|Saijo Naka|ja|西條奈加|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saijo Naka|qq=}}, ISBN 439634015X * "{{ill|Sakuya-hime|ja|咲夜姫|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakuya-hime|qq=}}" by {{ill|Yamaguchi Kaito|ja|山口歌糸|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamaguchi Kaito|qq=}}, ISBN 4783880204
== References == <references /> == See Also == * {{ill|Hagaki|ja|羽書|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hagaki|qq=}} * {{ill|Okage-mairi|ja|お蔭参り|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okage-mairi|qq=}} * {{ill|Shukubō|ja|宿坊|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shukubō|qq=}} * {{ill|Accommodation facilities|ja|宿泊施設|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Accommodation facilities|qq=}} * {{ill|Ise Calendar|ja|伊勢暦|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Calendar|qq=}} * [http://www.nikko-annai.com/ Dōshabiki] - Edo-period guides for worshippers at {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|ja|日光東照宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nikkō Tōshō-gū|qq=}}

[[sn:Onshi]] [[ja:御師]] [[sn:nshi]] [[en:Onshi]]
<!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi-->

{{Otheruses|Shrine and temple guides|Music teachers for the emperor or imperial family|{{ill|御師 (音楽)|ja|御師 (音楽)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/御師 (音楽)|qq=}}}} [[File:Oshi 20190826a.jpg|thumb|Fuji-ko Guide (御師), photographed August 26, 2019]] '''Oshi''' (御師) refers to shrine priests associated with specific {{ill|shrines and temples|ja|社寺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/shrines and temples|qq=}}, who perform {{ill|prayers|ja|祈祷|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/prayers|qq=}}, guide worshippers, and assist pilgrims with visits and accommodations. In the case of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Grand Shrine|qq=}}, it was traditionally pronounced as "onshi." These guides often formed concentrated communities along highways, known as {{ill|Oshi towns|ja|御師町|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oshi towns|qq=}}.
== Overview == Originally an abbreviation for "御祈祷師" (prayer guides), the term "oshi" emerged during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heian period|qq=}}, initially referring to {{ill|temple monks|ja|社僧|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/temple monks|qq=}}. Later, it extended to include shrine priests who facilitated pilgrimages.
== History == During the Heian period, notable examples of oshi included those from {{ill|Iwashimizu Hachiman Shrine|ja|石清水八幡宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwashimizu Hachiman Shrine|qq=}}, {{ill|Kamo shrines|lt=Kamo shrines|lt=Kamo Shrine|ja|賀茂神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamo Shrine|qq=}}, and {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|ja|日吉大社|1=Hiyoshi Taisha|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hiyoshi Taisha|13=User:Immanuelle/Hiyoshi Taisha|qq=}}, with the {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Sanzan|qq=}} "Kumano Oshi" being particularly famous.<ref name="ushiyama">Yoshiyuki Ushiyama, "Faith and Culture in Medieval Ōhara," Shinshu University Bulletin of Education, Vol. 79 (1993), {{hdl|10091/1626}}</ref> The Kumano Oshi organized prayers, accommodations, and guided noble pilgrims during the late Heian period's {{ill|Kumano pilgrimage|ja|熊野詣|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano pilgrimage|qq=}}. Relationships between oshi and pilgrims evolved into stable "master-disciple" ties, known as {{ill|師檀関係|ja|師檀関係|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/師檀関係|qq=}}, encompassing even commoners by the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamakura period|qq=}}.
Between the Kamakura and {{ill|Muromachi periods|ja|室町時代|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muromachi periods|qq=}}, other prominent oshi groups emerged, such as those from {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Grand Shrine|qq=}}, {{ill|Fuji-ko|ja|富士講|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fuji-ko|qq=}}, {{ill|Matsuo Shrine|ja|松尾大社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsuo Shrine|qq=}}, {{ill|Mishima Taisha|ja|三嶋大社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mishima Taisha|qq=}}, and {{ill|Hakusan Shrine|ja|白山|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hakusan Shrine|qq=}}. Notably, records like the {{ill|Azuma Kagami|ja|吾妻鏡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Azuma Kagami|qq=}} document oshi from {{ill|Izumo-taisha|lt=Izumo-taisha|lt=Izumo Taisha|ja|出雲大社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumo Taisha|qq=}} serving {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Yoritomo|qq=}}.<ref>'Izumo Taisha Teacher Training Manual,' Izumo Taisha Headquarters, 1989, p. 319</ref>
During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Edo period|qq=}}, oshi played an essential role as intermediaries between commoners and shrine priests. Pilgrimages like {{ill|Okage-mairi|lt=Ise pilgrimage|ja|伊勢参り|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okage-mairi|qq=}} and those to {{ill|Mt. Fuji|ja|富士山|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mt. Fuji|qq=}} became widely accessible. Oshi organized {{ill|Kō (lecture)|ja|講|Kō (lecture)=Kō (lecture)|lt=kō (pilgrimage associations)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kō (lecture)|qq=}} and hosted pilgrims in their {{ill|Shukubo|ja|宿坊|Shukubo=Shukubo|lt=shukubō (pilgrim lodgings)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shukubo|qq=}}. The economic stability of the Edo period allowed pilgrimages to combine faith and leisure, enhancing oshi influence. However, groups like the Kumano Oshi declined alongside {{ill|Kumano faith|ja|熊野信仰|1=Kumano Three Shrines|lt=Kumano faith|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano faith|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Three Shrines|qq=}}.
In Izumo, oshi structures laid the foundation for the {{ill|Izumo Taisha Faith|ja|出雲大社教|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumo Taisha Faith|qq=}}, creating facilities such as "tanjo" (mission centers) to support local preaching.<ref>'Izumo Taisha Teacher Training Manual,' Izumo Taisha Headquarters, 1989, p. 319</ref>
With the establishment of the {{ill|Meiji government|ja|明治政府|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji government|qq=}}'s {{ill|Shinto|ja|神祇|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto|qq=}} system, oshi rapidly declined. In 1869, oshi were barred from conducting {{ill|Shinto funerals|ja|神葬祭|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto funerals|qq=}}, and by 1871, the profession was abolished. Most oshi transitioned into farming or running inns. Some attempted to continue religious activities, forming organizations such as {{ill|Fusōkyō|ja|扶桑教|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fusōkyō|qq=}}.<ref>Takahiko Takano, "The Court and Religion in Early Modern Japan," Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2014, ISBN 978-4-642-03461-6, pp. 345-348.</ref>
=== Fuji Oshi === In the {{ill|Tenshōjingo Conflict|ja|天正壬午の乱|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenshōjingo Conflict|qq=}}, figures like Watanabe Shūgokuyū played significant roles. Notable Fuji Oshi residences, including {{ill|Old Togawa Residence|ja|旧外川家住宅|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Old Togawa Residence|qq=}} and {{ill|Kosano Residence|ja|小佐野家住宅|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kosano Residence|qq=}}, are designated as {{ill|World Heritage Sites|ja|世界遺産|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/World Heritage Sites|qq=}} and {{ill|Important Cultural Properties of Japan|ja|重要文化財|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)|2=ja|3=重要文化財|lt=Important Cultural Property|qq= (Japan)|4=ko|5=일본의_중요문화재|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Important Cultural Properties of Japan|13=User:Immanuelle/Important Cultural Property (Japan)|qq=}}.<ref>{{Cite web|title=World Cultural Heritage: Mt. Fuji Components|url=https://www.yamanashi-kankou.jp/heritage/fujisanwatcher/jp/world_heritage/index.html|publisher=Yamanashi Tourism Organization|access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Old Togawa Residence|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/185831|website=National Cultural Heritage Database|access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref>
== Cultural Depictions == * "Oshi Yagorō: Journey to Ise" by {{ill|Saijo Naka|ja|西條奈加|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saijo Naka|qq=}}, ISBN 439634015X * "{{ill|Sakuya-hime|ja|咲夜姫|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakuya-hime|qq=}}" by {{ill|Yamaguchi Kaito|ja|山口歌糸|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamaguchi Kaito|qq=}}, ISBN 4783880204
== References == <references /> == See Also == * {{ill|Hagaki|ja|羽書|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hagaki|qq=}} * {{ill|Okage-mairi|ja|お蔭参り|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okage-mairi|qq=}} * {{ill|Shukubō|ja|宿坊|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shukubō|qq=}} * {{ill|Accommodation facilities|ja|宿泊施設|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Accommodation facilities|qq=}} * {{ill|Ise Calendar|ja|伊勢暦|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Calendar|qq=}} * [http://www.nikko-annai.com/ Dōshabiki] - Edo-period guides for worshippers at {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|ja|日光東照宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nikkō Tōshō-gū|qq=}}

<!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi-->

{{Otheruses|Shrine and temple guides|Music teachers for the emperor or imperial family|{{ill|御師 (音楽)|ja|御師 (音楽)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/御師 (音楽)|qq=}}}} [[File:Oshi 20190826a.jpg|thumb|Fuji-ko Guide (御師), photographed August 26, 2019]] '''Oshi''' (御師) refers to shrine priests associated with specific {{ill|shrines and temples|ja|社寺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/shrines and temples|qq=}}, who perform {{ill|prayers|ja|祈祷|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/prayers|qq=}}, guide worshippers, and assist pilgrims with visits and accommodations. In the case of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Grand Shrine|qq=}}, it was traditionally pronounced as "onshi." These guides often formed concentrated communities along highways, known as {{ill|Oshi towns|ja|御師町|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oshi towns|qq=}}.
== Overview == Originally an abbreviation for "御祈祷師" (prayer guides), the term "oshi" emerged during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heian period|qq=}}, initially referring to {{ill|temple monks|ja|社僧|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/temple monks|qq=}}. Later, it extended to include shrine priests who facilitated pilgrimages.
== History == During the Heian period, notable examples of oshi included those from {{ill|Iwashimizu Hachiman Shrine|ja|石清水八幡宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwashimizu Hachiman Shrine|qq=}}, {{ill|Kamo shrines|lt=Kamo shrines|lt=Kamo Shrine|ja|賀茂神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamo Shrine|qq=}}, and {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|ja|日吉大社|1=Hiyoshi Taisha|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hiyoshi Taisha|13=User:Immanuelle/Hiyoshi Taisha|qq=}}, with the {{ill|Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Sanzan|qq=}} "Kumano Oshi" being particularly famous.<ref name="ushiyama">Yoshiyuki Ushiyama, "Faith and Culture in Medieval Ōhara," Shinshu University Bulletin of Education, Vol. 79 (1993), {{hdl|10091/1626}}</ref> The Kumano Oshi organized prayers, accommodations, and guided noble pilgrims during the late Heian period's {{ill|Kumano pilgrimage|ja|熊野詣|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano pilgrimage|qq=}}. Relationships between oshi and pilgrims evolved into stable "master-disciple" ties, known as {{ill|師檀関係|ja|師檀関係|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/師檀関係|qq=}}, encompassing even commoners by the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamakura period|qq=}}.
Between the Kamakura and {{ill|Muromachi periods|ja|室町時代|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muromachi periods|qq=}}, other prominent oshi groups emerged, such as those from {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Grand Shrine|qq=}}, {{ill|Fuji-ko|ja|富士講|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fuji-ko|qq=}}, {{ill|Matsuo Shrine|ja|松尾大社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsuo Shrine|qq=}}, {{ill|Mishima Taisha|ja|三嶋大社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mishima Taisha|qq=}}, and {{ill|Hakusan Shrine|ja|白山|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hakusan Shrine|qq=}}. Notably, records like the {{ill|Azuma Kagami|ja|吾妻鏡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Azuma Kagami|qq=}} document oshi from {{ill|Izumo-taisha|lt=Izumo-taisha|lt=Izumo Taisha|ja|出雲大社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumo Taisha|qq=}} serving {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Yoritomo|qq=}}.<ref>'Izumo Taisha Teacher Training Manual,' Izumo Taisha Headquarters, 1989, p. 319</ref>
During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Edo period|qq=}}, oshi played an essential role as intermediaries between commoners and shrine priests. Pilgrimages like {{ill|Okage-mairi|lt=Ise pilgrimage|ja|伊勢参り|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okage-mairi|qq=}} and those to {{ill|Mt. Fuji|ja|富士山|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mt. Fuji|qq=}} became widely accessible. Oshi organized {{ill|Kō (lecture)|ja|講|Kō (lecture)=Kō (lecture)|lt=kō (pilgrimage associations)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kō (lecture)|qq=}} and hosted pilgrims in their {{ill|Shukubo|ja|宿坊|Shukubo=Shukubo|lt=shukubō (pilgrim lodgings)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shukubo|qq=}}. The economic stability of the Edo period allowed pilgrimages to combine faith and leisure, enhancing oshi influence. However, groups like the Kumano Oshi declined alongside {{ill|Kumano faith|ja|熊野信仰|1=Kumano Three Shrines|lt=Kumano faith|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano faith|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Three Shrines|qq=}}.
In Izumo, oshi structures laid the foundation for the {{ill|Izumo Taisha Faith|ja|出雲大社教|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumo Taisha Faith|qq=}}, creating facilities such as "tanjo" (mission centers) to support local preaching.<ref>'Izumo Taisha Teacher Training Manual,' Izumo Taisha Headquarters, 1989, p. 319</ref>
With the establishment of the {{ill|Meiji government|ja|明治政府|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji government|qq=}}'s {{ill|Shinto|ja|神祇|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto|qq=}} system, oshi rapidly declined. In 1869, oshi were barred from conducting {{ill|Shinto funerals|ja|神葬祭|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto funerals|qq=}}, and by 1871, the profession was abolished. Most oshi transitioned into farming or running inns. Some attempted to continue religious activities, forming organizations such as {{ill|Fusōkyō|ja|扶桑教|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fusōkyō|qq=}}.<ref>Takahiko Takano, "The Court and Religion in Early Modern Japan," Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2014, ISBN 978-4-642-03461-6, pp. 345-348.</ref>
=== Fuji Oshi === In the {{ill|Tenshōjingo Conflict|ja|天正壬午の乱|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenshōjingo Conflict|qq=}}, figures like Watanabe Shūgokuyū played significant roles. Notable Fuji Oshi residences, including {{ill|Old Togawa Residence|ja|旧外川家住宅|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Old Togawa Residence|qq=}} and {{ill|Kosano Residence|ja|小佐野家住宅|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kosano Residence|qq=}}, are designated as {{ill|World Heritage Sites|ja|世界遺産|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/World Heritage Sites|qq=}} and {{ill|Important Cultural Properties of Japan|ja|重要文化財|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)|2=ja|3=重要文化財|lt=Important Cultural Property|qq= (Japan)|4=ko|5=일본의_중요문화재|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Important Cultural Properties of Japan|13=User:Immanuelle/Important Cultural Property (Japan)|qq=}}.<ref>{{Cite web|title=World Cultural Heritage: Mt. Fuji Components|url=https://www.yamanashi-kankou.jp/heritage/fujisanwatcher/jp/world_heritage/index.html|publisher=Yamanashi Tourism Organization|access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Old Togawa Residence|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/185831|website=National Cultural Heritage Database|access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref>
== Cultural Depictions == * "Oshi Yagorō: Journey to Ise" by {{ill|Saijo Naka|ja|西條奈加|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saijo Naka|qq=}}, ISBN 439634015X * "{{ill|Sakuya-hime|ja|咲夜姫|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakuya-hime|qq=}}" by {{ill|Yamaguchi Kaito|ja|山口歌糸|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamaguchi Kaito|qq=}}, ISBN 4783880204
== References == <references /> == See Also == * {{ill|Hagaki|ja|羽書|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hagaki|qq=}} * {{ill|Okage-mairi|ja|お蔭参り|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okage-mairi|qq=}} * {{ill|Shukubō|ja|宿坊|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shukubō|qq=}} * {{ill|Accommodation facilities|ja|宿泊施設|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Accommodation facilities|qq=}} * {{ill|Ise Calendar|ja|伊勢暦|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Calendar|qq=}} * [http://www.nikko-annai.com/ Dōshabiki] - Edo-period guides for worshippers at {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|ja|日光東照宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nikkō Tōshō-gū|qq=}}

== Overview == Originally an abbreviation for "御祈祷師" (prayer guides), the term "oshi" emerged during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heian period|qq=}}, initially referring to {{ill|temple monks|ja|社僧|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/temple monks|qq=}}. Later, it extended to include shrine priests who facilitated pilgrimages.
== History == During the Heian period, notable examples of oshi included those from {{ill|Iwashimizu Hachiman Shrine|ja|石清水八幡宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwashimizu Hachiman Shrine|qq=}}, {{ill|Kamo shrines|lt=Kamo shrines|lt=Kamo Shrine|ja|賀茂神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamo Shrine|qq=}}, and {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|ja|日吉大社|1=Hiyoshi Taisha|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hiyoshi Taisha|13=User:Immanuelle/Hiyoshi Taisha|qq=}}, with the {{ill|Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Sanzan|qq=}} "Kumano Oshi" being particularly famous.<ref name="ushiyama">Yoshiyuki Ushiyama, "Faith and Culture in Medieval Ōhara," Shinshu University Bulletin of Education, Vol. 79 (1993), {{hdl|10091/1626}}</ref> The Kumano Oshi organized prayers, accommodations, and guided noble pilgrims during the late Heian period's {{ill|Kumano pilgrimage|ja|熊野詣|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano pilgrimage|qq=}}. Relationships between oshi and pilgrims evolved into stable "master-disciple" ties, known as {{ill|師檀関係|ja|師檀関係|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/師檀関係|qq=}}, encompassing even commoners by the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamakura period|qq=}}.
Between the Kamakura and {{ill|Muromachi periods|ja|室町時代|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muromachi periods|qq=}}, other prominent oshi groups emerged, such as those from {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Grand Shrine|qq=}}, {{ill|Fuji-ko|ja|富士講|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fuji-ko|qq=}}, {{ill|Matsuo Shrine|ja|松尾大社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsuo Shrine|qq=}}, {{ill|Mishima Taisha|ja|三嶋大社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mishima Taisha|qq=}}, and {{ill|Hakusan Shrine|ja|白山|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hakusan Shrine|qq=}}. Notably, records like the {{ill|Azuma Kagami|ja|吾妻鏡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Azuma Kagami|qq=}} document oshi from {{ill|Izumo-taisha|lt=Izumo-taisha|lt=Izumo Taisha|ja|出雲大社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumo Taisha|qq=}} serving {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Yoritomo|qq=}}.<ref>'Izumo Taisha Teacher Training Manual,' Izumo Taisha Headquarters, 1989, p. 319</ref>
During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Edo period|qq=}}, oshi played an essential role as intermediaries between commoners and shrine priests. Pilgrimages like {{ill|Okage-mairi|lt=Ise pilgrimage|ja|伊勢参り|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okage-mairi|qq=}} and those to {{ill|Mt. Fuji|ja|富士山|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mt. Fuji|qq=}} became widely accessible. Oshi organized {{ill|Kō (lecture)|ja|講|Kō (lecture)=Kō (lecture)|lt=kō (pilgrimage associations)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kō (lecture)|qq=}} and hosted pilgrims in their {{ill|Shukubo|ja|宿坊|Shukubo=Shukubo|lt=shukubō (pilgrim lodgings)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shukubo|qq=}}. The economic stability of the Edo period allowed pilgrimages to combine faith and leisure, enhancing oshi influence. However, groups like the Kumano Oshi declined alongside {{ill|Kumano faith|ja|熊野信仰|1=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Three Shrines|lt=Kumano faith|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano faith|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Three Shrines|qq=}}.
In Izumo, oshi structures laid the foundation for the {{ill|Izumo Taisha Faith|ja|出雲大社教|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumo Taisha Faith|qq=}}, creating facilities such as "tanjo" (mission centers) to support local preaching.<ref>'Izumo Taisha Teacher Training Manual,' Izumo Taisha Headquarters, 1989, p. 319</ref>
With the establishment of the {{ill|Meiji government|ja|明治政府|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji government|qq=}}'s {{ill|Shinto|ja|神祇|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto|qq=}} system, oshi rapidly declined. In 1869, oshi were barred from conducting {{ill|Shinto funerals|ja|神葬祭|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto funerals|qq=}}, and by 1871, the profession was abolished. Most oshi transitioned into farming or running inns. Some attempted to continue religious activities, forming organizations such as {{ill|Fusōkyō|ja|扶桑教|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fusōkyō|qq=}}.<ref>Takahiko Takano, "The Court and Religion in Early Modern Japan," Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2014, ISBN 978-4-642-03461-6, pp. 345-348.</ref>
=== Fuji Oshi === In the {{ill|Tenshōjingo Conflict|ja|天正壬午の乱|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenshōjingo Conflict|qq=}}, figures like Watanabe Shūgokuyū played significant roles. Notable Fuji Oshi residences, including {{ill|Old Togawa Residence|ja|旧外川家住宅|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Old Togawa Residence|qq=}} and {{ill|Kosano Residence|ja|小佐野家住宅|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kosano Residence|qq=}}, are designated as {{ill|World Heritage Sites|ja|世界遺産|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/World Heritage Sites|qq=}} and {{ill|Important Cultural Properties of Japan|ja|重要文化財|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)|2=ja|3=重要文化財|lt=Important Cultural Property|qq= (Japan)|4=ko|5=일본의_중요문화재|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Important Cultural Properties of Japan|13=User:Immanuelle/Important Cultural Property (Japan)|qq=}}.<ref>{{Cite web|title=World Cultural Heritage: Mt. Fuji Components|url=https://www.yamanashi-kankou.jp/heritage/fujisanwatcher/jp/world_heritage/index.html|publisher=Yamanashi Tourism Organization|access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Old Togawa Residence|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/185831|website=National Cultural Heritage Database|access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref>
== Cultural Depictions == * "Oshi Yagorō: Journey to Ise" by {{ill|Saijo Naka|ja|西條奈加|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saijo Naka|qq=}}, ISBN 439634015X * "{{ill|Sakuya-hime|ja|咲夜姫|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakuya-hime|qq=}}" by {{ill|Yamaguchi Kaito|ja|山口歌糸|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamaguchi Kaito|qq=}}, ISBN 4783880204
== References == <references /> == See Also == * {{ill|Hagaki|ja|羽書|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hagaki|qq=}} * {{ill|Okage-mairi|ja|お蔭参り|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okage-mairi|qq=}} * {{ill|Shukubō|ja|宿坊|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shukubō|qq=}} * {{ill|Accommodation facilities|ja|宿泊施設|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Accommodation facilities|qq=}} * {{ill|Ise Calendar|ja|伊勢暦|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Calendar|qq=}} * [http://www.nikko-annai.com/ Dōshabiki] - Edo-period guides for worshippers at {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|ja|日光東照宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nikkō Tōshō-gū|qq=}}

{{Otheruses|Shrine and temple guides|Music teachers for the emperor or imperial family|{{ill|御師 (音楽)|ja|御師 (音楽)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/御師 (音楽)|qq=}}}} [[File:Oshi 20190826a.jpg|thumb|Fuji-ko Guide (御師), photographed August 26, 2019]] '''Oshi''' (御師) refers to shrine priests associated with specific {{ill|shrines and temples|ja|社寺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/shrines and temples|qq=}}, who perform {{ill|prayers|ja|祈祷|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/prayers|qq=}}, guide worshippers, and assist pilgrims with visits and accommodations. In the case of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Grand Shrine|qq=}}, it was traditionally pronounced as "onshi." These guides often formed concentrated communities along highways, known as {{ill|Oshi towns|ja|御師町|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oshi towns|qq=}}.

== History == During the Heian period, notable examples of oshi included those from {{ill|Iwashimizu Hachiman Shrine|ja|石清水八幡宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwashimizu Hachiman Shrine|qq=}}, {{ill|Kamo shrines|lt=Kamo shrines|lt=Kamo Shrine|ja|賀茂神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamo Shrine|qq=}}, and {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|ja|日吉大社|1=Hiyoshi Taisha|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hiyoshi Taisha|13=User:Immanuelle/Hiyoshi Taisha|qq=}}, with the {{ill|Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Sanzan|qq=}} "Kumano Oshi" being particularly famous.<ref name="ushiyama">Yoshiyuki Ushiyama, "Faith and Culture in Medieval Ōhara," Shinshu University Bulletin of Education, Vol. 79 (1993), {{hdl|10091/1626}}</ref> The Kumano Oshi organized prayers, accommodations, and guided noble pilgrims during the late Heian period's {{ill|Kumano pilgrimage|ja|熊野詣|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano pilgrimage|qq=}}. Relationships between oshi and pilgrims evolved into stable "master-disciple" ties, known as {{ill|師檀関係|ja|師檀関係|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/師檀関係|qq=}}, encompassing even commoners by the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamakura period|qq=}}.
Between the Kamakura and {{ill|Muromachi periods|ja|室町時代|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muromachi periods|qq=}}, other prominent oshi groups emerged, such as those from {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Grand Shrine|qq=}}, {{ill|Fuji-ko|ja|富士講|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fuji-ko|qq=}}, {{ill|Matsunoo Taisha|lt=Matsunoo Taisha|lt=Matsuo Shrine|ja|松尾大社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsuo Shrine|qq=}}, {{ill|Mishima Taisha|ja|三嶋大社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mishima Taisha|qq=}}, and {{ill|Hakusan Shrine|ja|白山|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hakusan Shrine|qq=}}. Notably, records like the {{ill|Azuma Kagami|ja|吾妻鏡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Azuma Kagami|qq=}} document oshi from {{ill|Izumo-taisha|lt=Izumo-taisha|lt=Izumo Taisha|ja|出雲大社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumo Taisha|qq=}} serving {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Yoritomo|qq=}}.<ref>'Izumo Taisha Teacher Training Manual,' Izumo Taisha Headquarters, 1989, p. 319</ref>
During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Edo period|qq=}}, oshi played an essential role as intermediaries between commoners and shrine priests. Pilgrimages like {{ill|Okage-mairi|lt=Ise pilgrimage|ja|伊勢参り|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okage-mairi|qq=}} and those to {{ill|Mt. Fuji|ja|富士山|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mt. Fuji|qq=}} became widely accessible. Oshi organized {{ill|Kō (lecture)|ja|講|Kō (lecture)=Kō (lecture)|lt=kō (pilgrimage associations)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kō (lecture)|qq=}} and hosted pilgrims in their {{ill|Shukubo|ja|宿坊|Shukubo=Shukubo|lt=shukubō (pilgrim lodgings)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shukubo|qq=}}. The economic stability of the Edo period allowed pilgrimages to combine faith and leisure, enhancing oshi influence. However, groups like the Kumano Oshi declined alongside {{ill|Kumano faith|ja|熊野信仰|1=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Three Shrines|lt=Kumano faith|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano faith|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Three Shrines|qq=}}.
In Izumo, oshi structures laid the foundation for the {{ill|Izumo Taisha Faith|ja|出雲大社教|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumo Taisha Faith|qq=}}, creating facilities such as "tanjo" (mission centers) to support local preaching.<ref>'Izumo Taisha Teacher Training Manual,' Izumo Taisha Headquarters, 1989, p. 319</ref>
With the establishment of the {{ill|Meiji government|ja|明治政府|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji government|qq=}}'s {{ill|Shinto|ja|神祇|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto|qq=}} system, oshi rapidly declined. In 1869, oshi were barred from conducting {{ill|Shinto funerals|ja|神葬祭|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto funerals|qq=}}, and by 1871, the profession was abolished. Most oshi transitioned into farming or running inns. Some attempted to continue religious activities, forming organizations such as {{ill|Fusōkyō|ja|扶桑教|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fusōkyō|qq=}}.<ref>Takahiko Takano, "The Court and Religion in Early Modern Japan," Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2014, ISBN 978-4-642-03461-6, pp. 345-348.</ref>
=== Fuji Oshi === In the {{ill|Tenshōjingo Conflict|ja|天正壬午の乱|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenshōjingo Conflict|qq=}}, figures like Watanabe Shūgokuyū played significant roles. Notable Fuji Oshi residences, including {{ill|Old Togawa Residence|ja|旧外川家住宅|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Old Togawa Residence|qq=}} and {{ill|Kosano Residence|ja|小佐野家住宅|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kosano Residence|qq=}}, are designated as {{ill|World Heritage Sites|ja|世界遺産|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/World Heritage Sites|qq=}} and {{ill|Important Cultural Properties of Japan|ja|重要文化財|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)|2=ja|3=重要文化財|lt=Important Cultural Property|qq= (Japan)|4=ko|5=일본의_중요문화재|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Important Cultural Properties of Japan|13=User:Immanuelle/Important Cultural Property (Japan)|qq=}}.<ref>{{Cite web|title=World Cultural Heritage: Mt. Fuji Components|url=https://www.yamanashi-kankou.jp/heritage/fujisanwatcher/jp/world_heritage/index.html|publisher=Yamanashi Tourism Organization|access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Old Togawa Residence|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/185831|website=National Cultural Heritage Database|access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref>
== Cultural Depictions == * "Oshi Yagorō: Journey to Ise" by {{ill|Saijo Naka|ja|西條奈加|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saijo Naka|qq=}}, ISBN 439634015X * "{{ill|Sakuya-hime|ja|咲夜姫|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakuya-hime|qq=}}" by {{ill|Yamaguchi Kaito|ja|山口歌糸|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamaguchi Kaito|qq=}}, ISBN 4783880204
== References == <references /> == See Also == * {{ill|Hagaki|ja|羽書|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hagaki|qq=}} * {{ill|Okage-mairi|ja|お蔭参り|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okage-mairi|qq=}} * {{ill|Shukubō|ja|宿坊|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shukubō|qq=}} * {{ill|Accommodation facilities|ja|宿泊施設|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Accommodation facilities|qq=}} * {{ill|Ise Calendar|ja|伊勢暦|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Calendar|qq=}} * [http://www.nikko-annai.com/ Dōshabiki] - Edo-period guides for worshippers at {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|ja|日光東照宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nikkō Tōshō-gū|qq=}}

{{Otheruses|Shrine and temple guides|Music teachers for the emperor or imperial family|{{ill|御師 (音楽)|ja|御師 (音楽)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/御師 (音楽)|qq=}}}} [[File:Oshi 20190826a.jpg|thumb|Fuji-ko Guide (御師), photographed August 26, 2019]] '''Oshi''' (御師) refers to shrine priests associated with specific {{ill|shrines and temples|ja|社寺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/shrines and temples|qq=}}, who perform {{ill|prayers|ja|祈祷|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/prayers|qq=}}, guide worshippers, and assist pilgrims with visits and accommodations. In the case of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Grand Shrine|qq=}}, it was traditionally pronounced as "onshi." These guides often formed concentrated communities along highways, known as {{ill|Oshi towns|ja|御師町|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oshi towns|qq=}}.
== Overview == Originally an abbreviation for "御祈祷師" (prayer guides), the term "oshi" emerged during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heian period|qq=}}, initially referring to {{ill|temple monks|ja|社僧|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/temple monks|qq=}}. Later, it extended to include shrine priests who facilitated pilgrimages.

Pilgrimages like {{ill|Okage-mairi|lt=Ise pilgrimage|ja|伊勢参り|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okage-mairi|qq=}} and those to {{ill|Mt. Fuji|ja|富士山|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mt. Fuji|qq=}} became widely accessible. Oshi organized {{ill|Kō (lecture)|ja|講|Kō (lecture)=Kō (lecture)|lt=kō (pilgrimage associations)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kō (lecture)|qq=}} and hosted pilgrims in their {{ill|Shukubo|ja|宿坊|Shukubo=Shukubo|lt=shukubō (pilgrim lodgings)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shukubo|qq=}}. Notable Fuji Oshi residences, including {{ill|Old Togawa Residence|ja|旧外川家住宅|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Old Togawa Residence|qq=}} and {{ill|Kosano Residence|ja|小佐野家住宅|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kosano Residence|qq=}}, are designated as {{ill|World Heritage Sites|ja|世界遺産|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/World Heritage Sites|qq=}} and {{ill|Important Cultural Properties of Japan|ja|重要文化財|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)|2=ja|3=重要文化財|lt=Important Cultural Property|qq= (Japan)|4=ko|5=일본의_중요문화재|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Important Cultural Properties of Japan|13=User:Immanuelle/Important Cultural Property (Japan)|qq=}}.<ref>{{Cite web|title=World Cultural Heritage: Mt. Fuji Components|url=https://www.yamanashi-kankou.jp/heritage/fujisanwatcher/jp/world_heritage/index.html|publisher=Yamanashi Tourism Organization|access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Old Togawa Residence|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/185831|website=National Cultural Heritage Database|access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref>

Relationships between oshi and pilgrims evolved into stable "master-disciple" ties, known as {{ill|師檀関係|ja|師檀関係|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/師檀関係|qq=}}, encompassing even commoners by the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamakura period|qq=}}.
Between the Kamakura and {{ill|Muromachi periods|ja|室町時代|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muromachi periods|qq=}}, other prominent oshi groups emerged, such as those from {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Grand Shrine|qq=}}, {{ill|Fuji-ko|ja|富士講|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fuji-ko|qq=}}, {{ill|Matsunoo Taisha|lt=Matsunoo Taisha|lt=Matsuo Shrine|ja|松尾大社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsuo Shrine|qq=}}, {{ill|Mishima Taisha|ja|三嶋大社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mishima Taisha|qq=}}, and {{ill|Hakusan Shrine|ja|白山|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hakusan Shrine|qq=}}. Notably, records like the {{ill|Azuma Kagami|ja|吾妻鏡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Azuma Kagami|qq=}} document oshi from {{ill|Izumo-taisha|lt=Izumo-taisha|lt=Izumo Taisha|ja|出雲大社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumo Taisha|qq=}} serving {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Yoritomo|qq=}}.<ref>'Izumo Taisha Teacher Training Manual,' Izumo Taisha Headquarters, 1989, p. 319</ref>
During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Edo period|qq=}}, oshi played an essential role as intermediaries between commoners and shrine priests. Pilgrimages like {{ill|Okage-mairi|lt=Ise pilgrimage|ja|伊勢参り|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okage-mairi|qq=}} and those to {{ill|Mt. Fuji|ja|富士山|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mt. Fuji|qq=}} became widely accessible. Oshi organized {{ill|Kō (lecture)|ja|講|Kō (lecture)=Kō (lecture)|lt=kō (pilgrimage associations)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kō (lecture)|qq=}} and hosted pilgrims in their {{ill|Shukubo|ja|宿坊|Shukubo=Shukubo|lt=shukubō (pilgrim lodgings)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shukubo|qq=}}. The economic stability of the Edo period allowed pilgrimages to combine faith and leisure, enhancing oshi influence. However, groups like the Kumano Oshi declined alongside {{ill|Kumano faith|ja|熊野信仰|1=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Three Shrines|lt=Kumano faith|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano faith|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Three Shrines|qq=}}.
In Izumo, oshi structures laid the foundation for the {{ill|Izumo Taisha Faith|ja|出雲大社教|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumo Taisha Faith|qq=}}, creating facilities such as "tanjo" (mission centers) to support local preaching.<ref>'Izumo Taisha Teacher Training Manual,' Izumo Taisha Headquarters, 1989, p. 319</ref>
With the establishment of the {{ill|Meiji government|ja|明治政府|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji government|qq=}}'s {{ill|Shinto|ja|神祇|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto|qq=}} system, oshi rapidly declined. In 1869, oshi were barred from conducting {{ill|Shinto funerals|ja|神葬祭|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto funerals|qq=}}, and by 1871, the profession was abolished. Most oshi transitioned into farming or running inns. Some attempted to continue religious activities, forming organizations such as {{ill|Fusōkyō|ja|扶桑教|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fusōkyō|qq=}}.<ref>Takahiko Takano, "The Court and Religion in Early Modern Japan," Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2014, ISBN 978-4-642-03461-6, pp. 345-348.</ref>
=== Fuji Oshi === In the {{ill|Tenshōjingo Conflict|ja|天正壬午の乱|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenshōjingo Conflict|qq=}}, figures like Watanabe Shūgokuyū played significant roles. Notable Fuji Oshi residences, including {{ill|Old Togawa Residence|ja|旧外川家住宅|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Old Togawa Residence|qq=}} and {{ill|Kosano Residence|ja|小佐野家住宅|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kosano Residence|qq=}}, are designated as {{ill|World Heritage Sites|ja|世界遺産|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/World Heritage Sites|qq=}} and {{ill|Important Cultural Properties of Japan|ja|重要文化財|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)|2=ja|3=重要文化財|lt=Important Cultural Property|qq= (Japan)|4=ko|5=일본의_중요문화재|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Important Cultural Properties of Japan|13=User:Immanuelle/Important Cultural Property (Japan)|qq=}}.<ref>{{Cite web|title=World Cultural Heritage: Mt. Fuji Components|url=https://www.yamanashi-kankou.jp/heritage/fujisanwatcher/jp/world_heritage/index.html|publisher=Yamanashi Tourism Organization|access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Old Togawa Residence|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/185831|website=National Cultural Heritage Database|access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref>
== Cultural Depictions == * "Oshi Yagorō: Journey to Ise" by {{ill|Saijo Naka|ja|西條奈加|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saijo Naka|qq=}}, ISBN 439634015X * "{{ill|Sakuya-hime|ja|咲夜姫|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakuya-hime|qq=}}" by {{ill|Yamaguchi Kaito|ja|山口歌糸|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamaguchi Kaito|qq=}}, ISBN 4783880204
== References == <references /> == See Also == * {{ill|Hagaki|ja|羽書|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hagaki|qq=}} * {{ill|Okage-mairi|ja|お蔭参り|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okage-mairi|qq=}} * {{ill|Shukubō|ja|宿坊|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shukubō|qq=}} * {{ill|Accommodation facilities|ja|宿泊施設|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Accommodation facilities|qq=}} * {{ill|Ise Calendar|ja|伊勢暦|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Calendar|qq=}} * [http://www.nikko-annai.com/ Dōshabiki] - Edo-period guides for worshippers at {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|ja|日光東照宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nikkō Tōshō-gū|qq=}}

revision.text:
{{Otheruses|Shrine and temple guides|Music teachers for the emperor or imperial family|{{ill|御師 (音楽)|ja|御師 (音楽)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/御師 (音楽)|qq=}}}} [[File:Oshi 20190826a.jpg|thumb|Fuji-ko Guide (御師), photographed August 26, 2019]] '''Oshi''' (御師) refers to shrine priests associated with specific {{ill|Temples and Shrines|lt=Temples and Shrines|lt=Shrines and temples|ja|社寺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/shrines and temples|qq=}}, who perform {{ill|prayers|ja|祈祷|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/prayers|qq=}}, guide worshippers, and assist pilgrims with visits and accommodations. In the case of [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]], it was traditionally pronounced as "onshi." These guides often formed concentrated communities along highways, known as {{ill|Oshi towns|ja|御師町|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oshi towns|qq=}}.
== Overview == Originally an abbreviation for "御祈祷師" (prayer guides), the term "oshi" emerged during the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], initially referring to {{ill|temple monks|ja|社僧|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/temple monks|qq=}}. Later, it extended to include shrine priests who facilitated pilgrimages.
== History == During the Heian period, notable examples of oshi included those from [[:en:Iwashimizu Hachimangū|Iwashimizu Hachiman Shrine]], [[:en:Kamo shrines|Kamo Shrine]], and [[:en:Hiyoshi Taisha|Hiyoshi Taisha]], with the {{ill|Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Sanzan|qq=}} "Kumano Oshi" being particularly famous.<ref name="ushiyama">Yoshiyuki Ushiyama, "Faith and Culture in Medieval Ōhara," Shinshu University Bulletin of Education, Vol. 79 (1993), {{hdl|10091/1626}}</ref> The Kumano Oshi organized prayers, accommodations, and guided noble pilgrims during the late Heian period's {{ill|Kumano pilgrimage|ja|熊野詣|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano pilgrimage|qq=}}. Relationships between oshi and pilgrims evolved into stable "master-disciple" ties, known as {{ill|師檀関係|ja|師檀関係|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/師檀関係|qq=}}, encompassing even commoners by the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]].
Between the Kamakura and [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi periods]], other prominent oshi groups emerged, such as those from [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]], [[:en:Fujiko (religion)|Fuji-ko]], [[:en:Matsunoo Taisha|Matsuo Shrine]], [[:en:Mishima Taisha|Mishima Taisha]], and [[:en:Mount Haku|Hakusan Shrine]]. Notably, records like the [[:en:Azuma Kagami|Azuma Kagami]] document oshi from [[:en:Izumo-taisha|Izumo Taisha]] serving [[:en:Minamoto no Yoritomo|Minamoto no Yoritomo]].<ref>'Izumo Taisha Teacher Training Manual,' Izumo Taisha Headquarters, 1989, p. 319</ref>
During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], oshi played an essential role as intermediaries between commoners and shrine priests. Pilgrimages like {{ill|Okage-mairi|lt=Ise pilgrimage|ja|伊勢参り|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okage-mairi|qq=}} and those to [[:en:Mount Fuji|Mt. Fuji]] became widely accessible. Oshi organized [[:en:Kō (lecture)|kō (pilgrimage associations)]] and hosted pilgrims in their [[:en:Shukubo|shukubō (pilgrim lodgings)]]. The economic stability of the Edo period allowed pilgrimages to combine faith and leisure, enhancing oshi influence. However, groups like the Kumano Oshi declined alongside {{ill|Kumano faith|ja|熊野信仰|1=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Three Shrines|lt=Kumano faith|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano faith|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Three Shrines|qq=}}.
In Izumo, oshi structures laid the foundation for the [[:en:Izumo-taishakyo|Izumo Taisha Faith]], creating facilities such as "tanjo" (mission centers) to support local preaching.<ref>'Izumo Taisha Teacher Training Manual,' Izumo Taisha Headquarters, 1989, p. 319</ref>
With the establishment of the {{ill|Meiji government|ja|明治政府|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji government|qq=}}'s {{ill|Shinto|ja|神祇|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto|qq=}} system, oshi rapidly declined. In 1869, oshi were barred from conducting {{ill|Shinto funerals|ja|神葬祭|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto funerals|qq=}}, and by 1871, the profession was abolished. Most oshi transitioned into farming or running inns. Some attempted to continue religious activities, forming organizations such as {{ill|Fusōkyō|ja|扶桑教|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fusōkyō|qq=}}.<ref>Takahiko Takano, "The Court and Religion in Early Modern Japan," Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2014, ISBN 978-4-642-03461-6, pp. 345-348.</ref>
=== Fuji Oshi === In the {{ill|Tenshōjingo Conflict|ja|天正壬午の乱|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenshōjingo Conflict|qq=}}, figures like Watanabe Shūgokuyū played significant roles. Notable Fuji Oshi residences, including {{ill|Old Togawa Residence|ja|旧外川家住宅|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Old Togawa Residence|qq=}} and {{ill|Kosano Residence|ja|小佐野家住宅|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kosano Residence|qq=}}, are designated as [[:en:World Heritage Site|World Heritage Sites]] and [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]].<ref>{{Cite web|title=World Cultural Heritage: Mt. Fuji Components|url=https://www.yamanashi-kankou.jp/heritage/fujisanwatcher/jp/world_heritage/index.html|publisher=Yamanashi Tourism Organization|access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Old Togawa Residence|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/185831|website=National Cultural Heritage Database|access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref>
== Cultural Depictions == * "Oshi Yagorō: Journey to Ise" by {{ill|Saijo Naka|ja|西條奈加|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saijo Naka|qq=}}, ISBN 439634015X * "{{ill|Sakuya-hime|ja|咲夜姫|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakuya-hime|qq=}}" by {{ill|Yamaguchi Kaito|ja|山口歌糸|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamaguchi Kaito|qq=}}, ISBN 4783880204
== References == <references /> == See Also == * {{ill|Hagaki|ja|羽書|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hagaki|qq=}} * {{ill|Okage-mairi|ja|お蔭参り|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okage-mairi|qq=}} * [[:en:Shukubo|Shukubō]] * {{ill|Accommodation facilities|ja|宿泊施設|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Accommodation facilities|qq=}} * {{ill|Ise Calendar|ja|伊勢暦|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Calendar|qq=}} * [http://www.nikko-annai.com/ Dōshabiki] - Edo-period guides for worshippers at [[:en:Nikkō Tōshō-gū|Nikkō Tōshō-gū]]

Fuji Components|url=https://www.yamanashi-kankou.jp/heritage/fujisanwatcher/jp/world_heritage/index.html|publisher=Yamanashi Tourism Organization|access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Old Togawa Residence|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/185831|website=National Cultural Heritage Database|access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref>

[[sn:Onshi]] [[ja:御師]] [[sn:nshi]] [[en:Onshi]]
<!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--> [[Category:Service occupations]]

{{Otheruses|Shrine and temple guides|Music teachers for the emperor or imperial family|{{ill|御師 (音楽)|ja|御師 (音楽)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/御師 (音楽)|qq=}}}} [[File:Oshi 20190826a.jpg|thumb|Fuji-ko Guide (御師), photographed August 26, 2019]] '''Oshi''' (御師) refers to shrine priests associated with specific {{ill|Temples and Shrines|lt=Temples and Shrines|lt=Shrines and temples|ja|社寺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/shrines and temples|qq=}}, who perform {{ill|prayers|ja|祈祷|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/prayers|qq=}}, guide worshippers, and assist pilgrims with visits and accommodations. In the case of [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]], it was traditionally pronounced as "onshi." These guides often formed concentrated communities along highways, known as {{ill|Oshi towns|ja|御師町|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oshi towns|qq=}}.
== Overview == Originally an abbreviation for "御祈祷師" (prayer guides), the term "oshi" emerged during the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], initially referring to {{ill|temple monks|ja|社僧|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/temple monks|qq=}}. Later, it extended to include shrine priests who facilitated pilgrimages.
== History == During the Heian period, notable examples of oshi included those from [[:en:Iwashimizu Hachimangū|Iwashimizu Hachiman Shrine]], [[:en:Kamo shrines|Kamo Shrine]], and [[:en:Hiyoshi Taisha|Hiyoshi Taisha]], with the {{ill|Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Sanzan|qq=}} "Kumano Oshi" being particularly famous.<ref name="ushiyama">Yoshiyuki Ushiyama, "Faith and Culture in Medieval Ōhara," Shinshu University Bulletin of Education, Vol. 79 (1993), {{hdl|10091/1626}}</ref> The Kumano Oshi organized prayers, accommodations, and guided noble pilgrims during the late Heian period's {{ill|Kumano pilgrimage|ja|熊野詣|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano pilgrimage|qq=}}. Relationships between oshi and pilgrims evolved into stable "master-disciple" ties, known as {{ill|師檀関係|ja|師檀関係|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/師檀関係|qq=}}, encompassing even commoners by the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]].
Between the Kamakura and [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi periods]], other prominent oshi groups emerged, such as those from [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]], [[:en:Fujiko (religion)|Fuji-ko]], [[:en:Matsunoo Taisha|Matsuo Shrine]], [[:en:Mishima Taisha|Mishima Taisha]], and [[:en:Mount Haku|Hakusan Shrine]]. Notably, records like the [[:en:Azuma Kagami|Azuma Kagami]] document oshi from [[:en:Izumo-taisha|Izumo Taisha]] serving [[:en:Minamoto no Yoritomo|Minamoto no Yoritomo]].<ref>'Izumo Taisha Teacher Training Manual,' Izumo Taisha Headquarters, 1989, p. 319</ref>
During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], oshi played an essential role as intermediaries between commoners and shrine priests. Pilgrimages like {{ill|Okage-mairi|lt=Ise pilgrimage|ja|伊勢参り|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okage-mairi|qq=}} and those to [[:en:Mount Fuji|Mt. Fuji]] became widely accessible. Oshi organized [[:en:Kō (lecture)|kō (pilgrimage associations)]] and hosted pilgrims in their [[:en:Shukubo|shukubō (pilgrim lodgings)]]. The economic stability of the Edo period allowed pilgrimages to combine faith and leisure, enhancing oshi influence. However, groups like the Kumano Oshi declined alongside {{ill|Kumano faith|ja|熊野信仰|1=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Three Shrines|lt=Kumano faith|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano faith|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Three Shrines|qq=}}.
In Izumo, oshi structures laid the foundation for the [[:en:Izumo-taishakyo|Izumo Taisha Faith]], creating facilities such as "tanjo" (mission centers) to support local preaching.<ref>'Izumo Taisha Teacher Training Manual,' Izumo Taisha Headquarters, 1989, p. 319</ref>
With the establishment of the {{ill|Meiji government|ja|明治政府|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji government|qq=}}'s {{ill|Shinto|ja|神祇|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto|qq=}} system, oshi rapidly declined. In 1869, oshi were barred from conducting {{ill|Shinto funerals|ja|神葬祭|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto funerals|qq=}}, and by 1871, the profession was abolished. Most oshi transitioned into farming or running inns. Some attempted to continue religious activities, forming organizations such as {{ill|Fusōkyō|ja|扶桑教|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fusōkyō|qq=}}.<ref>Takahiko Takano, "The Court and Religion in Early Modern Japan," Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2014, ISBN 978-4-642-03461-6, pp. 345-348.</ref>
=== Fuji Oshi === In the {{ill|Tenshōjingo Conflict|ja|天正壬午の乱|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenshōjingo Conflict|qq=}}, figures like Watanabe Shūgokuyū played significant roles. Notable Fuji Oshi residences, including {{ill|Old Togawa Residence|ja|旧外川家住宅|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Old Togawa Residence|qq=}} and {{ill|Kosano Residence|ja|小佐野家住宅|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kosano Residence|qq=}}, are designated as [[:en:World Heritage Site|World Heritage Sites]] and [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]].<ref>{{Cite web|title=World Cultural Heritage: Mt. Fuji Components|url=https://www.yamanashi-kankou.jp/heritage/fujisanwatcher/jp/world_heritage/index.html|publisher=Yamanashi Tourism Organization|access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Old Togawa Residence|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/185831|website=National Cultural Heritage Database|access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref>
== Cultural Depictions == * "Oshi Yagorō: Journey to Ise" by {{ill|Saijo Naka|ja|西條奈加|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saijo Naka|qq=}}, ISBN 439634015X * "{{ill|Sakuya-hime|ja|咲夜姫|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakuya-hime|qq=}}" by {{ill|Yamaguchi Kaito|ja|山口歌糸|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamaguchi Kaito|qq=}}, ISBN 4783880204
== References == <references /> == See Also == * {{ill|Hagaki|ja|羽書|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hagaki|qq=}} * {{ill|Okage-mairi|ja|お蔭参り|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okage-mairi|qq=}} * [[:en:Shukubo|Shukubō]] * {{ill|Accommodation facilities|ja|宿泊施設|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Accommodation facilities|qq=}} * {{ill|Ise Calendar|ja|伊勢暦|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Calendar|qq=}} * [http://www.nikko-annai.com/ Dōshabiki] - Edo-period guides for worshippers at [[:en:Nikkō Tōshō-gū|Nikkō Tōshō-gū]]

[[sn:Onshi]] [[ja:御師]] [[sn:nshi]] [[en:Onshi]]
<!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--> [[Category:Service occupations]] [[Category:Street culture]] [[Category:Buddhist titles]] [[Category:Ise Shrine]]

{{Otheruses|Shrine and temple guides|Music teachers for the emperor or imperial family|{{ill|御師 (音楽)|ja|御師 (音楽)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/御師 (音楽)|qq=}}}} [[File:Oshi 20190826a.jpg|thumb|Fuji-ko Guide (御師), photographed August 26, 2019]] '''Oshi''' (御師) refers to shrine priests associated with specific {{ill|Temples and Shrines|lt=Temples and Shrines|lt=Shrines and temples|ja|社寺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/shrines and temples|qq=}}, who perform {{ill|prayers|ja|祈祷|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/prayers|qq=}}, guide worshippers, and assist pilgrims with visits and accommodations. In the case of [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]], it was traditionally pronounced as "onshi." These guides often formed concentrated communities along highways, known as {{ill|Oshi towns|ja|御師町|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oshi towns|qq=}}.
== Overview == Originally an abbreviation for "御祈祷師" (prayer guides), the term "oshi" emerged during the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], initially referring to {{ill|temple monks|ja|社僧|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/temple monks|qq=}}. Later, it extended to include shrine priests who facilitated pilgrimages.
== History == During the Heian period, notable examples of oshi included those from [[:en:Iwashimizu Hachimangū|Iwashimizu Hachiman Shrine]], [[:en:Kamo shrines|Kamo Shrine]], and [[:en:Hiyoshi Taisha|Hiyoshi Taisha]], with the {{ill|Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Sanzan|qq=}} "Kumano Oshi" being particularly famous.<ref name="ushiyama">Yoshiyuki Ushiyama, "Faith and Culture in Medieval Ōhara," Shinshu University Bulletin of Education, Vol. 79 (1993), {{hdl|10091/1626}}</ref> The Kumano Oshi organized prayers, accommodations, and guided noble pilgrims during the late Heian period's {{ill|Kumano pilgrimage|ja|熊野詣|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano pilgrimage|qq=}}. Relationships between oshi and pilgrims evolved into stable "master-disciple" ties, known as {{ill|師檀関係|ja|師檀関係|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/師檀関係|qq=}}, encompassing even commoners by the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]].
Between the Kamakura and [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi periods]], other prominent oshi groups emerged, such as those from [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]], [[:en:Fujiko (religion)|Fuji-ko]], [[:en:Matsunoo Taisha|Matsuo Shrine]], [[:en:Mishima Taisha|Mishima Taisha]], and [[:en:Mount Haku|Hakusan Shrine]]. Notably, records like the [[:en:Azuma Kagami|Azuma Kagami]] document oshi from [[:en:Izumo-taisha|Izumo Taisha]] serving [[:en:Minamoto no Yoritomo|Minamoto no Yoritomo]].<ref>'Izumo Taisha Teacher Training Manual,' Izumo Taisha Headquarters, 1989, p. 319</ref>
During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], oshi played an essential role as intermediaries between commoners and shrine priests. Pilgrimages like {{ill|Okage-mairi|lt=Ise pilgrimage|ja|伊勢参り|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okage-mairi|qq=}} and those to [[:en:Mount Fuji|Mt. Fuji]] became widely accessible. Oshi organized [[:en:Kō (lecture)|kō (pilgrimage associations)]] and hosted pilgrims in their [[:en:Shukubo|shukubō (pilgrim lodgings)]]. The economic stability of the Edo period allowed pilgrimages to combine faith and leisure, enhancing oshi influence. However, groups like the Kumano Oshi declined alongside {{ill|Kumano faith|ja|熊野信仰|1=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Three Shrines|lt=Kumano faith|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano faith|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Three Shrines|qq=}}.
In Izumo, oshi structures laid the foundation for the [[:en:Izumo-taishakyo|Izumo Taisha Faith]], creating facilities such as "tanjo" (mission centers) to support local preaching.<ref>'Izumo Taisha Teacher Training Manual,' Izumo Taisha Headquarters, 1989, p. 319</ref>
With the establishment of the {{ill|Meiji government|ja|明治政府|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji government|qq=}}'s {{ill|Shinto|ja|神祇|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto|qq=}} system, oshi rapidly declined. In 1869, oshi were barred from conducting {{ill|Shinto funerals|ja|神葬祭|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto funerals|qq=}}, and by 1871, the profession was abolished. Most oshi transitioned into farming or running inns. Some attempted to continue religious activities, forming organizations such as {{ill|Fusōkyō|ja|扶桑教|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fusōkyō|qq=}}.<ref>Takahiko Takano, "The Court and Religion in Early Modern Japan," Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2014, ISBN 978-4-642-03461-6, pp. 345-348.</ref>
=== Fuji Oshi === In the {{ill|Tenshōjingo Conflict|ja|天正壬午の乱|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenshōjingo Conflict|qq=}}, figures like Watanabe Shūgokuyū played significant roles. Notable Fuji Oshi residences, including {{ill|Old Togawa Residence|ja|旧外川家住宅|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Old Togawa Residence|qq=}} and {{ill|Kosano Residence|ja|小佐野家住宅|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kosano Residence|qq=}}, are designated as [[:en:World Heritage Site|World Heritage Sites]] and [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]].<ref>{{Cite web|title=World Cultural Heritage: Mt. Fuji Components|url=https://www.yamanashi-kankou.jp/heritage/fujisanwatcher/jp/world_heritage/index.html|publisher=Yamanashi Tourism Organization|access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Old Togawa Residence|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/185831|website=National Cultural Heritage Database|access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref>
== Cultural Depictions == * "Oshi Yagorō: Journey to Ise" by {{ill|Saijo Naka|ja|西條奈加|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saijo Naka|qq=}}, ISBN 439634015X * "{{ill|Sakuya-hime|ja|咲夜姫|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakuya-hime|qq=}}" by {{ill|Yamaguchi Kaito|ja|山口歌糸|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamaguchi Kaito|qq=}}, ISBN 4783880204
== References == <references /> == See Also == * {{ill|Hagaki|ja|羽書|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hagaki|qq=}} * {{ill|Okage-mairi|ja|お蔭参り|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okage-mairi|qq=}} * [[:en:Shukubo|Shukubō]] * {{ill|Accommodation facilities|ja|宿泊施設|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Accommodation facilities|qq=}} * {{ill|Ise Calendar|ja|伊勢暦|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Calendar|qq=}} * [http://www.nikko-annai.com/ Dōshabiki] - Edo-period guides for worshippers at [[:en:Nikkō Tōshō-gū|Nikkō Tōshō-gū]]

In 1869, oshi were barred from conducting {{ill|Shinto funerals|ja|神葬祭|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto funerals|qq=}}, and by 1871, the profession was abolished. Most oshi transitioned into farming or running inns. Some attempted to continue religious activities, forming organizations such as {{ill|Fusōkyō|ja|扶桑教|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fusōkyō|qq=}}.<ref>Takahiko Takano, "The Court and Religion in Early Modern Japan," Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2014, ISBN 978-4-642-03461-6, pp. 345-348.</ref>
=== Fuji Oshi === In the {{ill|Tenshōjingo Conflict|ja|天正壬午の乱|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenshōjingo Conflict|qq=}}, figures like Watanabe Shūgokuyū played significant roles. Notable Fuji Oshi residences, including {{ill|Old Togawa Residence|ja|旧外川家住宅|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Old Togawa Residence|qq=}} and {{ill|Kosano Residence|ja|小佐野家住宅|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kosano Residence|qq=}}, are designated as [[:en:World Heritage Site|World Heritage Sites]] and [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]].<ref>{{Cite web|title=World Cultural Heritage: Mt. Fuji Components|url=https://www.yamanashi-kankou.jp/heritage/fujisanwatcher/jp/world_heritage/index.html|publisher=Yamanashi Tourism Organization|access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Old Togawa Residence|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/185831|website=National Cultural Heritage Database|access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref>
== Cultural Depictions == * "Oshi Yagorō: Journey to Ise" by {{ill|Saijo Naka|ja|西條奈加|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saijo Naka|qq=}}, ISBN 439634015X * "{{ill|Sakuya-hime|ja|咲夜姫|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakuya-hime|qq=}}" by {{ill|Yamaguchi Kaito|ja|山口歌糸|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamaguchi Kaito|qq=}}, ISBN 4783880204
== References == <references /> == See Also == * {{ill|Hagaki|ja|羽書|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hagaki|qq=}} * {{ill|Okage-mairi|ja|お蔭参り|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okage-mairi|qq=}} * [[:en:Shukubo|Shukubō]] * {{ill|Accommodation facilities|ja|宿泊施設|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Accommodation facilities|qq=}} * {{ill|Ise Calendar|ja|伊勢暦|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Calendar|qq=}} * [http://www.nikko-annai.com/ Dōshabiki] - Edo-period guides for worshippers at [[:en:Nikkō Tōshō-gū|Nikkō Tōshō-gū]]
