<!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--> [[Category:Service occupations]] [[Category:Street culture]] [[Category:Buddhist titles]] [[Category:Ise Shrine]] [[Category:Fuji worship]] [[Category:Travel]]
[[Category:Titles and Positions in Shintō]] [[Category:translated pages with valid en interwikis]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> <!--enwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{wikidata link|Q11488972}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Onshi]][[ja:御師]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11488972]] Wikidata name: Onshi [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:49, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Otheruses|Shrine and temple guides|Music teachers for the emperor or imperial family|{{ill|御師 (音楽)|ja|御師 (音楽)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/御師 (音楽)|qq=|1=御師 (音楽)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q48759541}}}} [[File:Oshi 20190826a.jpg|thumb|Fuji-ko Guide (御師), photographed August 26, 2019]] '''Oshi''' (御師) refers to shrine priests associated with specific {{ill|Temples and Shrines|lt=Temples and Shrines|lt=Shrines and temples|ja|社寺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/shrines and temples|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 寺社}}, who perform {{ill|prayers|ja|祈祷|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/prayers|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 祈り|1=prayer|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q40953}}, guide worshippers, and assist pilgrims with visits and accommodations. In the case of [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]], it was traditionally pronounced as "onshi." These guides often formed concentrated communities along highways, known as {{ill|Oshi towns|ja|御師町|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oshi towns|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
== Overview == Originally an abbreviation for "御祈祷師" (prayer guides), the term "oshi" emerged during the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], initially referring to {{ill|temple monks|ja|社僧|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/temple monks|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 別当寺|1=bettō-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11396755}}. Later, it extended to include shrine priests who facilitated pilgrimages.
== History == During the Heian period, notable examples of oshi included those from [[:en:Iwashimizu Hachimangū|Iwashimizu Hachiman Shrine]], [[:en:Kamo shrines|Kamo Shrine]], and [[:en:Hiyoshi Taisha|Hiyoshi Taisha]], with the {{ill|Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Sanzan|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} "Kumano Oshi" being particularly famous.<ref name="ushiyama">Yoshiyuki Ushiyama, "Faith and Culture in Medieval Ōhara," Shinshu University Bulletin of Education, Vol. 79 (1993), {{hdl|10091/1626}}</ref> The Kumano Oshi organized prayers, accommodations, and guided noble pilgrims during the late Heian period's {{ill|Kumano pilgrimage|ja|熊野詣|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano pilgrimage|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 熊野三山|1=Kumano Sanzan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10403700}}. Relationships between oshi and pilgrims evolved into stable "master-disciple" ties, known as {{ill|師檀関係|ja|師檀関係|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/師檀関係|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, encompassing even commoners by the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]].
Between the Kamakura and [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi periods]], other prominent oshi groups emerged, such as those from [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]], [[:en:Fujiko (religion)|Fuji-ko]], [[:en:Matsunoo Taisha|Matsuo Shrine]], [[:en:Mishima Taisha|Mishima Taisha]], and [[:en:Mount Haku|Hakusan Shrine]]. Notably, records like the [[:en:Azuma Kagami|Azuma Kagami]] document oshi from [[:en:Izumo-taisha|Izumo Taisha]] serving [[:en:Minamoto no Yoritomo|Minamoto no Yoritomo]].<ref>'Izumo Taisha Teacher Training Manual,' Izumo Taisha Headquarters, 1989, p. 319</ref>
During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], oshi played an essential role as intermediaries between commoners and shrine priests. Pilgrimages like {{ill|Okage-mairi|lt=Ise pilgrimage|ja|伊勢参り|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okage-mairi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to お蔭参り|1=Okage Mairi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11263449}} and those to [[:en:Mount Fuji|Mt. Fuji]] became widely accessible. Oshi organized [[:en:Kō (lecture)|kō (pilgrimage associations)]] and hosted pilgrims in their [[:en:Shukubo|shukubō (pilgrim lodgings)]]. The economic stability of the Edo period allowed pilgrimages to combine faith and leisure, enhancing oshi influence. However, groups like the Kumano Oshi declined alongside {{ill|Kumano faith|ja|熊野信仰|1=Kumano Faith|2=ja|3=熊野信仰|WD=Q135501741}}{{efn|The religion of [[Kumano Shrines]]|1=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Three Shrines|lt=Kumano faith|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano faith|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Three Shrines|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 熊野三山}}.
In Izumo, oshi structures laid the foundation for the [[:en:Izumo-taishakyo|Izumo Taisha Faith]], creating facilities such as "tanjo" (mission centers) to support local preaching.<ref>'Izumo Taisha Teacher Training Manual,' Izumo Taisha Headquarters, 1989, p. 319</ref>
With the establishment of the {{ill|Meiji government|ja|明治政府|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji government|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 明治維新|1=Meiji restoration|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8707}}'s {{ill|Shinto|ja|神祇|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天津神・国津神}} system, oshi rapidly declined. In 1869, oshi were barred from conducting {{ill|Shinto funerals|ja|神葬祭|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto funerals|qq=|1=Shinto funeral|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11590783}}, and by 1871, the profession was abolished. Most oshi transitioned into farming or running inns. Some attempted to continue religious activities, forming organizations such as {{ill|Fusōkyō|ja|扶桑教|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fusōkyō|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Fuso-kyo|WD=Q11497295}}.<ref>Takahiko Takano, "The Court and Religion in Early Modern Japan," Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2014, ISBN 978-4-642-03461-6, pp. 345-348.</ref>
=== Fuji Oshi === In the {{ill|Tenshōjingo Conflict|ja|天正壬午の乱|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenshōjingo Conflict|qq=|1=Battle of Tenshō-Jingo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10939819}}, figures like Watanabe Shūgokuyū played significant roles. Notable Fuji Oshi residences, including {{ill|Old Togawa Residence|ja|旧外川家住宅|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Old Togawa Residence|qq=|1=former house of the Togawa Family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q66089879}} and {{ill|Kosano Residence|ja|小佐野家住宅|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kosano Residence|qq=|1=house of the Osano Family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q66089882}}, are designated as [[:en:World Heritage Site|World Heritage Sites]] and [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]].<ref>{{Cite web|title=World Cultural Heritage: Mt. Fuji Components|url=https://www.yamanashi-kankou.jp/heritage/fujisanwatcher/jp/world_heritage/index.html|publisher=Yamanashi Tourism Organization|access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Old Togawa Residence|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/185831|website=National Cultural Heritage Database|access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref>
== Cultural Depictions == * "Oshi Yagorō: Journey to Ise" by {{ill|Saijo Naka|ja|西條奈加|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saijo Naka|qq=|1=Naka Saijō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11628806}}, ISBN 439634015X * "{{ill|Sakuya-hime|ja|咲夜姫|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakuya-hime|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}" by {{ill|Yamaguchi Kaito|ja|山口歌糸|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamaguchi Kaito|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, ISBN 4783880204
== References == <references /> == See Also == * {{ill|Hagaki|ja|羽書|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hagaki|qq=|1=hagaki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11610188}} * {{ill|Okage-mairi|ja|お蔭参り|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okage-mairi|qq=|1=Okage Mairi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11263449}} * [[:en:Shukubo|Shukubō]] * {{ill|Accommodation facilities|ja|宿泊施設|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Accommodation facilities|qq=|1=accommodation facility|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q48835818}} * {{ill|Ise Calendar|ja|伊勢暦|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Calendar|qq=}} * [http://www.nikko-annai.com/ Dōshabiki] - Edo-period guides for worshippers at [[:en:Nikkō Tōshō-gū|Nikkō Tōshō-gū]]

== Overview == Originally an abbreviation for "御祈祷師" (prayer guides), the term "oshi" emerged during the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], initially referring to {{ill|temple monks|ja|社僧|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/temple monks|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 別当寺|1=bettō-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11396755}}. Later, it extended to include shrine priests who facilitated pilgrimages.
== History == During the Heian period, notable examples of oshi included those from [[:en:Iwashimizu Hachimangū|Iwashimizu Hachiman Shrine]], [[:en:Kamo shrines|Kamo Shrine]], and [[:en:Hiyoshi Taisha|Hiyoshi Taisha]], with the {{ill|Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Sanzan|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} "Kumano Oshi" being particularly famous.<ref name="ushiyama">Yoshiyuki Ushiyama, "Faith and Culture in Medieval Ōhara," Shinshu University Bulletin of Education, Vol. 79 (1993), {{hdl|10091/1626}}</ref> The Kumano Oshi organized prayers, accommodations, and guided noble pilgrims during the late Heian period's {{ill|Kumano pilgrimage|ja|熊野詣|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano pilgrimage|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 熊野三山|1=Kumano Sanzan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10403700}}. Relationships between oshi and pilgrims evolved into stable "master-disciple" ties, known as {{ill|師檀関係|ja|師檀関係|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/師檀関係|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, encompassing even commoners by the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]].
Between the Kamakura and [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi periods]], other prominent oshi groups emerged, such as those from [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]], [[:en:Fujiko (religion)|Fuji-ko]], [[:en:Matsunoo Taisha|Matsuo Shrine]], [[:en:Mishima Taisha|Mishima Taisha]], and [[:en:Mount Haku|Hakusan Shrine]]. Notably, records like the [[:en:Azuma Kagami|Azuma Kagami]] document oshi from [[:en:Izumo-taisha|Izumo Taisha]] serving [[:en:Minamoto no Yoritomo|Minamoto no Yoritomo]].<ref>'Izumo Taisha Teacher Training Manual,' Izumo Taisha Headquarters, 1989, p. 319</ref>
During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], oshi played an essential role as intermediaries between commoners and shrine priests. Pilgrimages like {{ill|Okage-mairi|lt=Ise pilgrimage|ja|伊勢参り|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okage-mairi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to お蔭参り|1=Okage Mairi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11263449}} and those to [[:en:Mount Fuji|Mt. Fuji]] became widely accessible. Oshi organized [[:en:Kō (lecture)|kō (pilgrimage associations)]] and hosted pilgrims in their [[:en:Shukubo|shukubō (pilgrim lodgings)]]. The economic stability of the Edo period allowed pilgrimages to combine faith and leisure, enhancing oshi influence. However, groups like the Kumano Oshi declined alongside {{ill|Kumano faith|ja|熊野信仰|1=Kumano Faith|2=ja|3=熊野信仰|WD=Q135501741}}{{efn|The religion of [[Kumano Shrines]]|1=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Three Shrines|lt=Kumano faith|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano faith|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Three Shrines|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 熊野三山}}.
In Izumo, oshi structures laid the foundation for the [[:en:Izumo-taishakyo|Izumo Taisha Faith]], creating facilities such as "tanjo" (mission centers) to support local preaching.<ref>'Izumo Taisha Teacher Training Manual,' Izumo Taisha Headquarters, 1989, p. 319</ref>
With the establishment of the {{ill|Meiji government|ja|明治政府|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji government|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 明治維新|1=Meiji restoration|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8707}}'s {{ill|Shinto|ja|神祇|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天津神・国津神}} system, oshi rapidly declined. In 1869, oshi were barred from conducting {{ill|Shinto funerals|ja|神葬祭|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto funerals|1=Shinto funeral|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11590783}}, and by 1871, the profession was abolished. Most oshi transitioned into farming or running inns. Some attempted to continue religious activities, forming organizations such as {{ill|Fusōkyō|ja|扶桑教|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fusōkyō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Fuso-kyo|WD=Q11497295}}.<ref>Takahiko Takano, "The Court and Religion in Early Modern Japan," Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2014, ISBN 978-4-642-03461-6, pp. 345-348.</ref>
=== Fuji Oshi === In the {{ill|Tenshōjingo Conflict|ja|天正壬午の乱|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenshōjingo Conflict|1=Battle of Tenshō-Jingo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10939819}}, figures like Watanabe Shūgokuyū played significant roles. Notable Fuji Oshi residences, including {{ill|Old Togawa Residence|ja|旧外川家住宅|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Old Togawa Residence|1=former house of the Togawa Family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q66089879}} and {{ill|Kosano Residence|ja|小佐野家住宅|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kosano Residence|1=house of the Osano Family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q66089882}}, are designated as [[:en:World Heritage Site|World Heritage Sites]] and [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]].<ref>{{Cite web|title=World Cultural Heritage: Mt. Fuji Components|url=https://www.yamanashi-kankou.jp/heritage/fujisanwatcher/jp/world_heritage/index.html|publisher=Yamanashi Tourism Organization|access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Old Togawa Residence|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/185831|website=National Cultural Heritage Database|access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref>
== Cultural Depictions == * "Oshi Yagorō: Journey to Ise" by {{ill|Saijo Naka|ja|西條奈加|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saijo Naka|1=Naka Saijō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11628806}}, ISBN 439634015X * "{{ill|Sakuya-hime|ja|咲夜姫|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakuya-hime|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}" by {{ill|Yamaguchi Kaito|ja|山口歌糸|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamaguchi Kaito|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, ISBN 4783880204
== References == <references /> == See Also == * {{ill|Hagaki|ja|羽書|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hagaki|1=hagaki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11610188}} * {{ill|Okage-mairi|ja|お蔭参り|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okage-mairi|1=Okage Mairi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11263449}} * [[:en:Shukubo|Shukubō]] * {{ill|Accommodation facilities|ja|宿泊施設|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Accommodation facilities|1=accommodation facility|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q48835818}} * {{ill|Ise Calendar|ja|伊勢暦|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Calendar|qq=}} * [http://www.nikko-annai.com/ Dōshabiki] - Edo-period guides for worshippers at [[:en:Nikkō Tōshō-gū|Nikkō Tōshō-gū]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Onshi]][[ja:御師]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11488972]] Wikidata name: Onshi [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:49, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Otheruses|Shrine and temple guides|Music teachers for the emperor or imperial family|{{ill|御師 (音楽)|ja|御師 (音楽)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/御師 (音楽)|1=御師 (音楽)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q48759541}}}} [[File:Oshi 20190826a.jpg|thumb|Fuji-ko Guide (御師), photographed August 26, 2019]] '''Oshi''' (御師) refers to shrine priests associated with specific {{ill|Temples and Shrines|lt=Temples and Shrines|lt=Shrines and temples|ja|社寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/shrines and temples|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 寺社}}, who perform {{ill|prayers|ja|祈祷|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/prayers|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 祈り|1=prayer|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q40953}}, guide worshippers, and assist pilgrims with visits and accommodations. In the case of [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]], it was traditionally pronounced as "onshi." These guides often formed concentrated communities along highways, known as {{ill|Oshi towns|ja|御師町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oshi towns|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
== Overview == Originally an abbreviation for "御祈祷師" (prayer guides), the term "oshi" emerged during the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], initially referring to {{ill|temple monks|ja|社僧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/temple monks|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 別当寺|1=bettō-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11396755}}. Later, it extended to include shrine priests who facilitated pilgrimages.
== History == During the Heian period, notable examples of oshi included those from [[:en:Iwashimizu Hachimangū|Iwashimizu Hachiman Shrine]], [[:en:Kamo shrines|Kamo Shrine]], and [[:en:Hiyoshi Taisha|Hiyoshi Taisha]], with the {{ill|Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Sanzan|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} "Kumano Oshi" being particularly famous.<ref name="ushiyama">Yoshiyuki Ushiyama, "Faith and Culture in Medieval Ōhara," Shinshu University Bulletin of Education, Vol. 79 (1993), {{hdl|10091/1626}}</ref> The Kumano Oshi organized prayers, accommodations, and guided noble pilgrims during the late Heian period's {{ill|Kumano pilgrimage|ja|熊野詣|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano pilgrimage|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 熊野三山|1=Kumano Sanzan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10403700}}. Relationships between oshi and pilgrims evolved into stable "master-disciple" ties, known as {{ill|師檀関係|ja|師檀関係|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/師檀関係|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, encompassing even commoners by the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]].
Between the Kamakura and [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi periods]], other prominent oshi groups emerged, such as those from [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]], [[:en:Fujiko (religion)|Fuji-ko]], [[:en:Matsunoo Taisha|Matsuo Shrine]], [[:en:Mishima Taisha|Mishima Taisha]], and [[:en:Mount Haku|Hakusan Shrine]]. Notably, records like the [[:en:Azuma Kagami|Azuma Kagami]] document oshi from [[:en:Izumo-taisha|Izumo Taisha]] serving [[:en:Minamoto no Yoritomo|Minamoto no Yoritomo]].<ref>'Izumo Taisha Teacher Training Manual,' Izumo Taisha Headquarters, 1989, p. 319</ref>
During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], oshi played an essential role as intermediaries between commoners and shrine priests. Pilgrimages like {{ill|Okage-mairi|lt=Ise pilgrimage|ja|伊勢参り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okage-mairi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to お蔭参り|1=Okage Mairi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11263449}} and those to [[:en:Mount Fuji|Mt. Fuji]] became widely accessible. Oshi organized [[:en:Kō (lecture)|kō (pilgrimage associations)]] and hosted pilgrims in their [[:en:Shukubo|shukubō (pilgrim lodgings)]]. The economic stability of the Edo period allowed pilgrimages to combine faith and leisure, enhancing oshi influence. However, groups like the Kumano Oshi declined alongside {{ill|Kumano faith|ja|熊野信仰|1=Kumano Faith|2=ja|3=熊野信仰|WD=Q135501741}}{{efn|The religion of [[Kumano Shrines]]|1=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Three Shrines|lt=Kumano faith|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano faith|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Three Shrines|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 熊野三山}}.
In Izumo, oshi structures laid the foundation for the [[:en:Izumo-taishakyo|Izumo Taisha Faith]], creating facilities such as "tanjo" (mission centers) to support local preaching.<ref>'Izumo Taisha Teacher Training Manual,' Izumo Taisha Headquarters, 1989, p. 319</ref>
With the establishment of the {{ill|Meiji government|ja|明治政府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji government|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 明治維新|1=Meiji restoration|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8707}}'s {{ill|Shinto|ja|神祇|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天津神・国津神}} system, oshi rapidly declined. In 1869, oshi were barred from conducting {{ill|Shinto funerals|ja|神葬祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto funerals|1=Shinto funeral|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11590783}}, and by 1871, the profession was abolished. Most oshi transitioned into farming or running inns. Some attempted to continue religious activities, forming organizations such as {{ill|Fusōkyō|ja|扶桑教|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fusōkyō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Fuso-kyo|WD=Q11497295}}.<ref>Takahiko Takano, "The Court and Religion in Early Modern Japan," Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2014, ISBN 978-4-642-03461-6, pp. 345-348.</ref>
=== Fuji Oshi === In the {{ill|Tenshōjingo Conflict|ja|天正壬午の乱|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenshōjingo Conflict|1=Battle of Tenshō-Jingo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10939819}}, figures like Watanabe Shūgokuyū played significant roles. Notable Fuji Oshi residences, including {{ill|Old Togawa Residence|ja|旧外川家住宅|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Old Togawa Residence|1=former house of the Togawa Family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q66089879}} and {{ill|Kosano Residence|ja|小佐野家住宅|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kosano Residence|1=house of the Osano Family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q66089882}}, are designated as [[:en:World Heritage Site|World Heritage Sites]] and [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]].<ref>{{Cite web|title=World Cultural Heritage: Mt. Fuji Components|url=https://www.yamanashi-kankou.jp/heritage/fujisanwatcher/jp/world_heritage/index.html|publisher=Yamanashi Tourism Organization|access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Old Togawa Residence|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/185831|website=National Cultural Heritage Database|access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref>
== Cultural Depictions == * "Oshi Yagorō: Journey to Ise" by {{ill|Saijo Naka|ja|西條奈加|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saijo Naka|1=Naka Saijō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11628806}}, ISBN 439634015X * "{{ill|Sakuya-hime|ja|咲夜姫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakuya-hime|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}" by {{ill|Yamaguchi Kaito|ja|山口歌糸|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamaguchi Kaito|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, ISBN 4783880204

However, groups like the Kumano Oshi declined alongside {{ill|Kumano faith|ja|熊野信仰|1=Kumano Faith|2=ja|3=熊野信仰|WD=Q135501741}}{{efn|The religion of [[Kumano Shrines]]|1=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Three Shrines|lt=Kumano faith|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano faith|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Three Shrines|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 熊野三山}}.
In Izumo, oshi structures laid the foundation for the [[:en:Izumo-taishakyo|Izumo Taisha Faith]], creating facilities such as "tanjo" (mission centers) to support local preaching.<ref>'Izumo Taisha Teacher Training Manual,' Izumo Taisha Headquarters, 1989, p. 319</ref>
With the establishment of the {{ill|Meiji government|ja|明治政府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji government|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 明治維新|1=Meiji restoration|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8707}}'s {{ill|Shinto|ja|神祇|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天津神・国津神}} system, oshi rapidly declined. In 1869, oshi were barred from conducting {{ill|Shinto funerals|ja|神葬祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto funerals|1=Shinto funeral|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11590783}}, and by 1871, the profession was abolished. Most oshi transitioned into farming or running inns. Some attempted to continue religious activities, forming organizations such as {{ill|Fusōkyō|ja|扶桑教|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fusōkyō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Fuso-kyo|WD=Q11497295}}.<ref>Takahiko Takano, "The Court and Religion in Early Modern Japan," Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2014, ISBN 978-4-642-03461-6, pp. 345-348.</ref>
=== Fuji Oshi === In the {{ill|Tenshōjingo Conflict|ja|天正壬午の乱|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenshōjingo Conflict|1=Battle of Tenshō-Jingo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10939819}}, figures like Watanabe Shūgokuyū played significant roles. Notable Fuji Oshi residences, including {{ill|Old Togawa Residence|ja|旧外川家住宅|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Old Togawa Residence|1=former house of the Togawa Family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q66089879}} and {{ill|Kosano Residence|ja|小佐野家住宅|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kosano Residence|1=house of the Osano Family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q66089882}}, are designated as [[:en:World Heritage Site|World Heritage Sites]] and [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]].<ref>{{Cite web|title=World Cultural Heritage: Mt. Fuji Components|url=https://www.yamanashi-kankou.jp/heritage/fujisanwatcher/jp/world_heritage/index.html|publisher=Yamanashi Tourism Organization|access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Old Togawa Residence|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/185831|website=National Cultural Heritage Database|access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref>
== Cultural Depictions == * "Oshi Yagorō: Journey to Ise" by {{ill|Saijo Naka|ja|西條奈加|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saijo Naka|1=Naka Saijō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11628806}}, ISBN 439634015X * "{{ill|Sakuya-hime|ja|咲夜姫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakuya-hime|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}" by {{ill|Yamaguchi Kaito|ja|山口歌糸|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamaguchi Kaito|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, ISBN 4783880204
== References == <references /> == See Also == * {{ill|Hagaki|ja|羽書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hagaki|1=hagaki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11610188}} * {{ill|Okage-mairi|ja|お蔭参り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okage-mairi|1=Okage Mairi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11263449}} * [[:en:Shukubo|Shukubō]] * {{ill|Accommodation facilities|ja|宿泊施設|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Accommodation facilities|1=accommodation facility|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q48835818}} * {{ill|Ise Calendar|ja|伊勢暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Calendar|qq=}} * [http://www.nikko-annai.com/ Dōshabiki] - Edo-period guides for worshippers at [[:en:Nikkō Tōshō-gū|Nikkō Tōshō-gū]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Onshi]][[ja:御師]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11488972]] Wikidata name: Onshi [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:49, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Otheruses|Shrine and temple guides|Music teachers for the emperor or imperial family|{{ill|御師 (音楽)|ja|御師 (音楽)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/御師 (音楽)|1=御師 (音楽)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q48759541}}}} [[File:Oshi 20190826a.jpg|thumb|Fuji-ko Guide (御師), photographed August 26, 2019]] '''Oshi''' (御師) refers to shrine priests associated with specific {{ill|Temples and Shrines|lt=Temples and Shrines|lt=Shrines and temples|ja|社寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/shrines and temples|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 寺社}}, who perform {{ill|prayers|ja|祈祷|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/prayers|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 祈り|1=prayer|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q40953}}, guide worshippers, and assist pilgrims with visits and accommodations. In the case of [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]], it was traditionally pronounced as "onshi." These guides often formed concentrated communities along highways, known as {{ill|Oshi towns|ja|御師町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oshi towns|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
== Overview == Originally an abbreviation for "御祈祷師" (prayer guides), the term "oshi" emerged during the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], initially referring to {{ill|temple monks|ja|社僧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/temple monks|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 別当寺|1=bettō-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11396755}}. Later, it extended to include shrine priests who facilitated pilgrimages.
== History == During the Heian period, notable examples of oshi included those from [[:en:Iwashimizu Hachimangū|Iwashimizu Hachiman Shrine]], [[:en:Kamo shrines|Kamo Shrine]], and [[:en:Hiyoshi Taisha|Hiyoshi Taisha]], with the {{ill|Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Sanzan|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} "Kumano Oshi" being particularly famous.<ref name="ushiyama">Yoshiyuki Ushiyama, "Faith and Culture in Medieval Ōhara," Shinshu University Bulletin of Education, Vol. 79 (1993), {{hdl|10091/1626}}</ref> The Kumano Oshi organized prayers, accommodations, and guided noble pilgrims during the late Heian period's {{ill|Kumano pilgrimage|ja|熊野詣|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano pilgrimage|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 熊野三山|1=Kumano Sanzan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10403700}}. Relationships between oshi and pilgrims evolved into stable "master-disciple" ties, known as {{ill|師檀関係|ja|師檀関係|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/師檀関係|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, encompassing even commoners by the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]].
Between the Kamakura and [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi periods]], other prominent oshi groups emerged, such as those from [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]], [[:en:Fujiko (religion)|Fuji-ko]], [[:en:Matsunoo Taisha|Matsuo Shrine]], [[:en:Mishima Taisha|Mishima Taisha]], and [[:en:Mount Haku|Hakusan Shrine]]. Notably, records like the [[:en:Azuma Kagami|Azuma Kagami]] document oshi from [[:en:Izumo-taisha|Izumo Taisha]] serving [[:en:Minamoto no Yoritomo|Minamoto no Yoritomo]].<ref>'Izumo Taisha Teacher Training Manual,' Izumo Taisha Headquarters, 1989, p. 319</ref>
During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], oshi played an essential role as intermediaries between commoners and shrine priests. Pilgrimages like {{ill|Okage-mairi|lt=Ise pilgrimage|ja|伊勢参り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okage-mairi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to お蔭参り|1=Okage Mairi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11263449}} and those to [[:en:Mount Fuji|Mt. Fuji]] became widely accessible. Oshi organized [[:en:Kō (lecture)|kō (pilgrimage associations)]] and hosted pilgrims in their [[:en:Shukubo|shukubō (pilgrim lodgings)]]. The economic stability of the Edo period allowed pilgrimages to combine faith and leisure, enhancing oshi influence. However, groups like the Kumano Oshi declined alongside {{ill|Kumano faith|ja|熊野信仰|1=Kumano Faith|2=ja|3=熊野信仰|WD=Q135501741}}{{efn|The religion of [[Kumano Shrines]]|1=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Three Shrines|lt=Kumano faith|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano faith|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Three Shrines|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 熊野三山}}.
In Izumo, oshi structures laid the foundation for the [[:en:Izumo-taishakyo|Izumo Taisha Faith]], creating facilities such as "tanjo" (mission centers) to support local preaching.<ref>'Izumo Taisha Teacher Training Manual,' Izumo Taisha Headquarters, 1989, p. 319</ref>
With the establishment of the {{ill|Meiji government|ja|明治政府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji government|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 明治維新|1=Meiji restoration|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8707}}'s {{ill|Shinto|ja|神祇|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天津神・国津神}} system, oshi rapidly declined. In 1869, oshi were barred from conducting {{ill|Shinto funerals|ja|神葬祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto funerals|1=Shinto funeral|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11590783}}, and by 1871, the profession was abolished. Most oshi transitioned into farming or running inns. Some attempted to continue religious activities, forming organizations such as {{ill|Fusōkyō|ja|扶桑教|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fusōkyō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Fuso-kyo|WD=Q11497295}}.<ref>Takahiko Takano, "The Court and Religion in Early Modern Japan," Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2014, ISBN 978-4-642-03461-6, pp. 345-348.</ref>
=== Fuji Oshi === In the {{ill|Tenshōjingo Conflict|ja|天正壬午の乱|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenshōjingo Conflict|1=Battle of Tenshō-Jingo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10939819}}, figures like Watanabe Shūgokuyū played significant roles. Notable Fuji Oshi residences, including {{ill|Old Togawa Residence|ja|旧外川家住宅|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Old Togawa Residence|1=former house of the Togawa Family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q66089879}} and {{ill|Kosano Residence|ja|小佐野家住宅|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kosano Residence|1=house of the Osano Family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q66089882}}, are designated as [[:en:World Heritage Site|World Heritage Sites]] and [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]].<ref>{{Cite web|title=World Cultural Heritage: Mt. Fuji Components|url=https://www.yamanashi-kankou.jp/heritage/fujisanwatcher/jp/world_heritage/index.html|publisher=Yamanashi Tourism Organization|access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Old Togawa Residence|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/185831|website=National Cultural Heritage Database|access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref>
== Cultural Depictions == * "Oshi Yagorō: Journey to Ise" by {{ill|Saijo Naka|ja|西條奈加|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saijo Naka|1=Naka Saijō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11628806}}, ISBN 439634015X * "{{ill|Sakuya-hime|ja|咲夜姫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakuya-hime|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}" by {{ill|Yamaguchi Kaito|ja|山口歌糸|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamaguchi Kaito|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, ISBN 4783880204
== References == <references /> == See Also == * {{ill|Hagaki|ja|羽書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hagaki|1=hagaki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11610188}} * {{ill|Okage-mairi|ja|お蔭参り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okage-mairi|1=Okage Mairi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11263449}} * [[:en:Shukubo|Shukubō]] * {{ill|Accommodation facilities|ja|宿泊施設|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Accommodation facilities|1=accommodation facility|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q48835818}} * {{ill|Ise Calendar|ja|伊勢暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Calendar|qq=}} * [http://www.nikko-annai.com/ Dōshabiki] - Edo-period guides for worshippers at [[:en:Nikkō Tōshō-gū|Nikkō Tōshō-gū]]

Fuji]] became widely accessible. Oshi organized [[:en:Kō (lecture)|kō (pilgrimage associations)]] and hosted pilgrims in their [[:en:Shukubo|shukubō (pilgrim lodgings)]]. === Fuji Oshi === In the {{ill|Tenshōjingo Conflict|ja|天正壬午の乱|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenshōjingo Conflict|1=Battle of Tenshō-Jingo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10939819}}, figures like Watanabe Shūgokuyū played significant roles. Notable Fuji Oshi residences, including {{ill|Old Togawa Residence|ja|旧外川家住宅|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Old Togawa Residence|1=former house of the Togawa Family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q66089879}} and {{ill|Kosano Residence|ja|小佐野家住宅|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kosano Residence|1=house of the Osano Family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q66089882}}, are designated as [[:en:World Heritage Site|World Heritage Sites]] and [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]].<ref>{{Cite web|title=World Cultural Heritage: Mt. Fuji Components|url=https://www.yamanashi-kankou.jp/heritage/fujisanwatcher/jp/world_heritage/index.html|publisher=Yamanashi Tourism Organization|access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Old Togawa Residence|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/185831|website=National Cultural Heritage Database|access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref>
== Cultural Depictions == * "Oshi Yagorō: Journey to Ise" by {{ill|Saijo Naka|ja|西條奈加|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saijo Naka|1=Naka Saijō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11628806}}, ISBN 439634015X * "{{ill|Sakuya-hime|ja|咲夜姫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakuya-hime|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}" by {{ill|Yamaguchi Kaito|ja|山口歌糸|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamaguchi Kaito|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, ISBN 4783880204

<!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--> [[Category:Service occupations]] [[Category:Street culture]] [[Category:Buddhist titles]] [[Category:Ise Shrine]] [[Category:Fuji worship]] [[Category:Travel]]
[[Category:Titles and Positions in Shintō]] [[Category:translated pages with valid en interwikis]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> <!--enwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{wikidata link|Q11488972}} [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Onshi]][[ja:御師]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11488972]] Wikidata name: Onshi [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:49, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Otheruses|Shrine and temple guides|Music teachers for the emperor or imperial family|{{ill|御師 (音楽)|ja|御師 (音楽)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/御師 (音楽)|1=御師 (音楽)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q48759541}}}} [[File:Oshi 20190826a.jpg|thumb|Fuji-ko Guide (御師), photographed August 26, 2019]] '''Oshi''' (御師) refers to shrine priests associated with specific {{ill|Temples and Shrines|lt=Temples and Shrines|lt=Shrines and temples|ja|社寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/shrines and temples|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 寺社}}, who perform {{ill|prayers|ja|祈祷|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/prayers|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 祈り|1=prayer|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q40953}}, guide worshippers, and assist pilgrims with visits and accommodations. In the case of [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]], it was traditionally pronounced as "onshi." These guides often formed concentrated communities along highways, known as {{ill|Oshi towns|ja|御師町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oshi towns|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
== Overview == Originally an abbreviation for "御祈祷師" (prayer guides), the term "oshi" emerged during the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], initially referring to {{ill|temple monks|ja|社僧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/temple monks|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 別当寺|1=bettō-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11396755}}. Later, it extended to include shrine priests who facilitated pilgrimages.
== History == During the Heian period, notable examples of oshi included those from [[:en:Iwashimizu Hachimangū|Iwashimizu Hachiman Shrine]], [[:en:Kamo shrines|Kamo Shrine]], and [[:en:Hiyoshi Taisha|Hiyoshi Taisha]], with the {{ill|Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Sanzan|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} "Kumano Oshi" being particularly famous.<ref name="ushiyama">Yoshiyuki Ushiyama, "Faith and Culture in Medieval Ōhara," Shinshu University Bulletin of Education, Vol. 79 (1993), {{hdl|10091/1626}}</ref> The Kumano Oshi organized prayers, accommodations, and guided noble pilgrims during the late Heian period's {{ill|Kumano pilgrimage|ja|熊野詣|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano pilgrimage|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 熊野三山|1=Kumano Sanzan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10403700}}. Relationships between oshi and pilgrims evolved into stable "master-disciple" ties, known as {{ill|師檀関係|ja|師檀関係|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/師檀関係|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, encompassing even commoners by the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]].
Between the Kamakura and [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi periods]], other prominent oshi groups emerged, such as those from [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]], [[:en:Fujiko (religion)|Fuji-ko]], [[:en:Matsunoo Taisha|Matsuo Shrine]], [[:en:Mishima Taisha|Mishima Taisha]], and [[:en:Mount Haku|Hakusan Shrine]]. Notably, records like the [[:en:Azuma Kagami|Azuma Kagami]] document oshi from [[:en:Izumo-taisha|Izumo Taisha]] serving [[:en:Minamoto no Yoritomo|Minamoto no Yoritomo]].<ref>'Izumo Taisha Teacher Training Manual,' Izumo Taisha Headquarters, 1989, p. 319</ref>
During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], oshi played an essential role as intermediaries between commoners and shrine priests. Pilgrimages like {{ill|Okage-mairi|lt=Ise pilgrimage|ja|伊勢参り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okage-mairi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to お蔭参り|1=Okage Mairi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11263449}} and those to [[:en:Mount Fuji|Mt. Fuji]] became widely accessible. Oshi organized [[:en:Kō (lecture)|kō (pilgrimage associations)]] and hosted pilgrims in their [[:en:Shukubo|shukubō (pilgrim lodgings)]]. The economic stability of the Edo period allowed pilgrimages to combine faith and leisure, enhancing oshi influence. However, groups like the Kumano Oshi declined alongside {{ill|Kumano faith|ja|熊野信仰|1=Kumano Faith|2=ja|3=熊野信仰|WD=Q135501741}}{{efn|The religion of [[Kumano Shrines]]|1=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Three Shrines|lt=Kumano faith|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano faith|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Three Shrines|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 熊野三山}}.
In Izumo, oshi structures laid the foundation for the [[:en:Izumo-taishakyo|Izumo Taisha Faith]], creating facilities such as "tanjo" (mission centers) to support local preaching.<ref>'Izumo Taisha Teacher Training Manual,' Izumo Taisha Headquarters, 1989, p. 319</ref>
With the establishment of the {{ill|Meiji government|ja|明治政府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji government|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 明治維新|1=Meiji restoration|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8707}}'s {{ill|Shinto|ja|神祇|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天津神・国津神}} system, oshi rapidly declined. In 1869, oshi were barred from conducting {{ill|Shinto funerals|ja|神葬祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto funerals|1=Shinto funeral|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11590783}}, and by 1871, the profession was abolished. Most oshi transitioned into farming or running inns. Some attempted to continue religious activities, forming organizations such as {{ill|Fusōkyō|ja|扶桑教|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fusōkyō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Fuso-kyo|WD=Q11497295}}.<ref>Takahiko Takano, "The Court and Religion in Early Modern Japan," Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2014, ISBN 978-4-642-03461-6, pp. 345-348.</ref>
=== Fuji Oshi === In the {{ill|Tenshōjingo Conflict|ja|天正壬午の乱|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenshōjingo Conflict|1=Battle of Tenshō-Jingo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10939819}}, figures like Watanabe Shūgokuyū played significant roles. Notable Fuji Oshi residences, including {{ill|Old Togawa Residence|ja|旧外川家住宅|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Old Togawa Residence|1=former house of the Togawa Family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q66089879}} and {{ill|Kosano Residence|ja|小佐野家住宅|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kosano Residence|1=house of the Osano Family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q66089882}}, are designated as [[:en:World Heritage Site|World Heritage Sites]] and [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]].<ref>{{Cite web|title=World Cultural Heritage: Mt. Fuji Components|url=https://www.yamanashi-kankou.jp/heritage/fujisanwatcher/jp/world_heritage/index.html|publisher=Yamanashi Tourism Organization|access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Old Togawa Residence|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/185831|website=National Cultural Heritage Database|access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref>
== Cultural Depictions == * "Oshi Yagorō: Journey to Ise" by {{ill|Saijo Naka|ja|西條奈加|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saijo Naka|1=Naka Saijō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11628806}}, ISBN 439634015X * "{{ill|Sakuya-hime|ja|咲夜姫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakuya-hime|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}" by {{ill|Yamaguchi Kaito|ja|山口歌糸|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamaguchi Kaito|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, ISBN 4783880204

Fuji Components|url=https://www.yamanashi-kankou.jp/heritage/fujisanwatcher/jp/world_heritage/index.html|publisher=Yamanashi Tourism Organization|access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Old Togawa Residence|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/185831|website=National Cultural Heritage Database|access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Onshi]][[ja:御師]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11488972]] Wikidata name: Onshi [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:49, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Otheruses|Shrine and temple guides|Music teachers for the emperor or imperial family|{{ill|御師 (音楽)|ja|御師 (音楽)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/御師 (音楽)|1=御師 (音楽)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q48759541}}}} [[File:Oshi 20190826a.jpg|thumb|Fuji-ko Guide (御師), photographed August 26, 2019]] '''Oshi''' (御師) refers to shrine priests associated with specific {{ill|Temples and Shrines|lt=Temples and Shrines|lt=Shrines and temples|ja|社寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/shrines and temples|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 寺社}}, who perform {{ill|prayers|ja|祈祷|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/prayers|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 祈り|1=prayer|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q40953}}, guide worshippers, and assist pilgrims with visits and accommodations. In the case of [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]], it was traditionally pronounced as "onshi." These guides often formed concentrated communities along highways, known as {{ill|Oshi towns|ja|御師町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oshi towns|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
== Overview == Originally an abbreviation for "御祈祷師" (prayer guides), the term "oshi" emerged during the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], initially referring to {{ill|temple monks|ja|社僧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/temple monks|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 別当寺|1=bettō-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11396755}}. Later, it extended to include shrine priests who facilitated pilgrimages.
== History == During the Heian period, notable examples of oshi included those from [[:en:Iwashimizu Hachimangū|Iwashimizu Hachiman Shrine]], [[:en:Kamo shrines|Kamo Shrine]], and [[:en:Hiyoshi Taisha|Hiyoshi Taisha]], with the {{ill|Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Sanzan|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} "Kumano Oshi" being particularly famous.<ref name="ushiyama">Yoshiyuki Ushiyama, "Faith and Culture in Medieval Ōhara," Shinshu University Bulletin of Education, Vol. 79 (1993), {{hdl|10091/1626}}</ref> The Kumano Oshi organized prayers, accommodations, and guided noble pilgrims during the late Heian period's {{ill|Kumano pilgrimage|ja|熊野詣|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano pilgrimage|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 熊野三山|1=Kumano Sanzan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10403700}}. Relationships between oshi and pilgrims evolved into stable "master-disciple" ties, known as {{ill|師檀関係|ja|師檀関係|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/師檀関係|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, encompassing even commoners by the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]].
Between the Kamakura and [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi periods]], other prominent oshi groups emerged, such as those from [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]], [[:en:Fujiko (religion)|Fuji-ko]], [[:en:Matsunoo Taisha|Matsuo Shrine]], [[:en:Mishima Taisha|Mishima Taisha]], and [[:en:Mount Haku|Hakusan Shrine]]. Notably, records like the [[:en:Azuma Kagami|Azuma Kagami]] document oshi from [[:en:Izumo-taisha|Izumo Taisha]] serving [[:en:Minamoto no Yoritomo|Minamoto no Yoritomo]].<ref>'Izumo Taisha Teacher Training Manual,' Izumo Taisha Headquarters, 1989, p. 319</ref>
During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], oshi played an essential role as intermediaries between commoners and shrine priests. Pilgrimages like {{ill|Okage-mairi|lt=Ise pilgrimage|ja|伊勢参り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okage-mairi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to お蔭参り|1=Okage Mairi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11263449}} and those to [[:en:Mount Fuji|Mt. Fuji]] became widely accessible. Oshi organized [[:en:Kō (lecture)|kō (pilgrimage associations)]] and hosted pilgrims in their [[:en:Shukubo|shukubō (pilgrim lodgings)]]. The economic stability of the Edo period allowed pilgrimages to combine faith and leisure, enhancing oshi influence. However, groups like the Kumano Oshi declined alongside {{ill|Kumano faith|ja|熊野信仰|1=Kumano Faith|2=ja|3=熊野信仰|WD=Q135501741}}{{efn|The religion of [[Kumano Shrines]]|1=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Three Shrines|lt=Kumano faith|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano faith|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Three Shrines|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 熊野三山}}.
In Izumo, oshi structures laid the foundation for the [[:en:Izumo-taishakyo|Izumo Taisha Faith]], creating facilities such as "tanjo" (mission centers) to support local preaching.<ref>'Izumo Taisha Teacher Training Manual,' Izumo Taisha Headquarters, 1989, p. 319</ref>
With the establishment of the {{ill|Meiji government|ja|明治政府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji government|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 明治維新|1=Meiji restoration|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8707}}'s {{ill|Shinto|ja|神祇|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天津神・国津神}} system, oshi rapidly declined. In 1869, oshi were barred from conducting {{ill|Shinto funerals|ja|神葬祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto funerals|1=Shinto funeral|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11590783}}, and by 1871, the profession was abolished. Most oshi transitioned into farming or running inns. Some attempted to continue religious activities, forming organizations such as {{ill|Fusōkyō|ja|扶桑教|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fusōkyō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Fuso-kyo|WD=Q11497295}}.<ref>Takahiko Takano, "The Court and Religion in Early Modern Japan," Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2014, ISBN 978-4-642-03461-6, pp. 345-348.</ref>
=== Fuji Oshi === In the {{ill|Tenshōjingo Conflict|ja|天正壬午の乱|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenshōjingo Conflict|1=Battle of Tenshō-Jingo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10939819}}, figures like Watanabe Shūgokuyū played significant roles. Notable Fuji Oshi residences, including {{ill|Old Togawa Residence|ja|旧外川家住宅|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Old Togawa Residence|1=former house of the Togawa Family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q66089879}} and {{ill|Kosano Residence|ja|小佐野家住宅|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kosano Residence|1=house of the Osano Family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q66089882}}, are designated as [[:en:World Heritage Site|World Heritage Sites]] and [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]].<ref>{{Cite web|title=World Cultural Heritage: Mt. Fuji Components|url=https://www.yamanashi-kankou.jp/heritage/fujisanwatcher/jp/world_heritage/index.html|publisher=Yamanashi Tourism Organization|access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Old Togawa Residence|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/185831|website=National Cultural Heritage Database|access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref>
== Cultural Depictions == * "Oshi Yagorō: Journey to Ise" by {{ill|Saijo Naka|ja|西條奈加|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saijo Naka|1=Naka Saijō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11628806}}, ISBN 439634015X * "{{ill|Sakuya-hime|ja|咲夜姫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakuya-hime|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}" by {{ill|Yamaguchi Kaito|ja|山口歌糸|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamaguchi Kaito|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, ISBN 4783880204
== References == <references /> == See Also == * {{ill|Hagaki|ja|羽書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hagaki|1=hagaki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11610188}} * {{ill|Okage-mairi|ja|お蔭参り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okage-mairi|1=Okage Mairi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11263449}} * [[:en:Shukubo|Shukubō]] * {{ill|Accommodation facilities|ja|宿泊施設|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Accommodation facilities|1=accommodation facility|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q48835818}} * {{ill|Ise Calendar|ja|伊勢暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Calendar|qq=}} * [http://www.nikko-annai.com/ Dōshabiki] - Edo-period guides for worshippers at [[:en:Nikkō Tōshō-gū|Nikkō Tōshō-gū]]

== Overview == Originally an abbreviation for "御祈祷師" (prayer guides), the term "oshi" emerged during the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], initially referring to {{ill|temple monks|ja|社僧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/temple monks|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 別当寺|1=bettō-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11396755}}. Later, it extended to include shrine priests who facilitated pilgrimages.
== History == During the Heian period, notable examples of oshi included those from [[:en:Iwashimizu Hachimangū|Iwashimizu Hachiman Shrine]], [[:en:Kamo shrines|Kamo Shrine]], and [[:en:Hiyoshi Taisha|Hiyoshi Taisha]], with the {{ill|Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Sanzan|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} "Kumano Oshi" being particularly famous.<ref name="ushiyama">Yoshiyuki Ushiyama, "Faith and Culture in Medieval Ōhara," Shinshu University Bulletin of Education, Vol. 79 (1993), {{hdl|10091/1626}}</ref> The Kumano Oshi organized prayers, accommodations, and guided noble pilgrims during the late Heian period's {{ill|Kumano pilgrimage|ja|熊野詣|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano pilgrimage|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 熊野三山|1=Kumano Sanzan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10403700}}. Relationships between oshi and pilgrims evolved into stable "master-disciple" ties, known as {{ill|師檀関係|ja|師檀関係|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/師檀関係|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, encompassing even commoners by the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]].
Between the Kamakura and [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi periods]], other prominent oshi groups emerged, such as those from [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]], [[:en:Fujiko (religion)|Fuji-ko]], [[:en:Matsunoo Taisha|Matsuo Shrine]], [[:en:Mishima Taisha|Mishima Taisha]], and [[:en:Mount Haku|Hakusan Shrine]]. Notably, records like the [[:en:Azuma Kagami|Azuma Kagami]] document oshi from [[:en:Izumo-taisha|Izumo Taisha]] serving [[:en:Minamoto no Yoritomo|Minamoto no Yoritomo]].<ref>'Izumo Taisha Teacher Training Manual,' Izumo Taisha Headquarters, 1989, p. 319</ref>
During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], oshi played an essential role as intermediaries between commoners and shrine priests. Pilgrimages like {{ill|Okage-mairi|lt=Ise pilgrimage|ja|伊勢参り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okage-mairi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to お蔭参り|1=Okage Mairi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11263449}} and those to [[:en:Mount Fuji|Mt. Fuji]] became widely accessible. Oshi organized [[:en:Kō (lecture)|kō (pilgrimage associations)]] and hosted pilgrims in their [[:en:Shukubo|shukubō (pilgrim lodgings)]]. The economic stability of the Edo period allowed pilgrimages to combine faith and leisure, enhancing oshi influence. However, groups like the Kumano Oshi declined alongside {{ill|Kumano faith|ja|熊野信仰|1=Kumano Faith|2=ja|3=熊野信仰|WD=Q135501741}}{{efn|The religion of [[Kumano Shrines]]|1=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Three Shrines|lt=Kumano faith|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano faith|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Three Shrines|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 熊野三山}}.
In Izumo, oshi structures laid the foundation for the [[:en:Izumo-taishakyo|Izumo Taisha Faith]], creating facilities such as "tanjo" (mission centers) to support local preaching.<ref>'Izumo Taisha Teacher Training Manual,' Izumo Taisha Headquarters, 1989, p. 319</ref>
With the establishment of the {{ill|Meiji government|ja|明治政府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji government|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 明治維新|1=Meiji restoration|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8707}}'s {{ill|Shinto|ja|神祇|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天津神・国津神}} system, oshi rapidly declined. In 1869, oshi were barred from conducting {{ill|Shinto funerals|ja|神葬祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto funerals|1=Shinto funeral|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11590783}}, and by 1871, the profession was abolished. Most oshi transitioned into farming or running inns. Some attempted to continue religious activities, forming organizations such as {{ill|Fusōkyō|ja|扶桑教|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fusōkyō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Fuso-kyo|WD=Q11497295}}.<ref>Takahiko Takano, "The Court and Religion in Early Modern Japan," Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2014, ISBN 978-4-642-03461-6, pp. 345-348.</ref>
=== Fuji Oshi === In the {{ill|Tenshōjingo Conflict|ja|天正壬午の乱|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenshōjingo Conflict|1=Battle of Tenshō-Jingo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10939819}}, figures like Watanabe Shūgokuyū played significant roles. Notable Fuji Oshi residences, including {{ill|Old Togawa Residence|ja|旧外川家住宅|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Old Togawa Residence|1=former house of the Togawa Family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q66089879}} and {{ill|Kosano Residence|ja|小佐野家住宅|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kosano Residence|1=house of the Osano Family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q66089882}}, are designated as [[:en:World Heritage Site|World Heritage Sites]] and [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]].<ref>{{Cite web|title=World Cultural Heritage: Mt. Fuji Components|url=https://www.yamanashi-kankou.jp/heritage/fujisanwatcher/jp/world_heritage/index.html|publisher=Yamanashi Tourism Organization|access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Old Togawa Residence|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/185831|website=National Cultural Heritage Database|access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref>
== Cultural Depictions == * "Oshi Yagorō: Journey to Ise" by {{ill|Saijo Naka|ja|西條奈加|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saijo Naka|1=Naka Saijō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11628806}}, ISBN 439634015X * "{{ill|Sakuya-hime|ja|咲夜姫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakuya-hime|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}" by {{ill|Yamaguchi Kaito|ja|山口歌糸|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamaguchi Kaito|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, ISBN 4783880204
== References == <references /> == See Also == * {{ill|Hagaki|ja|羽書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hagaki|1=hagaki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11610188}} * {{ill|Okage-mairi|ja|お蔭参り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okage-mairi|1=Okage Mairi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11263449}} * [[:en:Shukubo|Shukubō]] * {{ill|Accommodation facilities|ja|宿泊施設|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Accommodation facilities|1=accommodation facility|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q48835818}} * {{ill|Ise Calendar|ja|伊勢暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Calendar|qq=}} * [http://www.nikko-annai.com/ Dōshabiki] - Edo-period guides for worshippers at [[:en:Nikkō Tōshō-gū|Nikkō Tōshō-gū]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Onshi]][[ja:御師]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Onshi]][[ja:御師]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Onshi]][[ja:御師]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11488972]] Wikidata name: Onshi [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:49, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Otheruses|Shrine and temple guides|Music teachers for the emperor or imperial family|{{ill|御師 (音楽)|ja|御師 (音楽)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/御師 (音楽)|1=御師 (音楽)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q48759541}}}} [[File:Oshi 20190826a.jpg|thumb|Fuji-ko Guide (御師), photographed August 26, 2019]] '''Oshi''' (御師) refers to shrine priests associated with specific {{ill|Temples and Shrines|lt=Temples and Shrines|lt=Shrines and temples|ja|社寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/shrines and temples|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 寺社}}, who perform {{ill|prayers|ja|祈祷|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/prayers|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 祈り|1=prayer|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q40953}}, guide worshippers, and assist pilgrims with visits and accommodations. In the case of [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]], it was traditionally pronounced as "onshi." These guides often formed concentrated communities along highways, known as {{ill|Oshi towns|ja|御師町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oshi towns|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
== Overview == Originally an abbreviation for "御祈祷師" (prayer guides), the term "oshi" emerged during the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], initially referring to {{ill|temple monks|ja|社僧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/temple monks|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 別当寺|1=bettō-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11396755}}. Pilgrimages like {{ill|Okage-mairi|lt=Ise pilgrimage|ja|伊勢参り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okage-mairi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to お蔭参り|1=Okage Mairi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11263449}} and those to [[:en:Mount Fuji|Mt. Fuji]] became widely accessible. Oshi organized [[:en:Kō (lecture)|kō (pilgrimage associations)]] and hosted pilgrims in their [[:en:Shukubo|shukubō (pilgrim lodgings)]]. The economic stability of the Edo period allowed pilgrimages to combine faith and leisure, enhancing oshi influence. However, groups like the Kumano Oshi declined alongside {{ill|Kumano faith|ja|熊野信仰|1=Kumano Faith|2=ja|3=熊野信仰|WD=Q135501741}}{{efn|The religion of [[Kumano Shrines]]|1=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Three Shrines|lt=Kumano faith|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano faith|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Three Shrines|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 熊野三山}}.

With the establishment of the {{ill|Meiji government|ja|明治政府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji government|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 明治維新|1=Meiji restoration|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8707}}'s {{ill|Shinto|ja|神祇|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天津神・国津神}} system, oshi rapidly declined. Most oshi transitioned into farming or running inns. Some attempted to continue religious activities, forming organizations such as {{ill|Fusōkyō|ja|扶桑教|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fusōkyō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Fuso-kyo|WD=Q11497295}}.<ref>Takahiko Takano, "The Court and Religion in Early Modern Japan," Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2014, ISBN 978-4-642-03461-6, pp. 345-348.</ref>
=== Fuji Oshi === In the {{ill|Tenshōjingo Conflict|ja|天正壬午の乱|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenshōjingo Conflict|1=Battle of Tenshō-Jingo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10939819}}, figures like Watanabe Shūgokuyū played significant roles. Notable Fuji Oshi residences, including {{ill|Old Togawa Residence|ja|旧外川家住宅|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Old Togawa Residence|1=former house of the Togawa Family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q66089879}} and {{ill|Kosano Residence|ja|小佐野家住宅|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kosano Residence|1=house of the Osano Family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q66089882}}, are designated as [[:en:World Heritage Site|World Heritage Sites]] and [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]].<ref>{{Cite web|title=World Cultural Heritage: Mt. Fuji Components|url=https://www.yamanashi-kankou.jp/heritage/fujisanwatcher/jp/world_heritage/index.html|publisher=Yamanashi Tourism Organization|access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Old Togawa Residence|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/185831|website=National Cultural Heritage Database|access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref>
== Cultural Depictions == * "Oshi Yagorō: Journey to Ise" by {{ill|Saijo Naka|ja|西條奈加|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saijo Naka|1=Naka Saijō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11628806}}, ISBN 439634015X * "{{ill|Sakuya-hime|ja|咲夜姫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakuya-hime|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}" by {{ill|Yamaguchi Kaito|ja|山口歌糸|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamaguchi Kaito|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, ISBN 4783880204

In Izumo, oshi structures laid the foundation for the [[:en:Izumo-taishakyo|Izumo Taisha Faith]], creating facilities such as "tanjo" (mission centers) to support local preaching.<ref>'Izumo Taisha Teacher Training Manual,' Izumo Taisha Headquarters, 1989, p. 319</ref>
With the establishment of the {{ill|Meiji government|ja|明治政府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji government|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 明治維新|1=Meiji restoration|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8707}}'s {{ill|Shinto|ja|神祇|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天津神・国津神}} system, oshi rapidly declined. In 1869, oshi were barred from conducting {{ill|Shinto funerals|ja|神葬祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto funerals|1=Shinto funeral|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11590783}}, and by 1871, the profession was abolished. Most oshi transitioned into farming or running inns. Some attempted to continue religious activities, forming organizations such as {{ill|Fusōkyō|ja|扶桑教|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fusōkyō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Fuso-kyo|WD=Q11497295}}.<ref>Takahiko Takano, "The Court and Religion in Early Modern Japan," Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2014, ISBN 978-4-642-03461-6, pp. 345-348.</ref>
=== Fuji Oshi === In the {{ill|Tenshōjingo Conflict|ja|天正壬午の乱|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenshōjingo Conflict|1=Battle of Tenshō-Jingo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10939819}}, figures like Watanabe Shūgokuyū played significant roles. Notable Fuji Oshi residences, including {{ill|Old Togawa Residence|ja|旧外川家住宅|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Old Togawa Residence|1=former house of the Togawa Family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q66089879}} and {{ill|Kosano Residence|ja|小佐野家住宅|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kosano Residence|1=house of the Osano Family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q66089882}}, are designated as [[:en:World Heritage Site|World Heritage Sites]] and [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]].<ref>{{Cite web|title=World Cultural Heritage: Mt. Fuji Components|url=https://www.yamanashi-kankou.jp/heritage/fujisanwatcher/jp/world_heritage/index.html|publisher=Yamanashi Tourism Organization|access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Old Togawa Residence|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/185831|website=National Cultural Heritage Database|access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Onshi]][[ja:御師]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Onshi]][[ja:御師]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Onshi]][[ja:御師]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11488972]] Wikidata name: Onshi [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:49, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Otheruses|Shrine and temple guides|Music teachers for the emperor or imperial family|{{ill|御師 (音楽)|ja|御師 (音楽)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/御師 (音楽)|1=御師 (音楽)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q48759541}}}} [[File:Oshi 20190826a.jpg|thumb|Fuji-ko Guide (御師), photographed August 26, 2019]] '''Oshi''' (御師) refers to shrine priests associated with specific {{ill|Temples and Shrines|lt=Temples and Shrines|lt=Shrines and temples|ja|社寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/shrines and temples|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 寺社}}, who perform {{ill|prayers|ja|祈祷|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/prayers|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 祈り|1=prayer|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q40953}}, guide worshippers, and assist pilgrims with visits and accommodations. In the case of [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]], it was traditionally pronounced as "onshi." These guides often formed concentrated communities along highways, known as {{ill|Oshi towns|ja|御師町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oshi towns|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
== Overview == Originally an abbreviation for "御祈祷師" (prayer guides), the term "oshi" emerged during the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], initially referring to {{ill|temple monks|ja|社僧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/temple monks|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 別当寺|1=bettō-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11396755}}. Later, it extended to include shrine priests who facilitated pilgrimages.
== History == During the Heian period, notable examples of oshi included those from [[:en:Iwashimizu Hachimangū|Iwashimizu Hachiman Shrine]], [[:en:Kamo shrines|Kamo Shrine]], and [[:en:Hiyoshi Taisha|Hiyoshi Taisha]], with the {{ill|Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Sanzan|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} "Kumano Oshi" being particularly famous.<ref name="ushiyama">Yoshiyuki Ushiyama, "Faith and Culture in Medieval Ōhara," Shinshu University Bulletin of Education, Vol. 79 (1993), {{hdl|10091/1626}}</ref> The Kumano Oshi organized prayers, accommodations, and guided noble pilgrims during the late Heian period's {{ill|Kumano pilgrimage|ja|熊野詣|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano pilgrimage|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 熊野三山|1=Kumano Sanzan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10403700}}. Relationships between oshi and pilgrims evolved into stable "master-disciple" ties, known as {{ill|師檀関係|ja|師檀関係|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/師檀関係|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, encompassing even commoners by the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]].
Between the Kamakura and [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi periods]], other prominent oshi groups emerged, such as those from [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]], [[:en:Fujiko (religion)|Fuji-ko]], [[:en:Matsunoo Taisha|Matsuo Shrine]], [[:en:Mishima Taisha|Mishima Taisha]], and [[:en:Mount Haku|Hakusan Shrine]]. Notably, records like the [[:en:Azuma Kagami|Azuma Kagami]] document oshi from [[:en:Izumo-taisha|Izumo Taisha]] serving [[:en:Minamoto no Yoritomo|Minamoto no Yoritomo]].<ref>'Izumo Taisha Teacher Training Manual,' Izumo Taisha Headquarters, 1989, p. 319</ref>
During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], oshi played an essential role as intermediaries between commoners and shrine priests. Pilgrimages like {{ill|Okage-mairi|lt=Ise pilgrimage|ja|伊勢参り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okage-mairi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to お蔭参り|1=Okage Mairi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11263449}} and those to [[:en:Mount Fuji|Mt. Fuji]] became widely accessible. Oshi organized [[:en:Kō (lecture)|kō (pilgrimage associations)]] and hosted pilgrims in their [[:en:Shukubo|shukubō (pilgrim lodgings)]]. The economic stability of the Edo period allowed pilgrimages to combine faith and leisure, enhancing oshi influence. However, groups like the Kumano Oshi declined alongside {{ill|Kumano faith|ja|熊野信仰|1=Kumano Faith|2=ja|3=熊野信仰|WD=Q135501741}}{{efn|The religion of [[Kumano Shrines]]|1=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Three Shrines|lt=Kumano faith|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano faith|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Three Shrines|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 熊野三山}}.
In Izumo, oshi structures laid the foundation for the [[:en:Izumo-taishakyo|Izumo Taisha Faith]], creating facilities such as "tanjo" (mission centers) to support local preaching.<ref>'Izumo Taisha Teacher Training Manual,' Izumo Taisha Headquarters, 1989, p. 319</ref>
With the establishment of the {{ill|Meiji government|ja|明治政府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji government|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 明治維新|1=Meiji restoration|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8707}}'s {{ill|Shinto|ja|神祇|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天津神・国津神}} system, oshi rapidly declined. In 1869, oshi were barred from conducting {{ill|Shinto funerals|ja|神葬祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto funerals|1=Shinto funeral|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11590783}}, and by 1871, the profession was abolished. Most oshi transitioned into farming or running inns. Some attempted to continue religious activities, forming organizations such as {{ill|Fusōkyō|ja|扶桑教|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fusōkyō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Fuso-kyo|WD=Q11497295}}.<ref>Takahiko Takano, "The Court and Religion in Early Modern Japan," Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2014, ISBN 978-4-642-03461-6, pp. 345-348.</ref>
=== Fuji Oshi === In the {{ill|Tenshōjingo Conflict|ja|天正壬午の乱|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenshōjingo Conflict|1=Battle of Tenshō-Jingo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10939819}}, figures like Watanabe Shūgokuyū played significant roles. Notable Fuji Oshi residences, including {{ill|Old Togawa Residence|ja|旧外川家住宅|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Old Togawa Residence|1=former house of the Togawa Family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q66089879}} and {{ill|Kosano Residence|ja|小佐野家住宅|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kosano Residence|1=house of the Osano Family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q66089882}}, are designated as [[:en:World Heritage Site|World Heritage Sites]] and [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]].<ref>{{Cite web|title=World Cultural Heritage: Mt. Fuji Components|url=https://www.yamanashi-kankou.jp/heritage/fujisanwatcher/jp/world_heritage/index.html|publisher=Yamanashi Tourism Organization|access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Old Togawa Residence|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/185831|website=National Cultural Heritage Database|access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref>

Notable Fuji Oshi residences, including {{ill|Old Togawa Residence|ja|旧外川家住宅|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Old Togawa Residence|1=former house of the Togawa Family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q66089879}} and {{ill|Kosano Residence|ja|小佐野家住宅|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kosano Residence|1=house of the Osano Family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q66089882}}, are designated as [[:en:World Heritage Site|World Heritage Sites]] and [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]].<ref>{{Cite web|title=World Cultural Heritage: Mt. Fuji Components|url=https://www.yamanashi-kankou.jp/heritage/fujisanwatcher/jp/world_heritage/index.html|publisher=Yamanashi Tourism Organization|access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Old Togawa Residence|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/185831|website=National Cultural Heritage Database|access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref>
== Cultural Depictions == * "Oshi Yagorō: Journey to Ise" by {{ill|Saijo Naka|ja|西條奈加|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saijo Naka|1=Naka Saijō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11628806}}, ISBN 439634015X * "{{ill|Sakuya-hime|ja|咲夜姫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakuya-hime|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}" by {{ill|Yamaguchi Kaito|ja|山口歌糸|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamaguchi Kaito|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, ISBN 4783880204
== References == <references /> == See Also == * {{ill|Hagaki|ja|羽書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hagaki|1=hagaki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11610188}} * {{ill|Okage-mairi|ja|お蔭参り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okage-mairi|1=Okage Mairi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11263449}} * [[:en:Shukubo|Shukubō]] * {{ill|Accommodation facilities|ja|宿泊施設|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Accommodation facilities|1=accommodation facility|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q48835818}} * {{ill|Ise Calendar|ja|伊勢暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Calendar|qq=}} * [http://www.nikko-annai.com/ Dōshabiki] - Edo-period guides for worshippers at [[:en:Nikkō Tōshō-gū|Nikkō Tōshō-gū]]
