revision.comment: v25: Convert interwiki links [[:en:...]] to {{ill}} templates
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Onshi]][[ja:御師]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Onshi]][[ja:御師]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Onshi]][[ja:御師]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11488972]] Wikidata name: Onshi [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:49, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Otheruses|Shrine and temple guides|Music teachers for the emperor or imperial family|{{ill|御師 (音楽)|ja|御師 (音楽)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/御師 (音楽)|1=御師 (音楽)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q48759541}}}} [[File:Oshi 20190826a.jpg|thumb|Fuji-ko Guide (御師), photographed August 26, 2019]] '''Oshi''' (御師) refers to shrine priests associated with specific {{ill|Temples and Shrines|lt=Temples and Shrines|lt=Shrines and temples|ja|社寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/shrines and temples|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 寺社}}, who perform {{ill|prayers|ja|祈祷|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/prayers|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 祈り|1=prayer|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q40953}}, guide worshippers, and assist pilgrims with visits and accommodations. In the case of {{ill|Ise Shrine|en|Ise Shrine|WD=Q687168|lt=Ise Grand Shrine}}, it was traditionally pronounced as "onshi." These guides often formed concentrated communities along highways, known as {{ill|Oshi towns|ja|御師町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oshi towns|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
== Overview == Originally an abbreviation for "御祈祷師" (prayer guides), the term "oshi" emerged during the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}}, initially referring to {{ill|temple monks|ja|社僧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/temple monks|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 別当寺|1=bettō-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11396755}}. Later, it extended to include shrine priests who facilitated pilgrimages.
== History == During the Heian period, notable examples of oshi included those from {{ill|Iwashimizu Hachimangū|en|Iwashimizu Hachimangū|WD=Q710098|lt=Iwashimizu Hachiman Shrine}}, {{ill|Kamo shrines|en|Kamo shrines|WD=Q1306606|lt=Kamo Shrine}}, and {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|en|Hiyoshi Taisha|WD=Q656451|lt=Hiyoshi Taisha}}, with the {{ill|Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Sanzan|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} "Kumano Oshi" being particularly famous.<ref name="ushiyama">Yoshiyuki Ushiyama, "Faith and Culture in Medieval Ōhara," Shinshu University Bulletin of Education, Vol. 79 (1993), {{hdl|10091/1626}}</ref> The Kumano Oshi organized prayers, accommodations, and guided noble pilgrims during the late Heian period's {{ill|Kumano pilgrimage|ja|熊野詣|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano pilgrimage|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 熊野三山|1=Kumano Sanzan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10403700}}. Relationships between oshi and pilgrims evolved into stable "master-disciple" ties, known as {{ill|師檀関係|ja|師檀関係|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/師檀関係|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, encompassing even commoners by the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period}}.
Between the Kamakura and {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi periods}}, other prominent oshi groups emerged, such as those from {{ill|Ise Shrine|en|Ise Shrine|WD=Q687168|lt=Ise Grand Shrine}}, {{ill|Fujiko (religion)|en|Fujiko (religion)|WD=Q11456201|lt=Fuji-ko}}, {{ill|Matsunoo Taisha|en|Matsunoo Taisha|WD=Q710302|lt=Matsuo Shrine}}, {{ill|Mishima Taisha|en|Mishima Taisha|WD=Q650772|lt=Mishima Taisha}}, and {{ill|Mount Haku|en|Mount Haku|WD=Q2639098|lt=Hakusan Shrine}}. Notably, records like the {{ill|Azuma Kagami|en|Azuma Kagami|WD=Q1042324|lt=Azuma Kagami}} document oshi from {{ill|Izumo-taisha|en|Izumo-taisha|WD=Q696362|lt=Izumo Taisha}} serving {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|en|Minamoto no Yoritomo|WD=Q242800|lt=Minamoto no Yoritomo}}.<ref>'Izumo Taisha Teacher Training Manual,' Izumo Taisha Headquarters, 1989, p. 319</ref>
During the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}, oshi played an essential role as intermediaries between commoners and shrine priests. Pilgrimages like {{ill|Okage-mairi|lt=Ise pilgrimage|ja|伊勢参り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okage-mairi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to お蔭参り|1=Okage Mairi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11263449}} and those to {{ill|Mount Fuji|en|Mount Fuji|WD=Q39231|lt=Mt. Fuji}} became widely accessible. Oshi organized {{ill|Kō (lecture)|en|Kō (lecture)|WD=Q11632184|lt=kō (pilgrimage associations)}} and hosted pilgrims in their {{ill|Shukubo|en|Shukubo|WD=Q11455614|lt=shukubō (pilgrim lodgings)}}. The economic stability of the Edo period allowed pilgrimages to combine faith and leisure, enhancing oshi influence. However, groups like the Kumano Oshi declined alongside {{ill|Kumano faith|ja|熊野信仰|1=Kumano Faith|2=ja|3=熊野信仰|WD=Q135501741}}{{efn|The religion of [[Kumano Shrines]]|1=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Three Shrines|lt=Kumano faith|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano faith|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Three Shrines|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 熊野三山}}.
In Izumo, oshi structures laid the foundation for the {{ill|Izumo-taishakyo|en|Izumo-taishakyo|WD=Q6102386|lt=Izumo Taisha Faith}}, creating facilities such as "tanjo" (mission centers) to support local preaching.<ref>'Izumo Taisha Teacher Training Manual,' Izumo Taisha Headquarters, 1989, p. 319</ref>
With the establishment of the {{ill|Meiji government|ja|明治政府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji government|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 明治維新|1=Meiji restoration|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8707}}'s {{ill|Shinto|ja|神祇|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天津神・国津神}} system, oshi rapidly declined. In 1869, oshi were barred from conducting {{ill|Shinto funerals|ja|神葬祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto funerals|1=Shinto funeral|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11590783}}, and by 1871, the profession was abolished. Most oshi transitioned into farming or running inns. Some attempted to continue religious activities, forming organizations such as {{ill|Fusōkyō|ja|扶桑教|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fusōkyō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Fuso-kyo|WD=Q11497295}}.<ref>Takahiko Takano, "The Court and Religion in Early Modern Japan," Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2014, ISBN 978-4-642-03461-6, pp. 345-348.</ref>
=== Fuji Oshi === In the {{ill|Tenshōjingo Conflict|ja|天正壬午の乱|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenshōjingo Conflict|1=Battle of Tenshō-Jingo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10939819}}, figures like Watanabe Shūgokuyū played significant roles. Notable Fuji Oshi residences, including {{ill|Old Togawa Residence|ja|旧外川家住宅|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Old Togawa Residence|1=former house of the Togawa Family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q66089879}} and {{ill|Kosano Residence|ja|小佐野家住宅|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kosano Residence|1=house of the Osano Family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q66089882}}, are designated as {{ill|World Heritage Site|en|World Heritage Site|WD=Q9259|lt=World Heritage Sites}} and {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property}}.<ref>{{Cite web|title=World Cultural Heritage: Mt. Fuji Components|url=https://www.yamanashi-kankou.jp/heritage/fujisanwatcher/jp/world_heritage/index.html|publisher=Yamanashi Tourism Organization|access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Old Togawa Residence|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/185831|website=National Cultural Heritage Database|access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref>
== Cultural Depictions == * "Oshi Yagorō: Journey to Ise" by {{ill|Saijo Naka|ja|西條奈加|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saijo Naka|1=Naka Saijō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11628806}}, ISBN 439634015X * "{{ill|Sakuya-hime|ja|咲夜姫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakuya-hime|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}" by {{ill|Yamaguchi Kaito|ja|山口歌糸|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamaguchi Kaito|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, ISBN 4783880204
== References == <references /> == See Also == * {{ill|Hagaki|ja|羽書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hagaki|1=hagaki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11610188}} * {{ill|Okage-mairi|ja|お蔭参り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okage-mairi|1=Okage Mairi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11263449}} * {{ill|Shukubo|en|Shukubo|WD=Q11455614|lt=Shukubō}} * {{ill|Accommodation facilities|ja|宿泊施設|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Accommodation facilities|1=accommodation facility|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q48835818}} * {{ill|Ise Calendar|ja|伊勢暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Calendar|qq=}} * [http://www.nikko-annai.com/ Dōshabiki] - Edo-period guides for worshippers at {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|en|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|WD=Q696641|lt=Nikkō Tōshō-gū}}

Fuji}} became widely accessible. Most oshi transitioned into farming or running inns. Some attempted to continue religious activities, forming organizations such as {{ill|Fusōkyō|ja|扶桑教|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fusōkyō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Fuso-kyo|WD=Q11497295}}.<ref>Takahiko Takano, "The Court and Religion in Early Modern Japan," Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2014, ISBN 978-4-642-03461-6, pp. 345-348.</ref>
=== Fuji Oshi === In the {{ill|Tenshōjingo Conflict|ja|天正壬午の乱|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenshōjingo Conflict|1=Battle of Tenshō-Jingo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10939819}}, figures like Watanabe Shūgokuyū played significant roles. Notable Fuji Oshi residences, including {{ill|Old Togawa Residence|ja|旧外川家住宅|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Old Togawa Residence|1=former house of the Togawa Family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q66089879}} and {{ill|Kosano Residence|ja|小佐野家住宅|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kosano Residence|1=house of the Osano Family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q66089882}}, are designated as {{ill|World Heritage Site|en|World Heritage Site|WD=Q9259|lt=World Heritage Sites}} and {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property}}.<ref>{{Cite web|title=World Cultural Heritage: Mt. Fuji Components|url=https://www.yamanashi-kankou.jp/heritage/fujisanwatcher/jp/world_heritage/index.html|publisher=Yamanashi Tourism Organization|access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Old Togawa Residence|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/185831|website=National Cultural Heritage Database|access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref>
== Cultural Depictions == * "Oshi Yagorō: Journey to Ise" by {{ill|Saijo Naka|ja|西條奈加|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saijo Naka|1=Naka Saijō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11628806}}, ISBN 439634015X * "{{ill|Sakuya-hime|ja|咲夜姫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakuya-hime|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}" by {{ill|Yamaguchi Kaito|ja|山口歌糸|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamaguchi Kaito|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, ISBN 4783880204
== References == <references /> == See Also == * {{ill|Hagaki|ja|羽書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hagaki|1=hagaki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11610188}} * {{ill|Okage-mairi|ja|お蔭参り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okage-mairi|1=Okage Mairi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11263449}} * {{ill|Shukubo|en|Shukubo|WD=Q11455614|lt=Shukubō}} * {{ill|Accommodation facilities|ja|宿泊施設|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Accommodation facilities|1=accommodation facility|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q48835818}} * {{ill|Ise Calendar|ja|伊勢暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Calendar|qq=}} * [http://www.nikko-annai.com/ Dōshabiki] - Edo-period guides for worshippers at {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|en|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|WD=Q696641|lt=Nikkō Tōshō-gū}}

[[en:Onshi]]
<!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi-->
<!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> <!--enwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{wikidata link|Q11488972}} [[Category:Buddhist monks]] [[Category:Buddhist titles]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Fuji worship]] [[Category:Ise Shrine]] [[Category:Service occupations]] [[Category:Street culture]] [[Category:Titles and Positions in Shintō]] [[Category:translated pages with valid en interwikis]] [[Category:Travel]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Onshi]][[ja:御師]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Onshi]][[ja:御師]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Onshi]][[ja:御師]]
{{Otheruses|Shrine and temple guides|Music teachers for the emperor or imperial family|{{ill|御師 (音楽)|ja|御師 (音楽)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/御師 (音楽)|1=御師 (音楽)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q48759541}}}} [[File:Oshi 20190826a.jpg|thumb|Fuji-ko Guide (御師), photographed August 26, 2019]] '''Oshi''' (御師) refers to shrine priests associated with specific {{ill|Temples and Shrines|lt=Temples and Shrines|lt=Shrines and temples|ja|社寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/shrines and temples|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 寺社}}, who perform {{ill|prayers|ja|祈祷|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/prayers|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 祈り|1=prayer|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q40953}}, guide worshippers, and assist pilgrims with visits and accommodations. In the case of {{ill|Ise Shrine|en|Ise Shrine|WD=Q687168|lt=Ise Grand Shrine}}, it was traditionally pronounced as "onshi." These guides often formed concentrated communities along highways, known as {{ill|Oshi towns|ja|御師町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oshi towns|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
== Overview == Originally an abbreviation for "御祈祷師" (prayer guides), the term "oshi" emerged during the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}}, initially referring to {{ill|temple monks|ja|社僧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/temple monks|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 別当寺|1=bettō-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11396755}}. Later, it extended to include shrine priests who facilitated pilgrimages.
== History == During the Heian period, notable examples of oshi included those from {{ill|Iwashimizu Hachimangū|en|Iwashimizu Hachimangū|WD=Q710098|lt=Iwashimizu Hachiman Shrine}}, {{ill|Kamo shrines|en|Kamo shrines|WD=Q1306606|lt=Kamo Shrine}}, and {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|en|Hiyoshi Taisha|WD=Q656451|lt=Hiyoshi Taisha}}, with the {{ill|Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Sanzan|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} "Kumano Oshi" being particularly famous.<ref name="ushiyama">Yoshiyuki Ushiyama, "Faith and Culture in Medieval Ōhara," Shinshu University Bulletin of Education, Vol. 79 (1993), {{hdl|10091/1626}}</ref> The Kumano Oshi organized prayers, accommodations, and guided noble pilgrims during the late Heian period's {{ill|Kumano pilgrimage|ja|熊野詣|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano pilgrimage|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 熊野三山|1=Kumano Sanzan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10403700}}. Relationships between oshi and pilgrims evolved into stable "master-disciple" ties, known as {{ill|師檀関係|ja|師檀関係|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/師檀関係|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, encompassing even commoners by the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period}}.
Between the Kamakura and {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi periods}}, other prominent oshi groups emerged, such as those from {{ill|Ise Shrine|en|Ise Shrine|WD=Q687168|lt=Ise Grand Shrine}}, {{ill|Fujiko (religion)|en|Fujiko (religion)|WD=Q11456201|lt=Fuji-ko}}, {{ill|Matsunoo Taisha|en|Matsunoo Taisha|WD=Q710302|lt=Matsuo Shrine}}, {{ill|Mishima Taisha|en|Mishima Taisha|WD=Q650772|lt=Mishima Taisha}}, and {{ill|Mount Haku|en|Mount Haku|WD=Q2639098|lt=Hakusan Shrine}}. Notably, records like the {{ill|Azuma Kagami|en|Azuma Kagami|WD=Q1042324|lt=Azuma Kagami}} document oshi from {{ill|Izumo-taisha|en|Izumo-taisha|WD=Q696362|lt=Izumo Taisha}} serving {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|en|Minamoto no Yoritomo|WD=Q242800|lt=Minamoto no Yoritomo}}.<ref>'Izumo Taisha Teacher Training Manual,' Izumo Taisha Headquarters, 1989, p. 319</ref>
During the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}, oshi played an essential role as intermediaries between commoners and shrine priests. Pilgrimages like {{ill|Okage-mairi|lt=Ise pilgrimage|ja|伊勢参り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okage-mairi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to お蔭参り|1=Okage Mairi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11263449}} and those to {{ill|Mount Fuji|en|Mount Fuji|WD=Q39231|lt=Mt. Fuji}} became widely accessible. Oshi organized {{ill|Kō (lecture)|en|Kō (lecture)|WD=Q11632184|lt=kō (pilgrimage associations)}} and hosted pilgrims in their {{ill|Shukubo|en|Shukubo|WD=Q11455614|lt=shukubō (pilgrim lodgings)}}. The economic stability of the Edo period allowed pilgrimages to combine faith and leisure, enhancing oshi influence. However, groups like the Kumano Oshi declined alongside {{ill|Kumano faith|ja|熊野信仰|1=Kumano Faith|2=ja|3=熊野信仰|WD=Q135501741}}{{efn|The religion of [[Kumano Shrines]]|1=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Three Shrines|lt=Kumano faith|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano faith|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Three Shrines|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 熊野三山}}.
In Izumo, oshi structures laid the foundation for the {{ill|Izumo-taishakyo|en|Izumo-taishakyo|WD=Q6102386|lt=Izumo Taisha Faith}}, creating facilities such as "tanjo" (mission centers) to support local preaching.<ref>'Izumo Taisha Teacher Training Manual,' Izumo Taisha Headquarters, 1989, p. 319</ref>
With the establishment of the {{ill|Meiji government|ja|明治政府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji government|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 明治維新|1=Meiji restoration|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8707}}'s {{ill|Shinto|ja|神祇|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天津神・国津神}} system, oshi rapidly declined. In 1869, oshi were barred from conducting {{ill|Shinto funerals|ja|神葬祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto funerals|1=Shinto funeral|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11590783}}, and by 1871, the profession was abolished. Most oshi transitioned into farming or running inns. Some attempted to continue religious activities, forming organizations such as {{ill|Fusōkyō|ja|扶桑教|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fusōkyō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Fuso-kyo|WD=Q11497295}}.<ref>Takahiko Takano, "The Court and Religion in Early Modern Japan," Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2014, ISBN 978-4-642-03461-6, pp. 345-348.</ref>
=== Fuji Oshi === In the {{ill|Tenshōjingo Conflict|ja|天正壬午の乱|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenshōjingo Conflict|1=Battle of Tenshō-Jingo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10939819}}, figures like Watanabe Shūgokuyū played significant roles. Notable Fuji Oshi residences, including {{ill|Old Togawa Residence|ja|旧外川家住宅|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Old Togawa Residence|1=former house of the Togawa Family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q66089879}} and {{ill|Kosano Residence|ja|小佐野家住宅|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kosano Residence|1=house of the Osano Family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q66089882}}, are designated as {{ill|World Heritage Site|en|World Heritage Site|WD=Q9259|lt=World Heritage Sites}} and {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property}}.<ref>{{Cite web|title=World Cultural Heritage: Mt. Fuji Components|url=https://www.yamanashi-kankou.jp/heritage/fujisanwatcher/jp/world_heritage/index.html|publisher=Yamanashi Tourism Organization|access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Old Togawa Residence|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/185831|website=National Cultural Heritage Database|access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref>
== Cultural Depictions == * "Oshi Yagorō: Journey to Ise" by {{ill|Saijo Naka|ja|西條奈加|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saijo Naka|1=Naka Saijō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11628806}}, ISBN 439634015X * "{{ill|Sakuya-hime|ja|咲夜姫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakuya-hime|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}" by {{ill|Yamaguchi Kaito|ja|山口歌糸|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamaguchi Kaito|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, ISBN 4783880204
== References == <references /> == See Also == * {{ill|Hagaki|ja|羽書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hagaki|1=hagaki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11610188}} * {{ill|Okage-mairi|ja|お蔭参り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okage-mairi|1=Okage Mairi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11263449}} * {{ill|Shukubo|en|Shukubo|WD=Q11455614|lt=Shukubō}} * {{ill|Accommodation facilities|ja|宿泊施設|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Accommodation facilities|1=accommodation facility|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q48835818}} * {{ill|Ise Calendar|ja|伊勢暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Calendar|qq=}} * [http://www.nikko-annai.com/ Dōshabiki] - Edo-period guides for worshippers at {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|en|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|WD=Q696641|lt=Nikkō Tōshō-gū}}

Fuji}} became widely accessible. Some attempted to continue religious activities, forming organizations such as {{ill|Fusōkyō|ja|扶桑教|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fusōkyō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Fuso-kyo|WD=Q11497295}}.<ref>Takahiko Takano, "The Court and Religion in Early Modern Japan," Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2014, ISBN 978-4-642-03461-6, pp. 345-348.</ref>

Notable Fuji Oshi residences, including {{ill|Old Togawa Residence|ja|旧外川家住宅|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Old Togawa Residence|1=former house of the Togawa Family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q66089879}} and {{ill|Kosano Residence|ja|小佐野家住宅|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kosano Residence|1=house of the Osano Family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q66089882}}, are designated as {{ill|World Heritage Site|en|World Heritage Site|WD=Q9259|lt=World Heritage Sites}} and {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property}}.<ref>{{Cite web|title=World Cultural Heritage: Mt. Fuji Components|url=https://www.yamanashi-kankou.jp/heritage/fujisanwatcher/jp/world_heritage/index.html|publisher=Yamanashi Tourism Organization|access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Old Togawa Residence|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/185831|website=National Cultural Heritage Database|access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref>
== Cultural Depictions == * "Oshi Yagorō: Journey to Ise" by {{ill|Saijo Naka|ja|西條奈加|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saijo Naka|1=Naka Saijō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11628806}}, ISBN 439634015X * "{{ill|Sakuya-hime|ja|咲夜姫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakuya-hime|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}" by {{ill|Yamaguchi Kaito|ja|山口歌糸|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamaguchi Kaito|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, ISBN 4783880204
== References == <references /> == See Also == * {{ill|Hagaki|ja|羽書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hagaki|1=hagaki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11610188}} * {{ill|Okage-mairi|ja|お蔭参り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okage-mairi|1=Okage Mairi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11263449}} * {{ill|Shukubo|en|Shukubo|WD=Q11455614|lt=Shukubō}} * {{ill|Accommodation facilities|ja|宿泊施設|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Accommodation facilities|1=accommodation facility|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q48835818}} * {{ill|Ise Calendar|ja|伊勢暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Calendar|qq=}} * [http://www.nikko-annai.com/ Dōshabiki] - Edo-period guides for worshippers at {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|en|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|WD=Q696641|lt=Nikkō Tōshō-gū}}

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Onshi]][[ja:御師]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Onshi]][[ja:御師]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Onshi]][[ja:御師]]
{{Otheruses|Shrine and temple guides|Music teachers for the emperor or imperial family|{{ill|御師 (音楽)|ja|御師 (音楽)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/御師 (音楽)|1=御師 (音楽)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q48759541}}}} [[File:Oshi 20190826a.jpg|thumb|Fuji-ko Guide (御師), photographed August 26, 2019]] '''Oshi''' (御師) refers to shrine priests associated with specific {{ill|Temples and Shrines|lt=Temples and Shrines|lt=Shrines and temples|ja|社寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/shrines and temples|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 寺社}}, who perform {{ill|prayers|ja|祈祷|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/prayers|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 祈り|1=prayer|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q40953}}, guide worshippers, and assist pilgrims with visits and accommodations. In the case of {{ill|Ise Shrine|en|Ise Shrine|WD=Q687168|lt=Ise Grand Shrine}}, it was traditionally pronounced as "onshi." These guides often formed concentrated communities along highways, known as {{ill|Oshi towns|ja|御師町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oshi towns|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
== Overview == Originally an abbreviation for "御祈祷師" (prayer guides), the term "oshi" emerged during the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}}, initially referring to {{ill|temple monks|ja|社僧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/temple monks|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 別当寺|1=bettō-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11396755}}. Later, it extended to include shrine priests who facilitated pilgrimages.
== History == During the Heian period, notable examples of oshi included those from {{ill|Iwashimizu Hachimangū|en|Iwashimizu Hachimangū|WD=Q710098|lt=Iwashimizu Hachiman Shrine}}, {{ill|Kamo shrines|en|Kamo shrines|WD=Q1306606|lt=Kamo Shrine}}, and {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|en|Hiyoshi Taisha|WD=Q656451|lt=Hiyoshi Taisha}}, with the {{ill|Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Sanzan|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} "Kumano Oshi" being particularly famous.<ref name="ushiyama">Yoshiyuki Ushiyama, "Faith and Culture in Medieval Ōhara," Shinshu University Bulletin of Education, Vol. 79 (1993), {{hdl|10091/1626}}</ref> The Kumano Oshi organized prayers, accommodations, and guided noble pilgrims during the late Heian period's {{ill|Kumano pilgrimage|ja|熊野詣|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano pilgrimage|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 熊野三山|1=Kumano Sanzan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10403700}}. Relationships between oshi and pilgrims evolved into stable "master-disciple" ties, known as {{ill|師檀関係|ja|師檀関係|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/師檀関係|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, encompassing even commoners by the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period}}.
Between the Kamakura and {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi periods}}, other prominent oshi groups emerged, such as those from {{ill|Ise Shrine|en|Ise Shrine|WD=Q687168|lt=Ise Grand Shrine}}, {{ill|Fujiko (religion)|en|Fujiko (religion)|WD=Q11456201|lt=Fuji-ko}}, {{ill|Matsunoo Taisha|en|Matsunoo Taisha|WD=Q710302|lt=Matsuo Shrine}}, {{ill|Mishima Taisha|en|Mishima Taisha|WD=Q650772|lt=Mishima Taisha}}, and {{ill|Mount Haku|en|Mount Haku|WD=Q2639098|lt=Hakusan Shrine}}. Notably, records like the {{ill|Azuma Kagami|en|Azuma Kagami|WD=Q1042324|lt=Azuma Kagami}} document oshi from {{ill|Izumo-taisha|en|Izumo-taisha|WD=Q696362|lt=Izumo Taisha}} serving {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|en|Minamoto no Yoritomo|WD=Q242800|lt=Minamoto no Yoritomo}}.<ref>'Izumo Taisha Teacher Training Manual,' Izumo Taisha Headquarters, 1989, p. 319</ref>
During the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}, oshi played an essential role as intermediaries between commoners and shrine priests. Pilgrimages like {{ill|Okage-mairi|lt=Ise pilgrimage|ja|伊勢参り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okage-mairi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to お蔭参り|1=Okage Mairi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11263449}} and those to {{ill|Mount Fuji|en|Mount Fuji|WD=Q39231|lt=Mt. Fuji}} became widely accessible. Oshi organized {{ill|Kō (lecture)|en|Kō (lecture)|WD=Q11632184|lt=kō (pilgrimage associations)}} and hosted pilgrims in their {{ill|Shukubo|en|Shukubo|WD=Q11455614|lt=shukubō (pilgrim lodgings)}}. The economic stability of the Edo period allowed pilgrimages to combine faith and leisure, enhancing oshi influence. However, groups like the Kumano Oshi declined alongside {{ill|Kumano faith|ja|熊野信仰|1=Kumano Faith|2=ja|3=熊野信仰|WD=Q135501741}}{{efn|The religion of [[Kumano Shrines]]|1=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Three Shrines|lt=Kumano faith|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano faith|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Three Shrines|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 熊野三山}}.
In Izumo, oshi structures laid the foundation for the {{ill|Izumo-taishakyo|en|Izumo-taishakyo|WD=Q6102386|lt=Izumo Taisha Faith}}, creating facilities such as "tanjo" (mission centers) to support local preaching.<ref>'Izumo Taisha Teacher Training Manual,' Izumo Taisha Headquarters, 1989, p. 319</ref>
With the establishment of the {{ill|Meiji government|ja|明治政府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji government|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 明治維新|1=Meiji restoration|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8707}}'s {{ill|Shinto|ja|神祇|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天津神・国津神}} system, oshi rapidly declined. In 1869, oshi were barred from conducting {{ill|Shinto funerals|ja|神葬祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto funerals|1=Shinto funeral|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11590783}}, and by 1871, the profession was abolished. Most oshi transitioned into farming or running inns. Some attempted to continue religious activities, forming organizations such as {{ill|Fusōkyō|ja|扶桑教|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fusōkyō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Fuso-kyo|WD=Q11497295}}.<ref>Takahiko Takano, "The Court and Religion in Early Modern Japan," Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2014, ISBN 978-4-642-03461-6, pp. 345-348.</ref>
=== Fuji Oshi === In the {{ill|Tenshōjingo Conflict|ja|天正壬午の乱|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenshōjingo Conflict|1=Battle of Tenshō-Jingo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10939819}}, figures like Watanabe Shūgokuyū played significant roles. Notable Fuji Oshi residences, including {{ill|Old Togawa Residence|ja|旧外川家住宅|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Old Togawa Residence|1=former house of the Togawa Family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q66089879}} and {{ill|Kosano Residence|ja|小佐野家住宅|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kosano Residence|1=house of the Osano Family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q66089882}}, are designated as {{ill|World Heritage Site|en|World Heritage Site|WD=Q9259|lt=World Heritage Sites}} and {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property}}.<ref>{{Cite web|title=World Cultural Heritage: Mt. Fuji Components|url=https://www.yamanashi-kankou.jp/heritage/fujisanwatcher/jp/world_heritage/index.html|publisher=Yamanashi Tourism Organization|access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Old Togawa Residence|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/185831|website=National Cultural Heritage Database|access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref>
== Cultural Depictions == * "Oshi Yagorō: Journey to Ise" by {{ill|Saijo Naka|ja|西條奈加|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saijo Naka|1=Naka Saijō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11628806}}, ISBN 439634015X * "{{ill|Sakuya-hime|ja|咲夜姫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakuya-hime|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}" by {{ill|Yamaguchi Kaito|ja|山口歌糸|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamaguchi Kaito|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, ISBN 4783880204
== References == <references /> == See Also == * {{ill|Hagaki|ja|羽書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hagaki|1=hagaki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11610188}} * {{ill|Okage-mairi|ja|お蔭参り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okage-mairi|1=Okage Mairi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11263449}} * {{ill|Shukubo|en|Shukubo|WD=Q11455614|lt=Shukubō}} * {{ill|Accommodation facilities|ja|宿泊施設|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Accommodation facilities|1=accommodation facility|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q48835818}} * {{ill|Ise Calendar|ja|伊勢暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Calendar|qq=}} * [http://www.nikko-annai.com/ Dōshabiki] - Edo-period guides for worshippers at {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|en|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|WD=Q696641|lt=Nikkō Tōshō-gū}}

Pilgrimages like {{ill|Okage-mairi|lt=Ise pilgrimage|ja|伊勢参り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okage-mairi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to お蔭参り|1=Okage Mairi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11263449}} and those to {{ill|Mount Fuji|en|Mount Fuji|WD=Q39231|lt=Mt. Some attempted to continue religious activities, forming organizations such as {{ill|Fusōkyō|ja|扶桑教|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fusōkyō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Fuso-kyo|WD=Q11497295}}.<ref>Takahiko Takano, "The Court and Religion in Early Modern Japan," Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2014, ISBN 978-4-642-03461-6, pp. 345-348.</ref>

Notable Fuji Oshi residences, including {{ill|Old Togawa Residence|ja|旧外川家住宅|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Old Togawa Residence|1=former house of the Togawa Family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q66089879}} and {{ill|Kosano Residence|ja|小佐野家住宅|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kosano Residence|1=house of the Osano Family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q66089882}}, are designated as {{ill|World Heritage Site|en|World Heritage Site|WD=Q9259|lt=World Heritage Sites|1=World Heritage Site}} and {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)}}.<ref>{{Cite web|title=World Cultural Heritage: Mt. Fuji Components|url=https://www.yamanashi-kankou.jp/heritage/fujisanwatcher/jp/world_heritage/index.html|publisher=Yamanashi Tourism Organization|access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Old Togawa Residence|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/185831|website=National Cultural Heritage Database|access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref>
== Cultural Depictions == * "Oshi Yagorō: Journey to Ise" by {{ill|Saijo Naka|ja|西條奈加|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saijo Naka|1=Naka Saijō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11628806}}, ISBN 439634015X * "{{ill|Sakuya-hime|ja|咲夜姫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakuya-hime|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}" by {{ill|Yamaguchi Kaito|ja|山口歌糸|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamaguchi Kaito|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, ISBN 4783880204
== References == <references /> == See Also == * {{ill|Hagaki|ja|羽書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hagaki|1=hagaki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11610188}} * {{ill|Okage-mairi|ja|お蔭参り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okage-mairi|1=Okage Mairi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11263449}} * {{ill|Shukubo|en|Shukubo|WD=Q11455614|lt=Shukubō|1=Shukubo}} * {{ill|Accommodation facilities|ja|宿泊施設|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Accommodation facilities|1=accommodation facility|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q48835818}} * {{ill|Ise Calendar|ja|伊勢暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Calendar|qq=}} * [http://www.nikko-annai.com/ Dōshabiki] - Edo-period guides for worshippers at {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|en|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|WD=Q696641|lt=Nikkō Tōshō-gū|1=Nikkō Tōshō-gū}}

<!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi--><!--Onshi-->
<!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> <!--enwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{wikidata link|Q11488972}} [[Category:Buddhist monks]] [[Category:Buddhist titles]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Fuji worship]] [[Category:Ise Shrine]] [[Category:Service occupations]] [[Category:Street culture]] [[Category:Titles and Positions in Shintō]] [[Category:translated pages with valid en interwikis]] [[Category:Travel]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:pages]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Onshi]][[ja:御師]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Onshi]][[ja:御師]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Onshi]][[ja:御師]]
{{Otheruses|Shrine and temple guides|Music teachers for the emperor or imperial family|{{ill|御師 (音楽)|ja|御師 (音楽)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/御師 (音楽)|1=Q48759541|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q48759541}}}} [[File:Oshi 20190826a.jpg|thumb|Fuji-ko Guide (御師), photographed August 26, 2019]] '''Oshi''' (御師) refers to shrine priests associated with specific {{ill|Temples and Shrines|lt=Temples and Shrines|lt=Shrines and temples|ja|社寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/shrines and temples|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 寺社}}, who perform {{ill|prayers|ja|祈祷|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/prayers|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 祈り|1=Prayer|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q40953}}, guide worshippers, and assist pilgrims with visits and accommodations. In the case of {{ill|Ise Shrine|en|Ise Shrine|WD=Q687168|lt=Ise Grand Shrine|1=Ise Grand Shrine}}, it was traditionally pronounced as "onshi." These guides often formed concentrated communities along highways, known as {{ill|Oshi towns|ja|御師町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oshi towns|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
== Overview == Originally an abbreviation for "御祈祷師" (prayer guides), the term "oshi" emerged during the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}, initially referring to {{ill|temple monks|ja|社僧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/temple monks|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 別当寺|1=Bettō-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11396755}}. Later, it extended to include shrine priests who facilitated pilgrimages.
== History == During the Heian period, notable examples of oshi included those from {{ill|Iwashimizu Hachimangū|en|Iwashimizu Hachimangū|WD=Q710098|lt=Iwashimizu Hachiman Shrine|1=Iwashimizu Hachimangū}}, {{ill|Kamo shrines|en|Kamo shrines|WD=Q1306606|lt=Kamo Shrine|1=Kamo shrines}}, and {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|en|Hiyoshi Taisha|WD=Q656451|lt=Hiyoshi Taisha|1=Hiyoshi Taisha}}, with the {{ill|Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Sanzan|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} "Kumano Oshi" being particularly famous.<ref name="ushiyama">Yoshiyuki Ushiyama, "Faith and Culture in Medieval Ōhara," Shinshu University Bulletin of Education, Vol. 79 (1993), {{hdl|10091/1626}}</ref> The Kumano Oshi organized prayers, accommodations, and guided noble pilgrims during the late Heian period's {{ill|Kumano pilgrimage|ja|熊野詣|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano pilgrimage|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 熊野三山|1=Kumano Three Shrines|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10403700}}. Relationships between oshi and pilgrims evolved into stable "master-disciple" ties, known as {{ill|師檀関係|ja|師檀関係|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/師檀関係|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, encompassing even commoners by the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period|1=Kamakura period}}.
Between the Kamakura and {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi periods|1=Muromachi period}}, other prominent oshi groups emerged, such as those from {{ill|Ise Shrine|en|Ise Shrine|WD=Q687168|lt=Ise Grand Shrine|1=Ise Grand Shrine}}, {{ill|Fujiko (religion)|en|Fujiko (religion)|WD=Q11456201|lt=Fuji-ko|1=Fujiko (religion)}}, {{ill|Matsunoo Taisha|en|Matsunoo Taisha|WD=Q710302|lt=Matsuo Shrine|1=Matsunoo Taisha}}, {{ill|Mishima Taisha|en|Mishima Taisha|WD=Q650772|lt=Mishima Taisha|1=Mishima Taisha}}, and {{ill|Mount Haku|en|Mount Haku|WD=Q2639098|lt=Hakusan Shrine|1=Mount Haku}}. Notably, records like the {{ill|Azuma Kagami|en|Azuma Kagami|WD=Q1042324|lt=Azuma Kagami|1=Azuma Kagami}} document oshi from {{ill|Izumo-taisha|en|Izumo-taisha|WD=Q696362|lt=Izumo Taisha|1=Izumo-taisha}} serving {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|en|Minamoto no Yoritomo|WD=Q242800|lt=Minamoto no Yoritomo|1=Minamoto no Yoritomo}}.<ref>'Izumo Taisha Teacher Training Manual,' Izumo Taisha Headquarters, 1989, p. 319</ref>
During the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}, oshi played an essential role as intermediaries between commoners and shrine priests. Pilgrimages like {{ill|Okage-mairi|lt=Ise pilgrimage|ja|伊勢参り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okage-mairi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to お蔭参り|1=Okage Mairi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11263449}} and those to {{ill|Mount Fuji|en|Mount Fuji|WD=Q39231|lt=Mt. Fuji|1=Mount Fuji}} became widely accessible. Oshi organized {{ill|Kō (lecture)|en|Kō (lecture)|WD=Q11632184|lt=kō (pilgrimage associations)|1=Kō (lecture)}} and hosted pilgrims in their {{ill|Shukubo|en|Shukubo|WD=Q11455614|lt=shukubō (pilgrim lodgings)|1=Shukubo}}. The economic stability of the Edo period allowed pilgrimages to combine faith and leisure, enhancing oshi influence. However, groups like the Kumano Oshi declined alongside {{ill|Kumano faith|ja|熊野信仰|1=Kumano Faith|2=ja|3=熊野信仰|WD=Q135501741}}{{efn|The religion of [[Kumano Shrines]]|1=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Three Shrines|lt=Kumano faith|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano faith|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Three Shrines|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 熊野三山}}.
In Izumo, oshi structures laid the foundation for the {{ill|Izumo-taishakyo|en|Izumo-taishakyo|WD=Q6102386|lt=Izumo Taisha Faith|1=Izumo-taishakyo}}, creating facilities such as "tanjo" (mission centers) to support local preaching.<ref>'Izumo Taisha Teacher Training Manual,' Izumo Taisha Headquarters, 1989, p. 319</ref>
With the establishment of the {{ill|Meiji government|ja|明治政府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji government|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 明治維新|1=Meiji Restoration|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8707}}'s {{ill|Shinto|ja|神祇|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天津神・国津神}} system, oshi rapidly declined. In 1869, oshi were barred from conducting {{ill|Shinto funerals|ja|神葬祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto funerals|1=Shinto funeral|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11590783}}, and by 1871, the profession was abolished. Most oshi transitioned into farming or running inns. Some attempted to continue religious activities, forming organizations such as {{ill|Fusōkyō|ja|扶桑教|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fusōkyō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Fuso-kyo|WD=Q11497295}}.<ref>Takahiko Takano, "The Court and Religion in Early Modern Japan," Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2014, ISBN 978-4-642-03461-6, pp. 345-348.</ref>
=== Fuji Oshi === In the {{ill|Tenshōjingo Conflict|ja|天正壬午の乱|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenshōjingo Conflict|1=Tenshō-Jingo war|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10939819}}, figures like Watanabe Shūgokuyū played significant roles. Notable Fuji Oshi residences, including {{ill|Old Togawa Residence|ja|旧外川家住宅|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Old Togawa Residence|1=former house of the Togawa Family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q66089879}} and {{ill|Kosano Residence|ja|小佐野家住宅|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kosano Residence|1=house of the Osano Family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q66089882}}, are designated as {{ill|World Heritage Site|en|World Heritage Site|WD=Q9259|lt=World Heritage Sites|1=World Heritage Site}} and {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)}}.<ref>{{Cite web|title=World Cultural Heritage: Mt. Fuji Components|url=https://www.yamanashi-kankou.jp/heritage/fujisanwatcher/jp/world_heritage/index.html|publisher=Yamanashi Tourism Organization|access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Old Togawa Residence|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/185831|website=National Cultural Heritage Database|access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref>
== Cultural Depictions == * "Oshi Yagorō: Journey to Ise" by {{ill|Saijo Naka|ja|西條奈加|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saijo Naka|1=Naka Saijō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11628806}}, ISBN 439634015X * "{{ill|Sakuya-hime|ja|咲夜姫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakuya-hime|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}" by {{ill|Yamaguchi Kaito|ja|山口歌糸|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamaguchi Kaito|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, ISBN 4783880204

Some attempted to continue religious activities, forming organizations such as {{ill|Fusōkyō|ja|扶桑教|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fusōkyō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Fuso-kyo|WD=Q11497295}}.<ref>Takahiko Takano, "The Court and Religion in Early Modern Japan," Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2014, ISBN 978-4-642-03461-6, pp. 345-348.</ref>
=== Fuji Oshi === In the {{ill|Tenshōjingo Conflict|ja|天正壬午の乱|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenshōjingo Conflict|1=Tenshō-Jingo war|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10939819}}, figures like Watanabe Shūgokuyū played significant roles. Notable Fuji Oshi residences, including {{ill|Old Togawa Residence|ja|旧外川家住宅|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Old Togawa Residence|1=former house of the Togawa Family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q66089879}} and {{ill|Kosano Residence|ja|小佐野家住宅|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kosano Residence|1=house of the Osano Family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q66089882}}, are designated as {{ill|World Heritage Site|en|World Heritage Site|WD=Q9259|lt=World Heritage Sites|1=World Heritage Site}} and {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)}}.<ref>{{Cite web|title=World Cultural Heritage: Mt. Fuji Components|url=https://www.yamanashi-kankou.jp/heritage/fujisanwatcher/jp/world_heritage/index.html|publisher=Yamanashi Tourism Organization|access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Old Togawa Residence|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/185831|website=National Cultural Heritage Database|access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref>
== Cultural Depictions == * "Oshi Yagorō: Journey to Ise" by {{ill|Saijo Naka|ja|西條奈加|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saijo Naka|1=Naka Saijō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11628806}}, ISBN 439634015X * "{{ill|Sakuya-hime|ja|咲夜姫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakuya-hime|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}" by {{ill|Yamaguchi Kaito|ja|山口歌糸|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamaguchi Kaito|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, ISBN 4783880204
== References == <references /> == See Also == * {{ill|Hagaki|ja|羽書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hagaki|1=hagaki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11610188}} * {{ill|Okage-mairi|ja|お蔭参り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okage-mairi|1=Okage Mairi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11263449}} * {{ill|Shukubo|en|Shukubo|WD=Q11455614|lt=Shukubō|1=Shukubo}} * {{ill|Accommodation facilities|ja|宿泊施設|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Accommodation facilities|1=accommodation facility|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q48835818}} * {{ill|Ise Calendar|ja|伊勢暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Calendar|qq=}} * [http://www.nikko-annai.com/ Dōshabiki] - Edo-period guides for worshippers at {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|en|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|WD=Q696641|lt=Nikkō Tōshō-gū|1=Nikkō Tōshō-gū}}

revision.text:
{{Otheruses|Shrine and temple guides|Music teachers for the emperor or imperial family|{{ill|御師 (音楽)|ja|御師 (音楽)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/御師 (音楽)|1=Q48759541|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q48759541}}}} [[File:Oshi 20190826a.jpg|thumb|Fuji-ko Guide (御師), photographed August 26, 2019]] '''Oshi''' (御師) refers to shrine priests associated with specific {{ill|Temples and Shrines|lt=Temples and Shrines|lt=Shrines and temples|ja|社寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/shrines and temples|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 寺社}}, who perform {{ill|prayers|ja|祈祷|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/prayers|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 祈り|1=Prayer|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q40953}}, guide worshippers, and assist pilgrims with visits and accommodations. In the case of {{ill|Ise Shrine|en|Ise Shrine|WD=Q687168|lt=Ise Grand Shrine|1=Ise Grand Shrine}}, it was traditionally pronounced as "onshi." These guides often formed concentrated communities along highways, known as {{ill|Oshi towns|ja|御師町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oshi towns|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
== Overview == Originally an abbreviation for "御祈祷師" (prayer guides), the term "oshi" emerged during the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}, initially referring to {{ill|temple monks|ja|社僧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/temple monks|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 別当寺|1=Bettō-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11396755}}. Later, it extended to include shrine priests who facilitated pilgrimages.
== History == During the Heian period, notable examples of oshi included those from {{ill|Iwashimizu Hachimangū|en|Iwashimizu Hachimangū|WD=Q710098|lt=Iwashimizu Hachiman Shrine|1=Iwashimizu Hachimangū}}, {{ill|Kamo shrines|en|Kamo shrines|WD=Q1306606|lt=Kamo Shrine|1=Kamo shrines}}, and {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|en|Hiyoshi Taisha|WD=Q656451|lt=Hiyoshi Taisha|1=Hiyoshi Taisha}}, with the {{ill|Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Sanzan|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} "Kumano Oshi" being particularly famous.<ref name="ushiyama">Yoshiyuki Ushiyama, "Faith and Culture in Medieval Ōhara," Shinshu University Bulletin of Education, Vol. 79 (1993), {{hdl|10091/1626}}</ref> The Kumano Oshi organized prayers, accommodations, and guided noble pilgrims during the late Heian period's {{ill|Kumano pilgrimage|ja|熊野詣|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano pilgrimage|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 熊野三山|1=Kumano Three Shrines|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10403700}}. Relationships between oshi and pilgrims evolved into stable "master-disciple" ties, known as {{ill|師檀関係|ja|師檀関係|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/師檀関係|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, encompassing even commoners by the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period|1=Kamakura period}}.
Between the Kamakura and {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi periods|1=Muromachi period}}, other prominent oshi groups emerged, such as those from {{ill|Ise Shrine|en|Ise Shrine|WD=Q687168|lt=Ise Grand Shrine|1=Ise Grand Shrine}}, {{ill|Fujiko (religion)|en|Fujiko (religion)|WD=Q11456201|lt=Fuji-ko|1=Fujiko (religion)}}, {{ill|Matsunoo Taisha|en|Matsunoo Taisha|WD=Q710302|lt=Matsuo Shrine|1=Matsunoo Taisha}}, {{ill|Mishima Taisha|en|Mishima Taisha|WD=Q650772|lt=Mishima Taisha|1=Mishima Taisha}}, and {{ill|Mount Haku|en|Mount Haku|WD=Q2639098|lt=Hakusan Shrine|1=Mount Haku}}. Notably, records like the {{ill|Azuma Kagami|en|Azuma Kagami|WD=Q1042324|lt=Azuma Kagami|1=Azuma Kagami}} document oshi from {{ill|Izumo-taisha|en|Izumo-taisha|WD=Q696362|lt=Izumo Taisha|1=Izumo-taisha}} serving {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|en|Minamoto no Yoritomo|WD=Q242800|lt=Minamoto no Yoritomo|1=Minamoto no Yoritomo}}.<ref>'Izumo Taisha Teacher Training Manual,' Izumo Taisha Headquarters, 1989, p. 319</ref>
During the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}, oshi played an essential role as intermediaries between commoners and shrine priests. Pilgrimages like {{ill|Okage-mairi|lt=Ise pilgrimage|ja|伊勢参り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okage-mairi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to お蔭参り|1=Okage Mairi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11263449}} and those to {{ill|Mount Fuji|en|Mount Fuji|WD=Q39231|lt=Mt. Fuji|1=Mount Fuji}} became widely accessible. Oshi organized {{ill|Kō (lecture)|en|Kō (lecture)|WD=Q11632184|lt=kō (pilgrimage associations)|1=Kō (lecture)}} and hosted pilgrims in their {{ill|Shukubo|en|Shukubo|WD=Q11455614|lt=shukubō (pilgrim lodgings)|1=Shukubo}}. The economic stability of the Edo period allowed pilgrimages to combine faith and leisure, enhancing oshi influence. However, groups like the Kumano Oshi declined alongside {{ill|Kumano faith|ja|熊野信仰|1=Kumano Faith|2=ja|3=熊野信仰|WD=Q135501741}}{{efn|The religion of [[Kumano Shrines]]|1=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Three Shrines|lt=Kumano faith|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano faith|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Three Shrines|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 熊野三山}}.
In Izumo, oshi structures laid the foundation for the {{ill|Izumo-taishakyo|en|Izumo-taishakyo|WD=Q6102386|lt=Izumo Taisha Faith|1=Izumo-taishakyo}}, creating facilities such as "tanjo" (mission centers) to support local preaching.<ref>'Izumo Taisha Teacher Training Manual,' Izumo Taisha Headquarters, 1989, p. 319</ref>
With the establishment of the {{ill|Meiji government|ja|明治政府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji government|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 明治維新|1=Meiji Restoration|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8707}}'s {{ill|Shinto|ja|神祇|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天津神・国津神}} system, oshi rapidly declined. In 1869, oshi were barred from conducting {{ill|Shinto funerals|ja|神葬祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto funerals|1=Shinto funeral|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11590783}}, and by 1871, the profession was abolished. Most oshi transitioned into farming or running inns. Some attempted to continue religious activities, forming organizations such as {{ill|Fusōkyō|ja|扶桑教|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fusōkyō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Fuso-kyo|WD=Q11497295}}.<ref>Takahiko Takano, "The Court and Religion in Early Modern Japan," Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2014, ISBN 978-4-642-03461-6, pp. 345-348.</ref>
=== Fuji Oshi === In the {{ill|Tenshōjingo Conflict|ja|天正壬午の乱|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenshōjingo Conflict|1=Tenshō-Jingo war|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10939819}}, figures like Watanabe Shūgokuyū played significant roles. Notable Fuji Oshi residences, including {{ill|Old Togawa Residence|ja|旧外川家住宅|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Old Togawa Residence|1=former house of the Togawa Family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q66089879}} and {{ill|Kosano Residence|ja|小佐野家住宅|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kosano Residence|1=house of the Osano Family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q66089882}}, are designated as {{ill|World Heritage Site|en|World Heritage Site|WD=Q9259|lt=World Heritage Sites|1=World Heritage Site}} and {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)}}.<ref>{{Cite web|title=World Cultural Heritage: Mt. Fuji Components|url=https://www.yamanashi-kankou.jp/heritage/fujisanwatcher/jp/world_heritage/index.html|publisher=Yamanashi Tourism Organization|access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Old Togawa Residence|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/185831|website=National Cultural Heritage Database|access-date=2022-04-16}}</ref>
== Cultural Depictions == * "Oshi Yagorō: Journey to Ise" by {{ill|Saijo Naka|ja|西條奈加|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saijo Naka|1=Naka Saijō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11628806}}, ISBN 439634015X * "{{ill|Sakuya-hime|ja|咲夜姫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakuya-hime|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}" by {{ill|Yamaguchi Kaito|ja|山口歌糸|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamaguchi Kaito|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, ISBN 4783880204
== References == <references /> == See Also == * {{ill|Hagaki|ja|羽書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hagaki|1=hagaki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11610188}} * {{ill|Okage-mairi|ja|お蔭参り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okage-mairi|1=Okage Mairi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11263449}} * {{ill|Shukubo|en|Shukubo|WD=Q11455614|lt=Shukubō|1=Shukubo}} * {{ill|Accommodation facilities|ja|宿泊施設|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Accommodation facilities|1=accommodation facility|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q48835818}} * {{ill|Ise Calendar|ja|伊勢暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Calendar|qq=}} * [http://www.nikko-annai.com/ Dōshabiki] - Edo-period guides for worshippers at {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|en|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|WD=Q696641|lt=Nikkō Tōshō-gū|1=Nikkō Tōshō-gū}}
